- 1\. TLS Segmentation Strategy

- Mesmer vs. Stardist:

 - Mesmer shows better performance with longer running times
 > compared to Stardist.

 - Mesmer runs on small cropped tiles; additional resources are
 > needed to apply it to full-slide images.

 - Tile stitching consideration: ensure proper handling of cells in
 > overlapping regions.

 - Black areas (bubbles) can cause segmentation error

Overview ofGeoMxAssayswithProCodeIndices
KeyConsiderations fortheImmuno- oncology Proteome Atlas
(IPA)withProCodeIndices
TheImmuno- oncology Proteome Atlas(IPA) covers 570+ protein targets across dozens ofpathways
andisthefirstGeoMx assay touseProCode i5andi7indices (sequences important inNext- Generation
Sequencing (NGS)). ProCode indices are2nucleotides longer thanSeqCode indices, which areused in
allother GeoMx NGS assays. Formore information oni5andi7indices, please refer totheGeoMx
DSP NGS Readout User Manual (MAN- 10153) .
Whenrunning aProCodeassaysuchastheImmuno- oncology Proteome Atlas,pleasepay
attention toProCode-specific instructions throughout theusermanuals, summarized here:
lGeoMx DSP software v3.1 orlater isrequired torunProCode assays such asIPA.
lTheIPAprobe mixispackaged astwotubes: core andmodule. Theconcentration ofthemodule is
different than theProtein- NGS assays with Seq Code indices, sotheAntibody Working Solution is
prepared differently. See details intheGeoMx DSP Manual Slide Preparation User Manual (MAN-
10150) orAutomated Slide Preparation User Manual (MAN- 10151) .
lGenerally, assays with ProCode indices such asIPAcannot becombined inthesame plate orreadout
group asassays with SeqCode indices. Compatibility rules aredetailed intheGeoMx DSP Instrument

y, assays with ProCode indices such asIPAcannot becombined inthesame plate orreadout
group asassays with SeqCode indices. Compatibility rules aredetailed intheGeoMx DSP Instrument
User Manual (MAN- 10152) .Anexception istheSpatial Proteogenomics Assay inwhich RNA and
Protein arecollected from thesame slide; refer totheGeoMx DSP Spatial Proteogenomics Assay User
Manual (MAN- 10158) .
lInNGS library preparation, tags with ProCode indices such asIPAarePCR- amplified with ProCode
primer plates, rather than Seq Code primer plates. Less DSP aspirate isrequired inthePCR
amplification step forProCode assays compared toSeqCode assays (2µLvs4µL).Seedetails inthe
GeoMx DSP NGS Readout User Manual (MAN- 10153) .Note that2Pro Code primer plates areavailable
(YandZ),allowing multiplexing ofupto192wells.
lProCode assays cangenerally besequenced tolower depth than SeqCode assays. Seedetails inthe
GeoMx DSP NGS Readout User Manual (MAN- 10153) .
lAtthistime, Spatial Proteogenomic Assays thatinclude IPAcanonly beprocessed onthestandalone
GeoMx NGS Pipeline software version 3.1, notontheGeoMx NGS Pipeline onDRAGEN via
BaseSpace Sequencing Hub. MacOS isnotsupported. Obtain GeoMx NGS Pipeline software version
3.1and installation instructions athttps://nanostring.box.com/v/GeoMxNGSPipeline .Refer tothe
GeoMx DSP Spatial Proteogenomics Assay User Manual (MAN- 10158).

ine software version
3.1and installation instructions athttps://nanostring.box.com/v/GeoMxNGSPipeline .Refer tothe

GeoMxDSPWorkflow
GeoMxDSPWorkflow
InGeoMx Digital Spatial Profiling (DSP), antibody ornucleic acid probes arecoupled tophotocleavable
oligonucleotide tags. After probes hybridize totargets inslide- mounted tissue sections, the
oligonucleotide tags arereleased from discrete regions ofthetissue viaUVexposure. Released tags are
quantified bynCounter technology orIllumina Next Generation Sequencing (NGS). Counts aremapped
back totissue location, yielding aspatially resolved digital profile ofanalyte abundance (Figure 1).
lDay1:SlideStaining. Prepare slides and incubate biological targets with UV-cleavable probes.
Prepare manually orusing the BOND RX/RXmFully Automated IHC/ISH Stainer from Leica
Biosystems®.
lDay2:Process SlidesonGeoMxDSP. Load prepared slides intotheGeoMx DSP instrument. Slides
arescanned tocapture fluorescent images used toselect regions ofinterest (ROIs). ROIs may be
segmented intodiscrete compartments orareas ofillumination (AOI). Theinstrument collects UV-
cleaved oligos from theAOIs intothewells ofacollection plate.
ForNGSreadout:
Day3:Transfer thecollected aspirates toaPCR plate and
perform LibraryPrep with SeqCode orProCode primers.
Pool andpurify theproducts, thenSequence onanIllumina
NGS instrument.
Day4:Process FASTQ sequencing filesintodigital count
conversion (DCC) filesusing theGeoMxNGSPipeline on

mers.
Pool andpurify theproducts, thenSequence onanIllumina
NGS instrument.
Day4:Process FASTQ sequencing filesintodigital count
conversion (DCC) filesusing theGeoMxNGSPipeline on
standalone software orIllumina's DRAGEN™ platform
accessed viaBaseSpace™ Sequence Hub(cloud) or
NextSeq 1000/2000 (local).FornCounter readout:

SlidePrepIntroduction
Slide preparation isthefirst step oftheGeoMx DSP workflow. Tissue sections areprocessed for
staining, followed bytheaddition ofmorphology reagents andbiological probes (Figure 2).
lMorphology reagents areantibody- fluorophore complexes thatbind tospecific targets onthetissue.
Tissue structure and cellcomponents most important toyour analysis areilluminated using the
fluorescence imaging ontheDSP system. Alternative labeling techniques aredescribed inthe
Appendices.
lBiological probes areeither antibodies orinsituhybridization (ISH) probes thatbind toprotein orRNA
targets, respectively. Each probe iscoupled toaphotocleavable oligonucleotide. These oligos, when
exposed totheGeoMx DSP instrument 'sUVlight, willbereleased intosolution andaspirated intoa
collection plate wellfordownstream processing.
Figure 2:RNA slide preparation with ISHprobes
SlidePrepEquipment, Materials, andReagents
Required equipment, materials, andreagents arelisted atthestart ofeach slide preparation process in
themanual. Individual Equipment, Materials, andReagents lists forevery application oftheGeoMx
DSP areavailable intheNanoString University Document Library (https://university.nanostring.com ).

lStorage forupto1day: submerge in1XTBS- Tandstore at4°C, protected from light tomaintain the

lStorage for1dayto3months:

totheother. Remove excess mounting medium.
4.Allowslidetodryatroom temperature overnight, protected from light (e.g. inabench
drawer).
5.Store slide at4°C, protected from light, forupto3months.
Slideunmounting procedure
1.Submerge mounted slide in1XTBS- TorPBS- Tuntil coverslip isloose orhasfallen off.With gentle
agitation, thecoverslip typically fallsoffwithin 15minutes.
2.Ifcoverslip hasfallen off,slide isready foruse. Ifcoverslip isstillattached butloose, gently remove it
using tweezers.
3.Wash slide with fresh 1XTBS- Tfor5minutes toensure removal ofmounting media.

1-9ontheprevious page.

Stripping andre-probing procedure forproteinslides
GeoMx Protein assay slides arereusable andcanberestained with adifferent commercial orcustom
panel following thisprocedure. This procedure requires aUVlight boxortransilluminator capable of
emitting 302/312 nmUVlight (example )and1XTBS- T.
1.Place slide flatonthesurface ofaUVtransilluminator.
2.Apply enough1XTBS-Ttocompletely cover thetissue (50–200μLdepending onthesize ofthe
tissue).
3.ExposetoUVlight for3minutes tocleave tags from bound antibodies.
4.Carefully tapslide onaclean, absorbent surface (e.g. paper towel) toremove liquid andavoid oligo
contamination.
5.Washslide bydipping inastaining jarwith1XTBS-T.
6.Transfer toanother staining jarwith fresh1XTBS-T.
7.Toapply new probes, proceed toPerform antigen retrieval (1hour) onpage 19.

Appendix I:Selecting and Sectioning FFPE Samples onpage 45covers FFPE block selection and
sectioning indetail. Review itprior tobeginning theRNA Slide Preparation protocol.
GeoMx hasbeen validated forsample blocks upto3years oldprepared from tissues with acold ischemic time
ofless than 1hour using 10% NBF orsimilar fixative. Forbest results, donotuseFFPE blocks more than 10
years old.Assay performance willbeinfluenced bytissue block ageandtreatment conditions such ascold/warm
ischemic time, fixative, andstorage.

 Probe mixes should behandled inanarea separate from nCounter work, NGS library prep,
orother GeoMx workflows. GeoMx detection reagents cancross- contaminate probe mixes andgive
misleading orincorrect results. Areas should becleaned thoroughly with RNase AWAY after probe mix
formulation. Alternatively, handle probe mixes inPCR workstations thataredecontaminated with UVlight.
Change gloves after handling anyprobe mixes toavoid cross- contamination.
Due tothehigh sensitivity ofthisassay, itisrecommended thatyouchange pipette filter tipsforevery step,
change gloves frequently, andusefresh wipes toremove excess liquids.

water.

4.One at a time, remove slides from 1X PBS, wipe away excess liquid, and set in hybridization
chamber in a horizontal position. Take care not to let the slides dry out.
5. Ensure that the Kimwipes and liquid do not contact the slides. Hybridization solution can wick off of the
slides if it comes into contact with Kimwipes or liquid.
6.Add 200 μL hybridization solution to each slide. Take care not to introduce any bubbles.
To avoid bubbles, leave a small residual volume in the pipette tip. If a bubble forms, aspirate it gently with the
pipette. Do not touch the tissue with the tip. It is preferable to lose some hybridization solution and remove
bubbles than to have bubbles in the solution, as long as sufficient solution remains to cover the tissue after the
coverslip is applied.
IMPORTANT:
 From this point on, minimize the slides' exposure to light to preserve the integrity of the
photocleavable barcodes.
7.Gently apply a Grace Bio-Labs HybriSlip .
Start by setting one edge of the coverslip down in solution on the slide, then gradually laying down the
rest of the coverslip to avoid the formation of air bubbles (Figure 7).
8. Repeat steps 4–7 for each slide.
9. Close hybridization chamber, insert into hybridization oven,
and clamp into place (Figure 8).Incubate at 37°C
overnight (16–24 hours).

Stripping andre-probing procedure forRNA slides
GeoMx RNA assay slides arereusable andcanberestained with adifferent commercial orcustom panel
following thisprocedure. This procedure requires aUVlight boxortransilluminator capable ofemitting
302/312 nmUVlight (example ),2XSSC- T,1XTris-EDTA, and2XSSC.
1.Place theslide flatonthesurface ofaUVtransilluminator.
2.Apply enough2XSSC-Ttocompletely cover thetissue (50–200μLdepending onthesize ofthe
tissue).
3.ExposetoUVlight for3minutes tocleave tags from bound probes.
4.Carefully tapeach slide onaclean, disposable surface (e.g., paper towel) toremove liquid andavoid
oligo contamination.
5.Washslides bydipping inastaining jarwith2XSSC-T.
6.Transfer toanother staining jarwith fresh2XSSC-T.
7.Incubate theslides in1XTris-EDTAat85°C using either ahotplate orapressure cooker onalow
pressure setting for15minutes.
8.Wash 3times in2XSSC.
9.Proceed toInsituhybridization (overnight) onpage 38.

Appendix I.Selecting, Sectioning FFPESamples
Appendices
Appendix I:Selecting andSectioning FFPESamples
When preparing, sectioning, andstoring FFPE blocks foruseintheGeoMx DSP Protein andRNA assays,
take care topreserve sample integrity atallsteps. The integrity ofFFPE samples isimpacted bymany
factors including time from excision tofixation, storage conditions, tissue type, andsample age. Samples
with poor integrity arelikely togivelowsignal, particularly inRNA assays.
GeoMx hasbeen validated forsample blocks upto3years oldprepared from tissues with acold ischemic time
ofless than 1hour using 10% NBF orsimilar fixative. Forbest results, donotuseFFPE blocks more than 10
years old.Assay performance willbeinfluenced bytissue block ageandtreatment conditions such ascold/warm
ischemic time, fixative, andstorage.
Selecting FFPEblocks
FFPE blocks should meet thefollowing criteria forthebest performance with GeoMx DSP assays.
1.Blocks should befixed in10% neutral buffered formalin for18to24hours atroom temperature. This
applies totissues less than 0.5cminthickness. Larger tissues have notbeen tested byBruker and
may require longer fixation times.
2.Tissues should befixed immediately after excision forbest results. Fixation within onehour post-
excision isacceptable.

en tested byBruker and
may require longer fixation times.
2.Tissues should befixed immediately after excision forbest results. Fixation within onehour post-
excision isacceptable.
3.Tissues should bethoroughly dehydrated inethanol gradients prior toembedding inparaffin.
4.FFPE blocks should bestored atroom temperature andambient humidity.
5.Forbest results, donotuseFFPE blocks thataremore than 10years old.

RNAscope canbeused tohelp setthelocation ofROIs (i.e., place ROIs where aparticular RNA isenriched).
However, thepunctate signals frommost RNAscope probes arenotcompatible with thesegmentation
algorithm ontheGeoMx, meaning thattheGeoMx may notbeable tosegment based ontheRNAscope signal.
Segmentation may bepossible with highly abundant RNAscope targets, soitisrecommended totest the
segmentation tools with anRNAscope probe ofinterest inadvance ofyour GeoMx experiments. Ifsegmenting
isnotsupported, consider using RNAscope in1-2channels andfluorescent antibody morphology markers inthe
other channel (s)todefine thesegments. Alternatively, prepare serial sections, onewith RNAscope andonewith
fluorescent antibody morphology markers. During ROI selection ontheantibody- labeled slide, overlay theimage
oftheRNAscope slide toguide ROI selection. Proceed with segmentation andcollection from theantibody-
labeled slide.

quantified bynCounter technology orIllumina NextGeneration Sequencing (NGS).Countsaremapped
backtotissuelocation, yieldingaspatially resolved digitalprofileofanalyteabundance (Figure1).

NGSLibraryPreparation andSequencing
Following collection ontheGeoMxDSPinstrument, the96-wellcollection platecontains aspirates from
theexperimental regionsofinterest. Theaspirates arephotocleaved oligos,eachcontaining areadout
tagsequence identifier (RTSID)toidentifythebiological target(Figure3),andauniquemolecular
identifier (UMI)enabling removal ofPCRduplicates inthesubsequent dataprocessing pipeline. Finally,
SPR1(sequencing primerread1)andSPR2(sequencing primerread2)sequences allowhybridization to
GeoMx SeqCodeorProCodeprimers inPCR amplification. NotethatSPR1andSPR2sequences are
different inSeqCodeversusProCodetags.
DuringPCR amplification, theGeoMxSeqCodeorProCodeprimersaddi5andi7indexing sequences
(uniquedualindexes) formultiplexing manyGeoMxDSP aspirate sequences intoasinglesequencing
run,aswellasP5andP7sequences forbindingandamplification onIlluminaflowcells.
Following apooling,purification, andqualitycontrolprocess, thefinalNGSlibraryisreadyforsequencing
onanIlluminaNGSplatform.
Figure3:SeqCodetagstructure andPCRproduct.
InProCodetags,thei5andi7are10ntinlength,andthesequences ofSPR1andSPR2aredifferent.

GeoMxNGS Pipeline
Following sequencing ontheIlluminaplatform, thesequencing resultsarerunthroughtheGeoMxNGS
Pipeline. Thisbioinformatic processing pipeline wasdeveloped toconvert FASTQfilesfromIllumina
sequencers according toparameters definedintheconfiguration filegenerated bytheGeoMxDSP.The
pipeline computationally processes thesefilestooutputDCCfiles,whichareuploaded totheGeoMx
DSPsystemfordataanalysis.
Theautomated dataprocessing pipelinedepicted here(Figure4)illustrates thestepsoftheGeoMxNGS
Pipeline.
Figure4:GeoMxNGSPipeline
Inthefirststep,therawreads(rawsequencing FASTQfiles)arecompiled withtheconfiguration file,
whichspecifies theprocessing parameters. Next,therawreadsareprocessed bycomputationally
removing theadaptersequences (resulting intrimmed reads),andmerging theoverlapping paired-end
reads(resulting institched reads).Inthethirdstep,thestitched readsarealignedtotheRTS-ID
barcodes inthereference assay,creating aligned readsandassigning rawcountstobiological target
names.Then,PCRduplicates areremoved usingtheUniqueMolecular Identifier ineachread,resulting in
deduplicated reads.TheDCCfilesarecreated andpresented asa.zipfileinafolderwhichyou
designate andcanthenbeuploaded intotheDSPControlCenterforstudycreation intheDSPData
Analysis Suite,orloadedintoRfordataprocessing withtheGeomxTools opensoftware package.

rwhichyou
designate andcanthenbeuploaded intotheDSPControlCenterforstudycreation intheDSPData
Analysis Suite,orloadedintoRfordataprocessing withtheGeomxTools opensoftware package.

GeoMxNGSReadout SiteReadiness
Itisrecommended tohavethreedistinct, physically separated areasforcontainment, eachwithitsown
setofconsumables (pipettetips,tubes)andequipment (pipettes, centrifuges, pens,labcoats,etc.):
1.Slidepreparation areaforhandling concentrated probepool:
lConcentrated probepoolusedforovernight insituhybridization shouldbekeptisolated frompre-
PCRandpost-PCRareas.
lNegative airpressure isideal.
2.Pre-PCRareaforPCRsetup:
lPCRsetupshouldtakeplaceinaseparate roomorcontainment area,ideallyinadedicated PCR
setuplaminarflowhood.
lPositiveairpressure isideal.
3.Post-PCRareaforhandling amplified PCRproducts:
lAmplified PCRproducts shouldbehandled inaseparate roomorcontainment areaawayfromthe
slidepreparation areaorpre-PCRarea.

3.Take all the tubes (Sample Pool, NTC, and optional PCR Positive Control) through the 
following AMPure cleanup protocol. In the subsequent section, you will check the quality and size 
of the purified pooled library against the purified NTC and the optional PCR Positive Control.
4.Measure the exact volume in the tube using a pipette. Do not skip this step.
5.Multiply the volume in the tube by 1.2; add this volume of AMPure XP beads. Mix well by pipetting up 
and down 10x. Pulse centrifuge.
6.Incubate 5 minutes at room temperature.
7.Prepare 20 mL fresh 80% ethanol by combining 16 mL 100% ethanol and 4 mL PCR-grade water.
8.Pellet beads on a magnetic stand for 5 minutes or until the solution is clear.
9.Keeping the tube on the magnet (through step 15), carefully remove the supernatant. Avoid 
disturbing the beads as the library is bound to them at this stage.
10.Add 1 mL of freshly prepared 80% ethanol. Incubate for 30 seconds.
11.Aspirate and discard the supernatant, being careful not to disturb the beads.
12.Add 1 mL of freshly-prepared 80% ethanol, then incubate for 30 seconds.
13.Aspirate and discard the supernatant, being careful not to disturb the beads.
14.Visually inspect the beads to ensure that as much ethanol as possible is removed. Use a low volume 
pipette (e.g., P20) to remove any residual ethanol, if necessary.

the beads.
14.Visually inspect the beads to ensure that as much ethanol as possible is removed. Use a low volume 
pipette (e.g., P20) to remove any residual ethanol, if necessary.
15.Dry beads on the magnet for up to 5 minutes with the tube lid open.
16.Resuspend beads in each tube in 54 µL Elution Buffer (off of the magnet). Mix well by pipetting. 
Incubate for 5 minutes at room temperature (off of the magnet).
17.Pellet beads on the magnet for 5 minutes (or until the solution is clear).
18.Keeping the tube on the magnet, extract 50 µL of supernatant to a new tube. Leave 2–4 µL of 
supernatant in tube, if necessary, to avoid disturbing beads. This supernatant contains the sequencing 
library.
19.Add 60 µL of Ampure XP beads to the 50 µL supernatant. Mix well by pipetting up and down 10x. Pulse 
centrifuge.
20.Incubate for 5 minutes at room temperature (off of the magnet).

TheGeoMxNGSPipeline looksforFASTQfileswithanamingstructure suchasDSP-1001250001985- A-A02_
S2_L001_R1_001.fastq.gz whereDSP-1001250001985- A-A02=Sample ID matching Configuration file,
SeqCodeIndices.csv, orSampleID Translator File.S2=Samplesheetnumber. L001=Lanenumber (include
evenifyourflowcellhadonly1lane).R1=Forward orreverse, read1or2.001=Shouldalwaysbe001.The
suffix.fastq.gz indicates acompressed file.
NextSeq 1000/2000 DRAGEN versions from3.8to4.2.6donotsupportlanesplitting (demultiplexing doesnot
generate FASTQs withstandard filenames; lanenumbers areomitted). Toensurecompatibility withoff-
instrument instances oftheGeoMx NGSPipeline (i.e.Option1),pleaseuseDRAGEN 3.7.4orbelow,or
DRAGEN 4.2.7orabovewiththeparameter “NoLaneSplitting” setto“FALSE”.

vector_chunks.text:
### **Small initial point** 

There is an interesting talk tomorrow, Zhihan will make a recording so
that we can check together:

### Speaker: Tullia Bruno, PhD, University of Pittsburgh and UPMC Hillman Cancer Center

### Talk Title: *\"The Activity of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in High-Grade Serous Ovarian Cancer Is Governed by Site, Stroma, and Cellular Interactions\"*

### Recording: Zhihan will record the session for team review, Zoom Link: [[Meeting Link]{.underline}](https://us02web.zoom.us/j/82392041446?pwd=IKzb4au8t203iZSpYMgJD2MpMcUFJf.1)

- **The recording is [here
 > <https://datacloud.helsinki.fi/index.php/s/HMdjyeoWPG4Pdri>]{.mark}**

### **Action Items and Discussion Points**

#### **1. Spatial Tumor Microenvironment (TME) Exploration**

- **New Experiments vs. Existing Data:\
 > **Discussed whether to conduct new experiments or utilize existing
 > data. We concluded that we will try to perform new experiments for
 > a cohort of IDS patients. In this regard, we have two main
 > interesting points coming out of the discussion:

 - **HRD Sample Updates:\
 > **Treatment protocol was adjusted for HRD samples---now with
 > only 3 chemotherapy cycles. We should anticipate a new batch
 > of HRD samples with higher tumor percentages.

 - **Discussion of whether to include low tumor purity samples:**

 - **Questions:** May these had and already dismantled TLSs?.

vector_chunks.source_file: 4. Meetings & Updates/Meeting_notes/2024/07.11.24 Task division meeting - Ada Zhihan Anniina_.md
vector_chunks.text:
> of HRD samples with higher tumor percentages.

 - **Discussion of whether to include low tumor purity samples:**

 - **Questions:** May these had and already dismantled TLSs?.
 > Joona will be consulted about exploring different
 > timepoints in distinct samples (formation, maturation,
 > dismantling).

#### **2. Next Steps on Profiling and Sample Selection**

- **Ada's Tasks:**

 - Profile all IDS (may narrow to HRP).

 - Select samples for HGSC, endometroid carcinoma, and omentum.

 - For these samples we will plan a panel similar to SPACE,
 > considering adding an extra cycle with a B-cell-oriented
 > panel or replacing failed markers.

 - Coordinate with Saun on ordering a 24-tube tray for the new
 > proteopicking. Updating her on a large batch of IDS samples
 > (50-60) she would profile around February.

 - Ask for help to Lina on staying longer in the lab and performing
 > the proteo-picks (meaning extension of 2-3 months of contract;
 > Anniina has funding).

- **Zhihan's Tasks:**

 - Check EyeMT annotations and pairs.

 - Review counts for pair compatibility with the experiment.

 - Monitor immune aggregates and similar annotations.

**3. Master's Thesis and Student Recruitment**

- **Potential for MSc Thesis:**

 - Consider involving a Master\'s student in analyzing HEs and
 > learning QuPath software.

- **Outreach and Advertising (Ada and Zhihan):**

vector_chunks.source_file: 4. Meetings & Updates/Meeting_notes/2024/07.11.24 Task division meeting - Ada Zhihan Anniina_.md

- **Outreach and Advertising (Ada and Zhihan):**

 - Target students interested in HE, clinical cohorts, etc.,
 > especially from the TRANSMED program.

 - Write and distribute an advertisement to Tiina.

 - Reach out to TRANSMED students through WhatsApp as well.

#### **4. Additional Considerations**

- **Project Planning Workshop:**

 - Discussed the possibility of Ada hosting a short workshop on

vector_chunks.text:
**[GeoMx strategic proposal - 20/11]{.underline}**

### **[Research Objective:]{.underline}**

We aim to dissect the molecular interactions in various areas within the
TLSs (Tfh-gc b cells and myeloid - T cell interactions)

### **[Experimental Design Overview:]{.underline}**

**Sample Set Proposal:** One of them has long PFS and the other, short
PFS

![](media/image1.png){width="6.046875546806649in"
height="0.7917344706911636in"}

**Sample Selection Criteria:**[BRCAwt, multiple TLS, HGSC IDS
samples]{.underline}

**Sample Exclusion criteria:** [BRCAmut, only single TLS, high fat
content, PDS samples, short follow-up,]{.underline} Pre-Post NACT sample
set slides where one of the pairs contained no TLS.

- [BRCAmut TLS multiple TLS]{.underline}s: S123 was discarded (BRCA1)
 > was good, but discarded and S128 (gBRCA1) had extremely poor tumor
 > content (almost all of it was fat)
 > ![](media/image2.png){width="1.381459973753281in"
 > height="1.0783847331583551in"}

**[Sample Processing:]{.underline}**

FFPE blocks are in the lab and we need to section them:

- [2 samples located in one slide.]{.underline} Mind that the
 > scannable area of the slide is 35.3x14.1 mm, so tissues need to be
 > placed accordingly

- Ideally we need at least 6 of these:

 - 1 slide for tCycIF and 1 slide for GeoMx (2)

 - 1 back-up for both tCycIF and GeoMx (2)

vector_chunks.text:
**Meeting Notes 15042025**

**Topic:** TLS Segmentation Issues\
**Date:** 15.04.2025\
**Attendees:** Anniina, Ada, Hanna, Zhihan, Ziqi

**1. TLS Segmentation Strategy**

- **Mesmer vs. Stardist:**

 - Mesmer shows better performance with longer running times
 compared to Stardist.

 - Mesmer runs on small cropped tiles; additional resources are
 needed to apply it to full-slide images.

 - Tile stitching consideration: ensure proper handling of cells in
 overlapping regions.

 - Black areas (bubbles) can cause segmentation error -- better
 skipping and need to filter or avoid black tiles.

- **Benefits of Mesmer:**

 - Performs more accurately on thicker tissue sections.

 - This improved performance will benefit future projects,
 including proteopicks using thicker sections.

- **Optimization Plan:**

 - Ziqi will spend one week post-Easter to optimize and upgrade the
 Mesmer-based segmentation pipeline. Lina has scripts that could
 support this process.

- **Backup Plan:**

 - If whole-slide segmentation is not feasible, fallback will be to
 crop individual TLS regions and segment them using Mesmer
 separately.

**2. Tissue Section Thickness Considerations**

- **4 μm sections:**

 - Used in SPACE project, better but still suboptimal for
 segmentation of mature TLS structures.

- **5 μm sections (current study):**

vector_chunks.source_file: 4. Meetings & Updates/Meeting_notes/2025/15.04.25 Segmentation issues - Anniina, Ada, Hanna, Ziqi, Zhihan.md

ss Considerations**

 - Thinner sections are preferable for H&E staining.

 - Thicker sections are more suitable for RNA/protein profiling.

![](media/image1.png){width="6.299212598425197in"

**https://www.researchgate.net/publication/372203129_Germinal_center-dependent_and\_-independent_immune_responses_of_tumor-infiltrating_B_cells_in_human_cancers**

**IMMUNE INFILTRATION METRICS**

- **Normalized Mixing Score (NMS):** Measures the degree of \"mixing\"
 > between two cell types (e.g., CD8+ T cells and Cytokeratin+ tumor
 > cells).

- **Tumor-Immune Partitioning & Clustering (TIPC):** A newer algorithm
 > that uses hexagonal tessellation to partition the tissue into
 > sub-regions. It classifies regions as \"hot/infiltrated\" or
 > \"cold/excluded\" based on the ratio and clustering of immune vs.
 > tumor cells within each hexagon.

**The \"Invasive Front\" Distance:** 1. Define the tumor boundary (using
your Tumor label coordinates).\
2. Measure the distance from each **CD8** and **CD4** cell to the
nearest **B-cell cluster**.\
3. Measure the distance from those same T-cells to the **Tumor
boundary**.\
4.

tes).\
2. Measure the distance from each **CD8** and **CD4** cell to the
nearest **B-cell cluster**.\
3. Measure the distance from those same T-cells to the **Tumor
boundary**.\
4. **Metric:** The **\"Relay Ratio.\"** Are your T-cells concentrated
*between* the TLS and the tumor? This indicates the TLS is successfully
\"seeding\" the tumor.

**Intratumoral Mixing Score:** Use the ConvexHull logic on your
**Tumor** cells. Then, count how many **Myeloid** and **CD8** cells fall
inside that Hull.

- *Infiltration Degree* =
 > \$\\frac{N\_{CD8\\\_Inside}}{Area\_{Tumor\\\_Hull}}\$

**TLS-shape and proximity metrics**

- **Maturity:**For each B-cell cluster, calculate the percentage of
 > cells positive for BCL6 and Ki67.*High BCL6+/Ki67+ count* = Active
 > Secondary Follicle (highly prognostic).

- Presence of CD21 within a B-cell cluster confirms a \"Primary\" or
 > \"Secondary\" follicle rather than a loose aggregate

- **TLS Density:** Total number of TLS divided by the total tissue
 > area

- **TLS** shape and eccentricity

 - **Area-to-Perimeter Ratio** or **Circularity**

 - **High Circularity: Suggests a mature, secondary follicle (Stage
 > 3).**

 - **(Amoeboid): Suggests an early, disorganized B cell aggregate
 > (Stage 1). THIS IS DONE THROUGH SCIMAP CONVEXHULL**

- **Define Intra/Peritumoral:** TLS within a defined buffer (usually
 > **2 mm**) from the tumor margin.

B cell aggregate
 > (Stage 1). THIS IS DONE THROUGH SCIMAP CONVEXHULL**

- **Define Intra/Peritumoral:** TLS within a defined buffer (usually
 > **2 mm**) from the tumor margin.

- **Proximity to Tumor (NND):** The **Nearest Neighbor Distance** from
 > the center of each TLS to the nearest tumor cell boundary

**TLS definition**

- **The T-cell Recruitment Metric:** Count how many \$CD4\^+\$ and
 > \$CD8\^+\$ T cells fall within a 50µm buffer of your DBSCAN B cell
 > clusters**.**

**TLS-composition explorationn**

- **B-T Zone Polarization:** Use the Centroid Distance between the
 > B-cell cluster and the T-cell cluster. In mature TLS, these zones
 > are spatially segregated but adjacent.

- **Shannon Entropy (Spatial):** Measures the randomness of cell
 > distribution within the TLS. Lower entropy usually indicates a
 > more organized, \"mature\" TLS structure.

- Calculate the density of HEV1 and CD31 cells within or immediately
 > adjacent (e.g., within 50µm) to the cluster.

**Important!! Correlate CD8 to

**\####### EF-response**

### The \"Neighborhood\" Analysis (Cell-to-Cell)

Look at the immediate neighbors of these interface B cells. Use a 30 µm
radius and check for:

- B cell + Myeloid: Suggests antigen presentation or a \"suppressive\"

ell-to-Cell)

Look at the immediate neighbors of these interface B cells. Use a 30 µm
radius and check for:

- B cell + Myeloid: Suggests antigen presentation or a \"suppressive\"
 > niche if those myeloid cells are \$CD163\^+\$ (M2 macrophages).

- B cell + CD8: Suggests the B cells are acting as local
 > antigen-presenting cells to boost T-cell killing at the edge

Are they proliferating (\$Ki67\^+\$)? If B cells at the interface are
dividing, it suggests the tumor edge is a site of active immune
expansion, not just a passive graveyard.

Are they mature (\$BCL6\^+\$)? BCL6 is usually in the germinal center.
If you find \$BCL6\^+\$ B cells \"leaking\" into the tumor interface, it
indicates an extremely aggressive local immune response

**What is the relevance of studying the normal omentum?**

Omental milky spots (MSs) are pre-existing serosal immune niches that
appear in the peritoneal cavity and already contain B cells,
macrophages, HEV-like vessels, and CXCL13⁺ stromal/myeloid cells.

TLSs (tertiary lymphoid structures) in OvCa are linked to stronger local
immunity (CD8⁺ T cells, B cell activity) and often better prognosis.
Biologically, TLSs share features with SLOs (B/T zoning, HEVs, sometimes
germinal centers).

**Are omental MSs the "seeds" of TLSs in ovarian cancer metastases?**

1. Are MSs diverse or homogeneous in their composition and spatial
 > organization?

- **Ovarian cancer metastasis**

 - Fallopian tube (where ovca develop) is exposed to abdominal
 > cavity → tumor cells have an easy access to peritoneal fluid →
 > metastasis to peritoneum and omentum (Miyamoto 2023)

 - Milky spots are the primary site where tumor spread in omentum.
 > It has been found that microtumors are found solely in milky
 > spots. As tumor grows, it can then move/grow in adipose part
 > of omentum.

 - neutrophils (neutrophil extracellular traps) and macrophages
 > (through CCR1 ligand) promote tumor cell migration to
 > milky spots (Miyamoto 2023)

## Panoptic cell segmentation model for TLS+ samples (Oskari Lehtonen, Hautaniemi lab)

> https://github.com/HautaniemiLab/histolytics

- **Overview:**

 - Oskari has created a cell segmentation model to detect and
 > classify tumor/stromal compartments and tumor and immune cells
 > from H&E-stained tissue sections

 - cannot detect GCs \> need to be manually annotated if want
 > to be studied

 - macrophages cannot be distinguished from lymphocytes very
 > well

 - Enables also tissue segmentation but it cannot properly
 > separate adipose tissue from empty white spaces and we do
 > not really need it, we use only cell segmentation

## TLS identification using DBSCAN

- [After running cell segmentation for H&E images, further spatial
 > analyses are conducted to detect and define Laggs and TLS and to
 > map their localization within TME]{.mark}

- [Density-Based Spatial Clustering of Applications with Noise]{.mark}

- [Algorithm that uses two main parameters:]{.mark}

 - [Epsilon: The max distance at which two points are considered as
 > neighbors]{.mark}

 - [MinPts: The minimum number of points required to form a dense
 > region]{.mark}

 - [→ Cluster: at least a specified minimum number of points
 > located close enough to each other]{.mark}

> ![](media/image7.png){width="6.267716535433071in"
> height="2.5972222222222223in"}

 - **CD20:** B cells

 - in most Laggs and milky spots, scattered

 - found in immature (zones), and mature (around GC) TLS

 - only CD20+ Lagg potentially can develop into mature TLS

 - **Ki-67:** Proliferating cells

 - possible expression in milky spots and Lagg

 - higher but scattered in immature TLS

 - typically high and localized to GCs in mature TLS

 - ki-67 expression in GC refer to active GC/TLS

 - **CD21:** Follicular dendritic cells (FDCs)

 - immature TLS and mTLS (in GC)

 - can be some level, scattered expression also in Lagg

 - typically absent in milky spots

 - identification of GCs (follicular structures:
 > architecture/skeleton), also possibly differentiating
 > milky spots from Lagg

 - **Some articles state that iTLS do not have CD21+FDCs (for
 > example Sun 2024 rw). Still CD23+ should be defining
 > marker for mTLS**

 - **PNAd:** high endothelial venules (HEVs)

 - HEVs are specialized immune cells trafficking immune cells
 > into lymph nodes

 - strongest expression in mature TLS (all around TLS) where
 > HEVs are fully developed

 - possible weak expression also in immature TLS

## 15.04.2025

TLS project meeting

Today\'s meeting notes:

1\. TLS Segmentation Strategy

- Mesmer vs. Stardist:

 - Mesmer shows better performance with longer running times
 > compared to Stardist.

 > Mesmer runs on small cropped tiles; additional resources are
 > needed to apply it to full-slide images.

 > Tile stitching consideration: ensure proper handling of cells
 > in overlapping regions.

 > Black areas (bubbles) can cause segmentation error -- better
 > skipping and need to filter or avoid black tiles.

 > Benefits of Mesmer:

 - Performs more accurately on thicker tissue sections.

 > This improved performance will benefit future projects,
 > including proteopicks using thicker sections.

 > Optimization Plan:

 - Ziqi will spend one week post-Easter to optimize and upgrade the

formance will benefit future projects,
 > including proteopicks using thicker sections.

 > Optimization Plan:

 - Ziqi will spend one week post-Easter to optimize and upgrade the
 > Mesmer-based segmentation pipeline. Lina has scripts that
 > could support this process.

 > Backup Plan:

 - If whole-slide segmentation is not feasible, fallback will be to
 > crop individual TLS regions and segment them using Mesmer
 > separately.

2\. Tissue Section Thickness Considerations

- 4 μm sections:

 - Used in SPACE project, better but still suboptimal for
 > segmentation of mature TLS structures.

 > 5 μm sections (current study):

 - Chosen to collect more material for GeoMx analysis, causing
 > segmentation issues in dense areas like TLSs.

 > General Note:

 - Thinner sections are preferable for H&E staining.

 > Thicker sections are more suitable for RNA/protein profiling.

 > A future reporting system is applied to track section
 > thickness (in μm) used per experiment.

3\. Action Points & Next Steps

- By next Friday, Ziqi will assess feasibility of Mesmer WSI
 > optimization and decide whether the current plan needs to be
 > adjusted. Ada will assist Ziqi with all necessary existing
 > materials on the meeting of next Tuesday 22th. Zhihan will also
 > follow and assist with segmentation optimization.

 > Hanna will contact:

## 22.04.2025

Ziqi and Ada's meeting for segmentation test

We assessed together the output that Lina obtained with her last script.
Already we can tell it is viable to tile and untile. The main issues
were (1) time of run - it took 2 days to segment this image, (2)
malfunctioning while passing totally black tiles. You can see that the
image looks great but the edges are full of masked individual pixels.Our
hypothesis is that these two issues are interconnected - full black
tiles are hard to handle and consume a lot of time to be masked.

Ziqi will work on a way to avoid passing these to MESMER, and she will
implement some benchmarks (time per tile, time per image etc). I will
assist with big errors if any. We will re-evaluate based on her results

passing these to MESMER, and she will
implement some benchmarks (time per tile, time per image etc). I will
assist with big errors if any. We will re-evaluate based on her results
the strategies and things to try next week.

## Segmentation Test - MESMER

Codes are in Github

Test files are here:
P:\\h30492\\farkkilab2\\7_TLS\\Segmentation_test\\STARDIST_AND_MESMER

Based on Ziqi, Zhihan and Ada's work:

### **1. Image Preprocessing Workflow**

- Multichannel TIFF images were processed using:

 - **Gamma correction** (gamma=γ) to enhance contrast.

 - **Unsharp masking** (sigma=σ, strength=s) to sharpen nuclear
 > features.

- Channels (e.g., DAPI, membrane) were stacked into a 3D array format
 > and reshaped to comply with MESMER's input shape: (1, H, W, C).

### **2. Tiling Strategy**

- Images were divided into overlapping tiles using a fixed tile size
 > and dynamically computed overlaps to ensure full coverage.

ape: (1, H, W, C).

### **2. Tiling Strategy**

- Images were divided into overlapping tiles using a fixed tile size
 > and dynamically computed overlaps to ensure full coverage.

- This produced a tile array of shape (B, num_x, num_y, tile_x,
 > tile_y, C).

### **3. Filtering Background Tiles with GMM and Other Metrics**

To optimize nuclear segmentation by avoiding background-only regions,
several strategies were tested for identifying and excluding
low-information tiles before running MESMER. These methods aimed to
reduce unnecessary computation and improve segmentation quality.

#### **3.1 Baseline Thresholding with Mean Intensity**

- **Method**: Calculated the **mean pixel intensity** of each tile on
 > the DAPI channel (channel_idx=0).

- **Threshold**: Tiles with mean \< 3500 were excluded from
 > segmentation.

- **Outcome**: Performed well on images with consistent staining but
 > failed in cases with uneven illumination or high signal
 > heterogeneity.

#### **3.2 Comparison with Sum-Based Filtering**

- **Method**: Used np.sum() of DAPI intensity values instead of the
 > mean.

- **Observation**: Performed poorly despite identical tile sizes.
 > Since np.sum scales linearly with total intensity, it was more
 > sensitive to background noise and illumination bias, leading to
 > misclassification of low-content tiles.

s.
 > Since np.sum scales linearly with total intensity, it was more
 > sensitive to background noise and illumination bias, leading to
 > misclassification of low-content tiles.

- **Conclusion**: np.mean was more robust due to normalization by
 > pixel count.

#### **3.3 Feature Combination: Mean + Standard Deviation**

- **Method**: Combined **mean** and **standard deviation** (std) of
 > DAPI intensities per tile into 2D feature vectors.

- **Goal**: Capture both brightness and texture/variance of tiles.

- **Input to GMM**: X = \[\[mean₁, std₁\], \[mean₂, std₂\], \...,
 > \[meanₙ, stdₙ\]\]

- **Result**: Improved separation of background vs. foreground in
 > heterogeneous images.

#### **3.4 Median Intensity Filtering**

- **Trial**: Replaced mean with **median intensity** as a more
 > outlier-robust statistic.

- **Result**: Median has a close result compared to mthe ean.

#### **3.5 Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) Clustering**

- **Method**: Applied sklearn.mixture.GaussianMixture to intensity
 > features (mean or \[mean + std\]) across all tiles.

- **Parameters**:

 - n_components = 3 (assumed: background, low-signal, high-signal)

 - random_state = 0 for reproducibility

- **Label Assignment**:

 - Automatically determined the cluster with the **lowest mean** as
 > background (via
 > filter_tiles_by_mean_intensity_gmm_auto_background)

ducibility

- **Label Assignment**:

 - Automatically determined the cluster with the **lowest mean** as
 > background (via
 > filter_tiles_by_mean_intensity_gmm_auto_background)

 - Background labels were excluded from downstream prediction

Update GANTT diagram, set new goals, and divide tasks for the next meeting.

TheGeoMx Digital Spatial Profiler isabenchtop instrument designed tospatially resolve andcollect
genomic information from distinct, user- defined regions ofinterest. The GeoMx DSP instrument is
intended forResearch UseOnly.ThisGeoMx DSPInstrument UserManual isconcerned exclusively
with theoperation oftheGeoMx DSP instrument. Separate user manuals provide information onslide
preparation ,data readout byNGS ornCounter platform, and data analysis (see User Manuals and
Resources on page 20)Please refer totraining resources inNanoString University at
https://university.nanostring.com andaddress questions orconcerns tosupport.spatial@bruker.com .

GeoMxDSPWorkflow
InGeoMx Digital Spatial Profiling (DSP), antibody ornucleic acid probes arecoupled tophotocleavable
oligonucleotide tags. After probes hybridize totargets inslide- mounted tissue sections, the
oligonucleotide tags arereleased from discrete regions ofthetissue viaUVexposure. Released tags are
quantified bynCounter technology orIllumina Next Generation Sequencing (NGS). Counts aremapped
back totissue location, yielding aspatially resolved digital profile ofanalyte abundance (Figure 5).
lDay1:SlideStaining. Prepare slides and incubate biological targets with UV-cleavable probes.
Prepare manually orusing the BOND RX/RXmFully Automated IHC/ISH Stainer from Leica
Biosystems®.
lDay2:Process SlidesonGeoMxDSP. Load prepared slides intotheGeoMx DSP instrument. Slides
arescanned tocapture fluorescent images used toselect regions ofinterest (ROIs). ROIs may be
segmented intodiscrete compartments orareas ofillumination (AOI). Theinstrument collects UV-
cleaved oligos from theAOIs intothewells ofacollection plate.
ForNGSreadout:
Day3:Transfer thecollected aspirates toaPCR plate and
perform LibraryPrep with SeqCode orProCode primers.
Pool andpurify theproducts, thenSequence onanIllumina
NGS instrument.
Day4:Process FASTQ sequencing filesintodigital count
conversion (DCC) filesusing theGeoMxNGSPipeline on
standalone software orIllumina's DRAGEN™ platform

nIllumina
NGS instrument.
Day4:Process FASTQ sequencing filesintodigital count
conversion (DCC) filesusing theGeoMxNGSPipeline on
standalone software orIllumina's DRAGEN™ platform
accessed viaBaseSpace™ Sequence Hub(cloud) or
NextSeq 1000/2000 (local).FornCounter readout:
Day2,continued :Transfer the
collected aspirates toahybridization
plate along with GeoMx HybCode
reagents. Hybridization occurs
overnight.
Day3:Pool wells andProcess on
annCounter ProorMAX/FLEX
Analysis System or
SPRINT Profiler togenerate
reporter count conversion (RCC)
files.
lDay4or5:Upload counts toGeoMx DSP andcreateastudy intheData Analysis Suite. Perform
quality- control checks anddata analysis, andgenerate analysis plots.

Before the system's 20x scan, it checks the tissue area. You may accept the default parameters

Define Scan Area
The scan area result is displayed (Figure 23). Use the tools in this window to select onlythe tissue on the
slide. This minimizes scan time and avoids unnecessary consumption of disk space. Areas within green
boundary lines will be scanned. To adjust:
lZoom in andoutusing the scroll wheel on your mouse or a pinch-zoom movement on your touch
screen or touch pad.
lAdjust the scan area using the X- and Y- sliders to define the area you would like to analyze.
lUse theMinimum Tissue slider to filter out particles from the image. Sliding it too far may cause small
areas of tissue to be excluded from the image.
lUse theSensitivity slider to adjust the intensity at which the instrument identifies tissue.

 The minimum scan area is 1.5 mm x 1.5 mm.

ROI Transfer
ROI Transfer copies ROIs from an existing scan onto another scan. This feature is useful for:
lTransferring ROIs from one section to a serial section.
lCreating ROI templates for large slide cohorts (to keep numbers, types, and labels of ROIs
consistent).
lRescanning failed slides.
Only scans with status Scanned orAnnotated can have ROIs transferred onto them.
1. Open the scan to which you will transfer ROIs in the Scan Workspace, and select the Overlays button
to open the Overlays Panel (Figure 44).
2. Click Transfer to open the Select Scan window, and select the scan from which to copy ROIs. Click
OK.
3. The ROI Transfer process begins and progress is shown on the screen. The time to transfer depends
on the size of the scanned tissue and the number of ROI to be transferred.
4. Anoverlay including ROIs is displayed on the current scan. Adjust the overlay using the tools Move,
Scale ,Resize,Rotate, or Mirror Image , to position the ROIs as you wish.
lROIs that are positioned outside the boundary of the destination scan will not transfer.
lROIs that are resized and are now larger than the maximum or smaller than the minimum ROI size will
not transfer.
lA rectangle ROIs that is rotated will transfer as a 4-sided polygon.

that are resized and are now larger than the maximum or smaller than the minimum ROI size will
not transfer.
lA rectangle ROIs that is rotated will transfer as a 4-sided polygon.
lA circle ROI that is stretched to an ellipse will transfer as a circle.

Finalize the Collection Plate
When you finalize the collection plate, you are designating the group of samples that will be processed
together in the subsequent readout steps.
lPlates are finalized by row , not by well, even if some wells in the row are unused.
lOnce you've finalized a set of rows as a readout group, you cannot add more unfinalized rows to
that readout group. If you intend to add additional aspirates to a readout group, do not finalize the
collection plate until all aspirates have been collected.
lYou can later re-insert the plate and use the unused rows:
• If the previous collection has been finalized, a different readout group must be used.
Collection will begin in the next available row of the plate.
• If the previous collection has not been finalized, the same readout group must be used.
Collection will begin in the next available well of the plate, unless the new collection is
with a different kit configuration, in which case it will begin in the next available row.

Deleting Scans and Studies
Scans must be in one of the following states to be deleted: Readout Complete, Scan Only, Error, or
Aborted.
Delete scans and studies from the system by right-clicking on the item's name in the navigation window
and selecting Delete (Figure 69).
Figure 69: Delete an item.The software asks to confirm the deletion in a pop-up window.
The deleted item is then moved to the Trash folder, listed as the
first item in the navigation pane.
IMPORTANT: Do not delete studies or scans that you plan to import back to the instrument at a later time.
Only delete data that you no longer need.
Click on the Trash folder to view the studies and
scans in the Trash (Figure 70).
From the Trash folder, deleted items may be
restored to Records by selecting the item and
then clicking Restore .
Figure 70: Trash folder with Restore and Delete buttons
outlined.Administrative-level users have the additional
option to permanently delete an item by
selecting the item and clicking Delete. This
action removes the item from the instrument and
the archive. It cannot be restored. Disable the Delete only items successfully transferred to AtoMx
SIP selection at the top right of the Trash window to enable permanent deletion. (AtoMx SIP connection
will be enabled in an upcoming software release).

NOTE: When a top-level item (e.g. folder) is moved to the Trash and deleted, all sub-level items will be
deleted. Sub-items cannot be selected individually for deletion from the Trash. To delete an individual
item, restore the top-level item to the Records folder, then select the individual item for deletion.

Administration Menu
Data Exchange
Data Exchange allows you to move GeoMx DSP scans and studies to and from any GeoMx DSP
instrument. This function is only available when logged in locally to the GeoMx system, not over remote
Chrome connection. External drives must be NTFS or exFAT format (NTFS recommended) and USB 3.0
is recommended over USB 2.0. SeagateExpansion Portable Hard Drives work well. Do not use a drive
greater than 10 TB.
It is recommended to transfer scans only when they have status Readout Complete. Repeating data exchange
will not overwrite previously transferred scan or study data.
IMPORTANT:
 Do not export studies and then delete them from
the instrument for re-import later, in an attempt to gain space on
the local drive. Re-importing deleted studies may result in
conflicts with data stored on the auxiliary server.
ExportingGeoMxscansandstudies
1. Plug the external drive into the GeoMx instrument's USB port.
Log into the GeoMx system as an administrative user.
2. Click on Data Exchange from the Administration menu. Within
the Data Exchange window, select the scans and/or studies to
export (Figure72).
The GeoMx system will automatically include corresponding scan data
with an exported study. Use Ctrl+click to select multiple files. It is not
possible to select entire folders.
3.

The GeoMx system will automatically include corresponding scan data
with an exported study. Use Ctrl+click to select multiple files. It is not
possible to select entire folders.
3. Once the selection(s) have been highlighted, select Export.
4. View available external drives (NTFS or exFAT format). Consider available space on any drives present
and the estimated size of the export (Figure 73). Select a drive to which to export and click Confirm.
Figure 73: Selecting external drive5. The Notification Bell in the Control Center will inform when
export is complete. Allow the export to fully complete before
starting a new export.
Figure 72: Navigation window for
Data Exchange study selection

browser. Users may connect their GeoMx DSP to their Illumina BaseSpace workgroup(s) to enable
seamless end-to-end workflows for experiments with NGS readout. These workflows allow a
sequencing job to be sent from the GeoMx DSP directly to the BaseSpace Sequence Hub, eliminating
the file processing steps of the conventional workflow.

Emptying the waste
The system will check the waste level periodically; if the waste is too full, you will be prompted to empty
it. You can also click on the Reagent Status Indicator in the GeoMx DSP Control Center to initiate the
waste emptying process if the instrument is in an idle software state (see Bring GeoMx DSP Software to
anIdleSoftware State onpage 92).
1. When prompted by the system, open the reagent bay door. Slide the reagent tray out for better
accessibility.
Figure 101: Disconnecting GeoMx
DSP waste container2. Remove the fitting from the waste bottle cap by pinching the
dark gray button on the QuickConnect with thumb and
forefinger (Figure 101).
3. Remove the waste bottle from the reagent bay holder (Figure 102).
Figure 102: Removing GeoMx DSP
waste4. Unscrew and remove the cap assembly from the waste bottle.
5. Empty the waste bottle.
6. Rinse the waste bottle several times, eliminating all foam.
Residual bubbles can falsely trigger the Waste Full sensor.
7. Screw the cap back on, insert the waste bottle into the reagent
bay of the instrument, and reattach the fitting, ensuring that the
QuickConnect clicks in tightly.
IMPORTANT:
 Use clean gloved hands during this process. Ensure that the bottles are securely

nstrument, and reattach the fitting, ensuring that the
QuickConnect clicks in tightly.
IMPORTANT:
 Use clean gloved hands during this process. Ensure that the bottles are securely
connected to their fittings after exchanging reagents or emptying waste. A connection that is not secure
may leak and cause damage to the instrument.
8. Gently push the reagent tray back in and close the door.

Cleaning theSlideHolder
Clean theslide holder after each runtoensure proper instrument function.
1.Ifnecessary, remove buffer from loaded slides with apipette andthen remove slides from theslide
holder.
2.Rinse theslide holder thoroughly with distilled water from asquirt bottle. Gently patdrywith
Kimwipes.
3.Inspect theentire slide holder. Ensure thatthesprings ofeach slide clamp andthegasket (blue rubber)
ofeach slide slotareclear ofdebris from salt,dust, orlint.
4.Ifnecessary, gaskets may beremoved forthorough cleaning with distilled water. Allow toairdryorpat
drywith Kimwipes. Inspects fortears ordamage. Carefully replace gaskets toslide holder, ensuring a
good fit.
Tominimize wear andtearongaskets, avoid frequent removal from theslide holder. Abrief cleaning following
each instrument runshould keep thegaskets clean without needing toremove them from theslide holder.
5.Visually inspect thedigital micromirror device (DMD) calibration target (Figure 104) toensure itisclear
ofalldebris. Donotcontact thetarget unless necessary togently wipe away debris. DO NOT apply
pressure tothetarget.
6.Return theslide holder totheGeoMx instrument between runs.

other nuclease- free) water. When desired, thesystem canbereturned tooperational asdescribed
below.

indelible penmark inthecenter andinthelabel area may suffice.

precautions, including useofpersonal protective equipment such assafety glasses andgloves.

ethanol .Adisinfectant such asDNAZap™from Invitrogen isrecommended. Useadamp towel rather
than spraying directly ontheinstrument.

rather than spraying directly ontheinstrument.
Replace AirFilter
ELECTRICAL HAZARD :Donotattempt todisassemble theinstrument atanytime. An
electric shock canoccur iftheinstrument isoperated without itsouter case. Properly
shutdown, then disconnect theinstrument from thepower source before attempting to
replace theairfilter.
Theairfilter located ontheback oftheinstrument willneed occasional replacement, typically every 12–
18months. Ifthefilter appears tobevisibly obstructed bydebris, contact support.spatial@bruker.com to

Samples with immune aggregates have been annotated in QuPath

aggregate 
Germinal 
center In total, we have data for WES/RNA seq of 206 patients 
- Matilda extracted successfully the clinical data for patients from 
S002-S204 (first batch)

Germinal centers ONLY appear in omentum tissues, and 
only one ovarian sample presents immune aggregates
Cohort summary

Immune aggregate presence varies across 
treatment-molecular profile groups

Germinal center presence 
- i_HRPs are few but they all have immune aggregate presence, opposite to what occurs in pHRD samples 
- The proportion of pHRP seems to be higher in the immune aggregate positive group

Low-PFS IDS HRP patients did not develop GC, but were 
able to establish immune aggregates

Idea
TLS Histology Atlas: 
● Superficial atlas on distinct tissue types 
● Later -> Plan a big tCycIF 
Heterogeneity in TLS/GC Presence 
● Why do HRP PDS/IDS samples display heterogeneity in TLS and GC presence? 
● Can observed heterogeneity inform prognosis or response to therapies? 
Patterns in Tissue Architecture 
● Are there distinct patterns, neighborhoods or structural changes within tissue architecture correlated with the 
presence of TLS? 
● How might these patterns influence the clinical outcomes? 
Comparison of TIL and TLS Regions 
● How do TILs and TLS-containing samples differ spatially and functionally? 
● Can these differences help to classify patients or tumor behavior? 
Proteomic and Transcriptomic Insights 
● Are there relevant proteomic or transcriptomic mechanisms in distinct areas of the tissue linked with TIL or TLS 
content? (sampling in GC, T cell zone, stroma, tumor) 
● Microenvironment/picks vs WSI scrapes 
● How might these mechanisms reveal therapeutic targets or biomarkers?

Tile filter system

Additional improvements on the filtering – sum/Integrated intensity
Additional improvements on the filtering – improved median threshold=3500
Additional improvements on the filtering – mean threshold=4000

Additional improvements on the filtering – mean+std+entropy - PCA+K-means

Perform dimensionality reduction (PCA or UMAP) and clustering (KMeans) on the simplified features of all tiles (mean DAPI, std DAPI, entropy). The clusters of pure background tiles can be recognized as cluster 1

Additional improvements on the filtering – Gaussian mixture model (GMM)

A Gaussian mixture model (GMM) is a machine learning method used to determine the probability each data point belongs to a given cluster. The model is a soft clustering method used in unsupervised learning.

components = 3

Pick the one with the lowest mean as background. Now we can automatically rank the GMM components and select the background cluster based on the lowest tile mean.

QC-Part1 SelectROIs in Cylinter

Images were processed with Cylinter 0.5 module selectROIs to remove:
Big detached areas
Bubbles, intensity blobs, hairs etc
Segmented cells around the tissue, eg near the borders, in corners etc
Segmentation artifacts
High autofluorescent regions within blood vessels that have been 
No area/intensity filters will be conducted in cylinter

2. UNIFORM

UNIFORM is based on the idea of matching distributions. Therefore, to ensure no inference of false positive cells (small masked pixels), we removed the <30px Area cells to calculate the shifts. Then, all data, with no exceptions, was normalized and exported

2. UNIFORM- Reference sample exporation

UNIFORM requires a reference sample as the model distribution for the algorithm. Results might vary a lot depending on which sample is the reference

Example: Marker normalized with two different reference samples

I iteratively tested every marker with every image, selected the best matching references and checked that the image patterns worked well before constructing the reference dictionnary

2. UNIFORM- Reference sample exporation

Test example on CD8a

3. QC pipeline2 - Area filter

Areas were QCd based on Area thresholds to remove False Negatives
Threshold was visualized over four images proving to be effective
Area= 30px

Selected cells
4. QC pipeline2 - DNA1/DNA7 Threshold
RESULTS - Around 90% of the full dataset was preserved

WHITE TOP-HAT FILTERING

5. White top-hat filtering

Some channels were later corrected to remove extreme background effects

Example:

High intensity artifacts

Problem: In working antibodies, scanning-related artifacts are as high intensity as the TLS areas. Example: CD20. This is common to >⅓ of the slides.

White top-hat filtering

1. Morphological Opening:
A morphological opening is an operation that combines erosion followed by dilation, using a structuring element.
The structuring element acts as a probe that slides over the image, and the erosion removes parts of the image that don't "fit" inside the structuring element. Then, dilation expands the remaining parts back out, but only to the extent allowed by the structuring element's shape and size.
In white top-hat filtering, the opening operation removes larger foreground objects and smooths out the background. 
2. White Top-Hat Calculation:
The white top-hat transform is then calculated by subtracting the opened image from the original image.
This subtraction isolates the bright features (details) in the original image that were removed by the opening operation

TLS Atlas - Our Cohort

Data available for WES/RNA sequencing of 206 patients.

#1 - H&E seg and low-plex CycIF

Hanna

Milky spots

>100 MS in human omentum, 300-700 um diameter

induce inflammation but lack DCs → no antigen presenting capability

unlike conventional lymphoid organs, develop in the absence of LTi

primary colonization site for tumor cells in omentum → can be later replaced with tumor mass

Fibroblastic reticular cells (FRC) are specialized lymphoid organ fibroblasts surrounding and supporting MS, Lagg, and TLS (increasing expression MS → Lagg → iTLS → mTLS)

Milky spots vs. Lagg

Lagg develop in response during inflammation but also MS may activate in some level

less HEVs: ICs in Lagg are recruited via HEVs
lower IL-6 expression
less proliferating cells (Ki-67)
less abundant and less active fibroblastic reticular cell (FRC) network around MS (=lower PDPN expression)
more or less M1/M2 macrophages? M1 count increases in the beginning of inflammation but M2 more prevalent in chronic inflammation and cancer

How many “Lagg” in malignant samples are actually MS
Are some ben/bord aggregates Lagg rather than MS

BCR profiling - SCellex

Main aims:
Verify the feasibility of the SCellex platform for BCR (and optional TCR) on our samples in FF and FFPE. 
STEPS:
Test in the tubes/whole FFPE 
whether SCellex's BCR/TCR amplification can work (gene-specific / random amplification) 
compared with Bulk BCR results
Test on regional FFPE tissue and the spotting regional barcode 
whether barcode beads on tissue spots can preserve spatial information and reconstruct BCR/TCR
FF samples for selected samples
TODO:
Sample selection for FFPE and sectioning + FF samples
2 tube - 1 with single scraped sample / 1 with multi scraped samples

BCR profiling - MiXCR for Bulk RNAseq

Main aims:
The BCR sequence was extracted and reconstructed from the existing Bulk RNA-seq data on the iCAN platform using the MiXCR software to identify clonal and immune diversity, and compared with morphology and expression data (B cell enrichment level, TLS group, etc.).
Compare MiXCR results with deconvolution results from Kassandra & PRISM. 
STEPS:
Cohort selection and prepare Bulk RNAseq data in personal folder
Run the MiXCR pipeline to perform BCR sequence assembly and clonotype calling
QC for MiXCR results
Use the R package immunarch for downstream visualization and statistical analysis
Compare with deconvolution results

GeoMx - Biological questions and next steps

Main questions:
TLS functionality
Is the emergence/maturation of TLS associated with antitumor immunity?
Do the distributions of immunosuppressive markers and activation markers differ in TLS at different maturity levels?
TLS maturation axis
How can the continuous spectrum of TLS from basic to mature be quantified?
Recruitment / positioning mechanism of TLS
How are TLS B/T cell recruitment and spatial positioning driven by chemokine?
Cell interactions and spatial mechanisms of TLS
How do B/T cell colocalization patterns change with TLS maturity?
Do specific cell-cell interaction patterns exist?
Clonality and function
TLS in TME
Does TLS drive the distribution or functional state of immune cells in the tumor microenvironment?
Treatment relevance & predictive markers

Can we find distinct spatial conformations of TLS? How do they correlate with GeoMx data and trajectory? And with the BCR/iCAN data?
How do iTLS/mTLS presence and abundance correlate with other tissue domains (e.g., immune hubs) and immune infiltration?

Next 3-month Plans

Obtain UTAG Domains: to gather more information of the tumor architecture
TLS Hub Annotation: Annotate the identified TLS hubs using the Spatial cells package//spacestat?
Explore TLS Composition: Investigate the cellular composition across the identified TLS hubs.

How TLS profile is associated with clinical and pathological factors? 
Can we reliably distinguish TLS maturation stages visually from H&Es?

Main questions
Defining TLS Maturation Trajectories (LAgg → iTLS → mTLS) and validating pseudo-time inference - real?
What are the molecular dynamics of TLS structural formation and functional maturation? - sequential molecular programs and temporal dynamics

GeoMx - Biological questions and next steps

Core objective: To understand the biological functions, molecular characteristics, and spatial organization of TLS at different maturation stages (lagg → iTLS → mTLS), while exploring heterogeneity within TLS and between patients.
Main questions:
Defining TLS Maturation Trajectories (LAgg → iTLS → mTLS) and Validating pseudo-time inference
Can pseudo-time trajectories capture the maturation gradient of TLS?
Which genes or pathways show linear or non-linear changes across pseudo-time？
How can we delineate early/intermediate/late TLS stages based on transcriptomic signatures?
Which canonical chemokines best distinguish TLS maturation stages? Are chemokine gradients spatially aligned with pseudo-time ordering?

What are the molecular dynamics of TLS structural formation and functional maturation?
Identify the sequential molecular programs driving TLS maturation
Do key programs such as B cell proliferation, SHM/CSR, terminal differentiation, and Tfh helper signaling initiate in the expected order?
What are the temporal dynamics of different functional modules during TLS/GC maturation? Are there any coordination or asynchrony between modules?
Does TB maturation precede or follow GC maturation, and what might that imply for TLS functionality?

GeoMx - Biological questions and next steps

Main questions:
Intra- and Inter-Patient Heterogeneity of TLS
Intra-patient heterogeneity
Are TLS maturation levels consistent within a single patient, or do multiple maturation states coexist?
Does TLS location (intra-tumoral vs. stromal/peritumoral) affect maturation level or composition?
Is TLS heterogeneity correlated with patients (HRD/HRP, BRCA status, immune infiltration pattern)?

Clinical Relevance and Predictive Potential
Are there stage-specific markers predictive of treatment response or resistance?

Spatial Ecosystem Integration
How does TLS maturation relate to the composition and spatial organization of the surrounding tumor microenvironment (e.g., myeloid or stromal niches)?
Are there specific cell–cell interaction motifs that emerge or disappear with TLS maturation?

Cell Phenotyping TLS-IDS

Examples

The binary classification of TLSs into immature TLSs (iTLSs)/early TLSs (eTLSs) and mature TLSs (mTLSs)

1) early TLSs (eTLSs), in which lymphocytes aggregate diffusely and CD21+ cells are scarce, and 2) follicle-formed mature TLSs, in which the follicular morphology of SLOs and CD21+ follicular DCs (FDCs) are distributed in a reticular pattern (17). Mature TLSs (mTLSs) with GCs and DCs reportedly represent privileged sites for local antigen presentation and contribute to the generation of tumor-targeting effector T cells and B cell

Nielsen et al. discovered that typical markers of antigen-presenting cells, such as MHC class I/II, CD40, CD80, and CD86, were expressed on TLS-associated B cells in HGSOC. As these TLS-associated B cells are disconnected from serum autoantibodies and colocalize with CD8+ T cells, Nielsen et al. suggested that instead of producing antibodies, the predominant function of TLS-associated B cells in HGSOC is to present antigens to CD8+ T cells

SPACEstat: Defining TLS

Size/cell count-based classification
TLS: immature TLS (20-300 nuclei), intermediate TLS (300-500 nuclei), mature TLS (500+ nuclei) (MacFawn 2024)

Cell count, type and proximity-based classification
Lagg: epithelial enrichment score <0.8, combined T and B cell enrichment scores >1, clusters must include >50 cells, cells must be within 15 µm of each other (Xu 2024)

Cell count, type and zoning (Kroeger 2016)
type I: 20-50 cells, T and B cells, occasionally DCs > “small Lagg” 
type II: 100+ cells, T and B cells, lacked discrete zones or follicles > “large Lagg”
type III: B cell follicles with GC-like structures, discrete T cell zone, DCs and HEV-like vessels > “mature TLS”
type IV: 100-300 B cells, DC follicles, few T cells > as primarily found in omentum, these were speculated to be mostly normal milky spots

Milky spots: 300-750 µm diameter (Liu 2016, Okabe 2024)

**Histopathological Characterization of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures
across Ovarian Cancer Histotypes and Molecular profiles in Ovarian
Cancer.**

**Authors:** Hanna Elomaa^1^, Ada Junquera^1^, Zhihan Liang^1^, Matilda
Salko^1^, Oskari Lehtonen^1^, Joona Sarkkinen^2^, Anni Virtanen^1,3^,
Anniina Färkkilä^1,4,5^

**Introduction**

Tertiary lymphoid structures (TLSs) are ectopic immune formations
associated with anti-tumor immunity, favorable prognosis, and improved
immunotherapy responses in several solid tumors. However, the
distribution and function of TLSs across ovarian cancer histologies are
poorly characterized. TLSs display a maturation spectrum, from
disorganized lymphoid aggregates to highly organized structures with
elevated immunological activity. In this study, we systematically
assessed the prevalence, maturation, and clinical relevance of TLSs, and
examined their associations with molecular features and treatment
strategies to clarify their role across ovarian cancer histotypes.

**Material and method**

Hematoxylin and eosin-stained tumor sections from a prospective cohort
of 283 ovarian cancer patients were analyzed to characterize TLSs using
a deep learning-based segmentation pipeline. Tumor mutational and gene
expression profiles were analysed using whole-exome sequencing,
homologous recombination deficiency (HRD) testing and deconvoluted bulk
RNA sequencing.

e. Tumor mutational and gene
expression profiles were analysed using whole-exome sequencing,
homologous recombination deficiency (HRD) testing and deconvoluted bulk
RNA sequencing. Using multi-modal spatial data, TLSs were classified
into lymphoid aggregates, immature TLSs, and mature TLSs. The spatial
localization and size of TLSs were recorded, and they were
computationally segmented for quantification of cellular composition and
structure. TLS patterns were compared across histotypes, and associated
with the molecular and clinical data of the patients, including
responses to chemotherapy and overall survival.

**Results and discussion**

The most common histotypes were omental (65%), adnexal (19%), and
ovarian (11%) tumors, reflecting typical primary and metastatic site
distribution. We observed marked heterogeneity in the presence, number,
and localization of TLSs. TLSs were detected in 49 percent of tumors,
most commonly as lymphoid aggregates and predominantly enriched in the
omentum. Immature or mature TLSs were found in 22 percent of tumors and
were exclusively located in the omentum. Their absence in other sites
suggests that TLS maturation could be influenced by the local tissue
context, with the omentum potentially providing favorable stromal or
immunological conditions for their development. Given that mature TLSs

could be influenced by the local tissue
context, with the omentum potentially providing favorable stromal or
immunological conditions for their development. Given that mature TLSs
have been linked to improved survival and better responses to
immunotherapy in other solid tumors, their presence in ovarian cancer
may hold prognostic and therapeutic relevance. Linking TLS features with
genomic and expression profiles may reveal drivers of TLS development
and clarify their significance.

**Conclusion**\
Our findings highlight the importance of histotypes and molecular
profiles in shaping anti-tumor immune responses and should be considered
when evaluating TLSs as prognostic or therapeutic targets in ovarian
cancer histotypes.

*A Spatially Resolved Multi-Omics Atlas of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures
and Tumor-Infiltrating Lymphocytes in Ovarian Cancer*

Authors: Zhihan Liang^1^, Ada Junquera^1^, Hanna Elomaa^1^, Lina
Maltrovsky^1^, Joona Sarkkinen^2^, Iga Niemiec^1^, Aleksandra
Shabanova^1^, Anni Virtanen^1,4^, Matilda Salko^1^, Anniina
Färkkilä^1,3,5,6^

1 Research Program in Systems Oncology, University of Helsinki,
Helsinki, Finland

2 Translational Immunology Research Program, University of Helsinki and
Helsinki University Hospital, Helsinki, Finland

3 Department of Obstetrics and Gynecology, Department of Oncology,
Clinical Trials Unit, Comprehensive Cancer Center, Helsinki University
Hospital, Helsinki, Finland

4 Department of Pathology, University of Helsinki and HUS Diagnostic
Center, Helsinki University Hospital, Helsinki, Finland

5 iCAN Digital Precision Cancer Medicine Flagship, Helsinki, Finland

6 Institute for Molecular Medicine Finland, Helsinki Institute for Life
Sciences, University of Helsinki, Helsinki, Finland

**Introduction**

Tertiary lymphoid structures (TLSs) and tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes
(TILs) are key components of the tumor microenvironment, facilitating
local anti-tumor immune responses. TLSs presence has been linked to
improved outcomes in ovarian cancer, yet their spatial organization and
molecular characteristics remain underexplored. We constructed a

esponses. TLSs presence has been linked to
improved outcomes in ovarian cancer, yet their spatial organization and
molecular characteristics remain underexplored. We constructed a
spatially resolved multi-omics atlas by integrating histopathology,
transcriptomics, proteomics, and multiplexed imaging with genomic and
clinical data (homologous recombination status, PDS/IDS) to characterize
TLSs and TILs features, aiming to reveal their clinical associations and
underlying mechanisms in ovarian cancer.

**Material and method**

Hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) sections from over 200 Oncosys-Ova samples
were annotated to identify immune aggregates, immature TLSs, and mature
TLSs with germinal centers (GCs).

From these, 45 formalin-fixed paraffin-embedded (FFPE) high-grade serous
ovarian cancer (HGSC) omentum samples were selected for detailed spatial
single-cell, transcriptomic and proteomic profiling. All samples were
collected post-neoadjuvant chemotherapy during interval debulking
surgery, accompanied by clinical and WES + bulk RNA sequencing data.
t-CyCIF imaging was performed using a 30-marker immune antibody panel,
and adjacent sections underwent spatial transcriptomic profiling by
GeoMx, focusing on selected regions of interest (ROIs) within TLSs and
TILs. Proteomic profiling was conducted via proteo-picking, a novel

went spatial transcriptomic profiling by
GeoMx, focusing on selected regions of interest (ROIs) within TLSs and
TILs. Proteomic profiling was conducted via proteo-picking, a novel
microtissue extraction method, followed by mass spectrometry to identify
proteins across TLS compartments.

**Result and discussion**

Our analysis revealed heterogeneity in TLSs and TILs features across
ovarian cancer samples. Using an algorithm-assisted approach, H&E
annotation identified distinct immune aggregate patterns, with TLSs
maturity varying between patients. Notably, patients with poor prognosis
and homologous recombination proficiency lacked GCs, despite numerous
immune aggregates. Spatial metrics, including TLSs proximity to tumor,
were calculated to assess TLSs distribution and functionality across
molecular subtypes and clinical outcomes.

Processed t-CycIF images were analyzed using SCIMAP and UTAG to uncover
functional immune composition within TLSs/TILs. Based on these results,
we selected 3 ROIs per TLSs on the adjacent sections: GCs, surrounding
T-B cells, and stromal areas, alongside another 5 ROIs per sample
representing immune aggregates.

In total, 268 ROIs were profiled by GeoMx to obtain spatial gene
expression data from TLSs/TILs compartments. These ROIs also guided
spatially matched proteomic analysis and achieved comprehensive
multi-omics data integration.

ain spatial gene
expression data from TLSs/TILs compartments. These ROIs also guided
spatially matched proteomic analysis and achieved comprehensive
multi-omics data integration.

**Conclusion**

Our spatially resolved multi-omics atlas reveals patient-specific
heterogeneity in TLS maturity, spatial organization, and immune
composition, uncovering mechanistic insights into immune activation
linked to clinical outcomes.

**Material and Methods**

HE

**tCycIF**

Samples were stained consecutively using a panel of validated antibodies
(IDS samples: Table XX, PDS and milky-spots samples: Table XX ) and
scanned on a RareCyte CyteFinder system according to the standard
t-CyCIF protocol (CITE) at a resolution of 0.65 um pixel with 2x2
binning. Raw image files underwent illumination correction using the
BaSiC tool. Stitching and registration were performed utilizing ASHLAR
(v1.17.0) (CITE) to generate unified, multi-channel OME-TIFF images.
Nuclear segmentation was performed using Mesmer (CITE), a deep
learning-based segmentation algorithm.

To address low signal-to-noise ratios (SNR) and mitigate tissue
autofluorescence, a morphological white top-hat filter was applied to a
subset of the lineage marker channels (Table XX) across all images. Mean
fluorescence intensities for individual cells were quantified from the
filtered images using Python's scikit-image library via custom in-house
scripts:
[[https://github.com/farkkilab/image_processing]{.underline}](https://github.com/farkkilab/image_processing)
. Fluorescence intensities were log-transformed and normalized through
the UNIFORM Python package (CITE).

Single-cell phenotyping was performed hierarchically and iteratively
using TRIBUS (CITE), which annotates cells semi-automatically and
iteratively through SOM- based clustering.

.

Single-cell phenotyping was performed hierarchically and iteratively
using TRIBUS (CITE), which annotates cells semi-automatically and
iteratively through SOM- based clustering. Single-cell feature
quantification table and cell phenotype marker positivity and negativity
were used as inputs. Semi-automated labels were further curated through
manual gates of the markers used for cell type characterization (Table
XX).

Recurrent cellular neighborhoods (RCNs) were computed using the Scimap
Python package. Neighborhood matrixes were computed using
tl.spatial_count with a r=100px (\~65um), and the resulting neighborhood
matrix was then clustered using tl.spatial_cluster using kmeans
clustering with k=8.
