#6 EF cells?

EF cells are fighting a dense matrix and they look exhausted (DUSP1, SOCS3)

IGHM

Main results so far (20-02-2026)
0- tCycIF: we observe EF Bcells s in tCycIF, near tumor and surrounded by stroma and other immune cells. Average distances from B cells to tumor and stroma cells are significantly shorter from EF than from TLS sites. Distances to other B cells average 20um, while in EF the average distance triplicates (60um), suggestive for looser distributions and increased heterotypic interactions.

Regarding functional states, few samples contain >5% Ki67+ of their EF B cells with higher BCL6+ frequency ( 5-15%) - I THINK THIS IS A TECHNICAL ARTIFACT FROM BCL6 stromal autofluorescence. BCL6+ and Ki67+ status of the TLSs (Averaged by sample) seems to correlate nicely with the EF functional status. EF B cell counts correlates with TLS Bcell counts as defined by DBSCAN density-based clustering. Both the functional status and count correlation with B cells in TLS suggest EF cells might be sourced out of the TLSs. 

Xenium: EF, TLS and LAgg areas were annotated based on tCycIF CD20+ stainings and Xenium morphology stainings, obtaining 182 regions from 5 patients.
 
1- Composition : EF B cell-containing areas present a smaller b cell fraction than LAgg and mature TLSs. Also lower CD8Tcell and myeloid content.

ing 182 regions from 5 patients.
 
1- Composition : EF B cell-containing areas present a smaller b cell fraction than LAgg and mature TLSs. Also lower CD8Tcell and myeloid content. As expected, EF sites present higher fractions of Fibroblasts and Tumor (explained by their spatial location), as well as higher % of plasma cells. EF sites are heterogeneous and display distinct phenotypic composition

2. DGE of phenotypes across TLS and EF.

EF B cells display high expression of various immunoglobulin heavy and light chains (IGKC, IGHG4, IGHG3, IGHGP). This indicates that EF B cells are likely transitioning toward an antibody-secreting effector state. DUSP1 suggests these cells are responding to environmental stressors

EF macrophages appear highly active and focused on tissue interaction and signaling. upregulation of FCGR3A (CD16) and FCGR2A suggests these macrophages are primed for antibody-dependent cellular cytotoxicity (ADCC) or phagocytosis of immune complexes. Interestingly CD163 is upreg in EF sites.

The EF plasma cells seem to be in a state of high protein synthesis but also potentially under regulatory control.SOCS3, a potent inhibitor of JAK/STAT signaling is upregulated. This might indicate a negative feedback loop to limit over-activation in the EF space or a response to specific local cytokines (like IL-6).

Motivering
Justification
The Master's thesis titled "Nano-Pick: A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression
 
Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer" submitted by Zhihan Liang addresses the
 
development of a novel methodology applied to ovarian cancer samples to study cellular diversity in
 
tertiary lymphoid structures (TLSs). Throughout the thesis, the student demonstrates a deep

novel methodology applied to ovarian cancer samples to study cellular diversity in
 
tertiary lymphoid structures (TLSs). Throughout the thesis, the student demonstrates a deep
 
understanding of the existing literature and a remarkable ability to apply and combine different
 
methodological protocols in a timely and relevant manner.
The novel technique introduced in this thesis allows precise microtissue extraction and spatial gene
 
expression profiling. This method is particularly notable for its application to formalin-fixed paraffin-
embedded (FFPE) samples, overcoming common challenges with innovative tissue cyclic
 
immunofluorescence (t-CycIF) and optimized immunostaining techniques.
The study design is well-structured, and the methods used are appropriate for addressing the research
 
aims proposed. Although it is clear that the project will continue, the student is aware of the current
 
limitations and clearly outlines the next steps.
In conclusion, Zhihan Liang's thesis represents a valuable contribution to the field of translational
 
medicine. The work demonstrates not only a solid theoretical understanding but also advanced technical
 
and analytical skills. I recommend that it be awarded the maximum grade (5), recognizing the effort and
 
quality of the work presented.

material, tools and equipment. The supervisor must allocate time for scientific supervision and provide 
feedback and advice on academic writing. If for any reason the supervisor in unable to carry out his or 
her supervisory duties and this to a significant extent extends the agreed schedule, TRANSMED director 
must be informed. 
The student is committed to working with the supervisor and completing the work within the agreed 
timetable. Responsibility for the progress of the work rests primarily with the student .

vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Thesis Plan Abstract.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
Nano -Pick: A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of 
Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer 
 
Thesis Plan Abstract 
Ovarian cancer ranks as one of the deadliest forms of gynecological malignancies, 
affecting over 300,000 women globally each year. High -grade serous carcinoma 
(HGSC), the most prevalent and aggressive subtype of ovarian cancer, typically 
presents with a poor prognosis. Due to the absence of distinct symptoms in the early 
stages and the high dissemination rate of HGSC, 75% of HGSC patients are 
diagnosed at stage III or IV according to the FIGO staging system . Despite extensive 
efforts, optimal cytoreduction alone with various combinations of chemotherapy 
cannot significantly improve the survival rate of patients with advanced HGSC . 
Recently, although advancements have been made in targeted therapies for ovarian 
cancer, including the use of PARP inhibitors and bevacizumab, immunotherapies have 
not achieved the same level of success in treating this disease. This can be attributed 
to the high heterogeneity and unique immunosuppressive tumor microenvironment 
(TME) of ovarian cancer . The complexity of immune cell interactions and regulatory 
immune networks among different cell types, including T cells, B cells, macrophages, 
dendritic cells, and myeloid -derived suppressor cells (MDSCs), underscores the

vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Thesis Plan Abstract.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
regulatory 
immune networks among different cell types, including T cells, B cells, macrophages, 
dendritic cells, and myeloid -derived suppressor cells (MDSCs), underscores the 
complexity of the immune landscape in cancer. Further exploration and understanding 
of components of the TME could illuminate mechanisms by which tumors evade 
immune surveillance and exploit immune checkpoints to suppress effective anti -tumor 
immunity . 
Tertiary lymphoid structure (TLS) is a spontaneously formed, lymph node -like 
aggregate of diverse immune cells within non -lymphoid tissues. The formation of 
TLS in tumor area is initiated by a cascade of inflammatory signals within the TME , 
including cytokines (like TNF -α, IL-6) and chemokines (such as CCL19, CCL21, 
CXCL13) . As TLS matures, there are similar compartmentalized structures with 
lymph nodes containing B -cell zones featuring germinal centers (GC), T -cell zones, 
and high endothelial venules (HEVs). Tertiary lymphoid structures (TLS) have been 
shown to significantly contribute to tumor adaptive immunity by enhancing T cell 
activation and assisting in antigen presentation . However, although research indicates 
that patients rich in TLS will experience a better prognosis, some studies have also 
highlighted the potential role of TLS in the immunosuppressive process facilitating

vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Thesis Plan Abstract.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
dicates 
that patients rich in TLS will experience a better prognosis, some studies have also 
highlighted the potential role of TLS in the immunosuppressive process facilitating 
the escape of cancer cells from immune surveillance . Considering the double -edged 
sword role of TLS in anti -tumor immunity, the analysis of spatially resolved gene

vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Thesis Plan Abstract.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
expression in both naive and mature TLS areas using clinical samples holds 
significant potential and value. 
In this study, I plan to develop a novel analysis methodology by integrating 
microtissue region extraction technology (Rarecyte) with the nCounter® gene panel 
analysis system (Nanostring) to achieve spatially resolved gene expression profiling 
of TLS regions in clinical ovarian cancer FFPE samples. To achieve this , I need to 
first verify whether a series of antibody products can bind to target antigens and have 
stable fluorescence signal expression without heat -induced antigen retrieval methods 
(HIER). Besides, I also need to test and find optimal parameters to implement a 
feasible microtissue extraction method for FFPE sections of ovarian cancer tissue. 
Based on these experiments, a protocol can be established to achieve stable and 
reliable tissue digestion and micro -mRNA extraction with test sample slides . Since 
only about 100 -200 cells are deposited in each tube during the microtissue extraction 
process, the amount of mRNA extracted is very small, and qPCR is required to verify 
the required number of amplification cycles. Before real picking experiments, t issue -
based cyclic immunofluorescence (t -CycIF) will be perfor med on the adjacent slides 
to help with the selection of ready -to-pick microtissue regions. In the final phase,

vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Thesis Plan Abstract.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
ssue -
based cyclic immunofluorescence (t -CycIF) will be perfor med on the adjacent slides 
to help with the selection of ready -to-pick microtissue regions. In the final phase, 
samples that meet the quality control standards will undergo analysis using the 
nCounter gene expression panel to discover spatially resolved gene expression 
patterns.

Deckblatt mit tCycIF Image

Ovarian Cancer

8th most common cancer for women
HGSC most lethal subtype
Limited treatment options
 🡪 primary/interval debulking surgery
 🡪 (neo)adjuvant chemotherapy
 🡪 PARP inhibitors
Low response rates for immune checkpoint inhibitors

‹#›

Speaker notes:
Ovarian Cancer – why is it relevant?

Tumor Microenvironment

promotes
 Tumor survival
 Tumor development
 Metastasis
 Immune evasion

🡪 interesting therapeutic target

‹#›

Speaker notes:
for an integrated spatial and proteomic exploration of the TME

Tumor microenvironment 
- heterologous environment 
plays a vital role within tumor survival, development, metastasis, immune evasion
And is therefore an interesting therapeutic target
Exploration necessary, since understanding mechanisms and interactions of tumor with its surrounding cells and how tumor recruits those cells could help to find new treatment approaches

That’s why:
Spatial resolution methods are crucial in tumor research as they enable the precise visualization and analysis of the tumor microenvironment (TME), revealing the spatial organization of tumor cells, immune cells, stromal components, and extracellular factors. These techniques help uncover key interactions driving tumor progression, metastasis, and immune evasion, ultimately guiding the development of targeted therapies and personalized treatment strategies.

Spatial-resolution Methods

Mass cytometry/spectrometry-based imaging methods 🡪 high multiplexing, expensive equipment, complex, time consuming

Fluorescent-based imaging methods 🡪limited multiplexing, cheap, AB-panel based, fast

‹#›

Speaker notes:
1. Mass Cytometry/Spectrometry-Based Imaging
These include imaging mass cytometry (IMC), multiplexed ion beam imaging (MIBI), and related mass spectrometry-based imaging methods.
2. Fluorescent Staining-Based Imaging
These include standard immunofluorescence (IF), confocal microscopy, STED, and multiplexed fluorescence imaging (e.g., CODEX, IBEX, CyCIF).
 🡪 method is lacking for dissection of specific regions of interest and getting spatial and full proteomics insight of small regions in an affordable, customizable, simple way

Problem: 
no method that provides spatial & FULL proteomic insight for small regions
🡪 simple, affordable & customizable

Why is it needed?

Tumor microenvironment (TME) 🡪 pro-tumor environment 🡪 therapeutic target
Spatial technologies – spatial organization

‹#›

Resources of the Lab

Collection of >500 clinical ovarian cancer samples 
Highly multiplexed tissue-based Cyclic ImmunoFluorescence (tCycIF) imaging
Rarecyte CyteFinder II imaging instrument
Established computational infrastructure & image processing pipeline

‹#›

Speaker notes:
Collection of clinical ovarian cancer samples which are collected as part of the ONCOSYS-OVA at the Helsinki Hospital 🡪 formalin fixed, paraffin embedded tissue samples
tCycIF: immuno staining + DNA dye, imaging, fluorophore bleaching
Rarecyte CyteFinder II = high-speed, whole slide imaging systems with options for multiplexed liquid biopsy and tissue spatial analysis
Computational infrastructure & imaging pipeline for image processing (illumination correction, stitching, segmentation, quanitification for single cell data, phenotyping)

ProteoPicking

combination of t-CycIF and MS-based proteomics analysis
dissection of regions of interest
structural, functional and proteomic landscape
high spatial precision

‹#›

Speaker notes:
The mass spectrometry analysis of the picked cell samples was performed by the Turku Proteomics facility, a unit of the Turku Bioscience Centre, University of Turku.The samples were analysed using a bottom-up approach, following the facility’s established protocols. The used method was data independent acquisition shot gun proteomics via liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS).

Overview ofGeoMxAssayswithProCodeIndices
KeyConsiderations fortheImmuno- oncology Proteome Atlas
(IPA)withProCodeIndices
TheImmuno- oncology Proteome Atlas(IPA) covers 570+ protein targets across dozens ofpathways
andisthefirstGeoMx assay touseProCode i5andi7indices (sequences important inNext- Generation
Sequencing (NGS)). ProCode indices are2nucleotides longer thanSeqCode indices, which areused in
allother GeoMx NGS assays. Formore information oni5andi7indices, please refer totheGeoMx
DSP NGS Readout User Manual (MAN- 10153) .
Whenrunning aProCodeassaysuchastheImmuno- oncology Proteome Atlas,pleasepay
attention toProCode-specific instructions throughout theusermanuals, summarized here:
lGeoMx DSP software v3.1 orlater isrequired torunProCode assays such asIPA.
lTheIPAprobe mixispackaged astwotubes: core andmodule. Theconcentration ofthemodule is
different than theProtein- NGS assays with Seq Code indices, sotheAntibody Working Solution is
prepared differently. See details intheGeoMx DSP Manual Slide Preparation User Manual (MAN-
10150) orAutomated Slide Preparation User Manual (MAN- 10151) .
lGenerally, assays with ProCode indices such asIPAcannot becombined inthesame plate orreadout
group asassays with SeqCode indices. Compatibility rules aredetailed intheGeoMx DSP Instrument

y, assays with ProCode indices such asIPAcannot becombined inthesame plate orreadout
group asassays with SeqCode indices. Compatibility rules aredetailed intheGeoMx DSP Instrument
User Manual (MAN- 10152) .Anexception istheSpatial Proteogenomics Assay inwhich RNA and
Protein arecollected from thesame slide; refer totheGeoMx DSP Spatial Proteogenomics Assay User
Manual (MAN- 10158) .
lInNGS library preparation, tags with ProCode indices such asIPAarePCR- amplified with ProCode
primer plates, rather than Seq Code primer plates. Less DSP aspirate isrequired inthePCR
amplification step forProCode assays compared toSeqCode assays (2µLvs4µL).Seedetails inthe
GeoMx DSP NGS Readout User Manual (MAN- 10153) .Note that2Pro Code primer plates areavailable
(YandZ),allowing multiplexing ofupto192wells.
lProCode assays cangenerally besequenced tolower depth than SeqCode assays. Seedetails inthe
GeoMx DSP NGS Readout User Manual (MAN- 10153) .
lAtthistime, Spatial Proteogenomic Assays thatinclude IPAcanonly beprocessed onthestandalone
GeoMx NGS Pipeline software version 3.1, notontheGeoMx NGS Pipeline onDRAGEN via
BaseSpace Sequencing Hub. MacOS isnotsupported. Obtain GeoMx NGS Pipeline software version
3.1and installation instructions athttps://nanostring.box.com/v/GeoMxNGSPipeline .Refer tothe
GeoMx DSP Spatial Proteogenomics Assay User Manual (MAN- 10158).

ine software version
3.1and installation instructions athttps://nanostring.box.com/v/GeoMxNGSPipeline .Refer tothe

Conventions
The following conventions areused intheGeoMx DSP user manuals and aredescribed foryour
reference.
Bold textistypically used tohighlight aspecific button, keystroke, ormenu option. Itmay alsobeused to
highlight important textorterms.
Blueunderlined text istypically used tohighlight links and/or references toother sections ofthe
manual. Itmay alsobeused tohighlight references toother manuals orinstructional material.
Agray boxindicates general information thatmay beuseful forimproving assay performance. These notes aim
toclarify other instructions orprovide guidance toimprove theefficiency oftheassay workflow.
IMPORTANT:
 This symbol indicates important information thatiscritical toensuring asuccessful assay.
Following these instructions may help improve thequality ofyour data.
WARNING:
 This symbol indicates thepotential forbodily injury ordamage totheinstrument ifthe

GeoMxDSPWorkflow
InGeoMx Digital Spatial Profiling (DSP), antibody ornucleic acid probes arecoupled tophotocleavable
oligonucleotide tags. After probes hybridize totargets inslide- mounted tissue sections, the
oligonucleotide tags arereleased from discrete regions ofthetissue viaUVexposure. Released tags are
quantified bynCounter technology orIllumina Next Generation Sequencing (NGS). Counts aremapped
back totissue location, yielding aspatially resolved digital profile ofanalyte abundance (Figure 1).
lDay1:SlideStaining. Prepare slides and incubate biological targets with UV-cleavable probes.
Prepare manually orusing the BOND RX/RXmFully Automated IHC/ISH Stainer from Leica
Biosystems®.
lDay2:Process SlidesonGeoMxDSP. Load prepared slides intotheGeoMx DSP instrument. Slides
arescanned tocapture fluorescent images used toselect regions ofinterest (ROIs). ROIs may be
segmented intodiscrete compartments orareas ofillumination (AOI). Theinstrument collects UV-
cleaved oligos from theAOIs intothewells ofacollection plate.
ForNGSreadout:
Day3:Transfer thecollected aspirates toaPCR plate and
perform LibraryPrep with SeqCode orProCode primers.
Pool andpurify theproducts, thenSequence onanIllumina
NGS instrument.
Day4:Process FASTQ sequencing filesintodigital count

and
perform LibraryPrep with SeqCode orProCode primers.
Pool andpurify theproducts, thenSequence onanIllumina
NGS instrument.
Day4:Process FASTQ sequencing filesintodigital count
conversion (DCC) filesusing theGeoMxNGSPipeline on
standalone software orIllumina's DRAGEN™ platform
accessed viaBaseSpace™ Sequence Hub(cloud) or
NextSeq 1000/2000 (local).FornCounter readout:
Day2,continued :Transfer the
collected aspirates toahybridization
plate along with GeoMx HybCode
reagents. Hybridization occurs
overnight.
Day3:Pool wells andProcess on
annCounter ProorMAX/FLEX
Analysis System or
SPRINT Profiler togenerate
reporter count conversion (RCC)
files.
lDay4or5:Upload counts toGeoMx DSP andcreateastudy intheData Analysis Suite. Perform
quality- control checks anddata analysis, andgenerate analysis plots.

Study Summary
The NGS study summary (Figure 18) plots the
total number of reads for each segment by

It also contains dropdown fields with Segment
Summary (Figure19),Probe Summary , and

Figure 18: NGS Study SummarySegment summary includes Q30 values. Q30
refers to the percentage of bases that get a Q
score of at least 30 (99.9% accuracy). Q score is
the most common metric to assess the accuracy
of a sequencing platform (refer to Understanding Illumina Quality Scores Technical Note for more
details).
The terms umiQ30 andrtsQ30 refer to the
percentage of bases with Q≥30 specifically in the
UMI and RTS_ ID portions of the reads,
respectively.
Figure 19: Segment Summary
FOR RESEARCH USE ONLY. Not for use in diagnostic procedures. 23

Data QC
Data QC allows you to specify parameters for evaluating the quality of your data and to qualify segments
by number of cells or by area. There are two steps in the QC process: Segment QC andBiological
probe QC. Segments outside of desirable quality criteria are tagged with QC failure tags. Find more
details on QC calculations in AlgorithmDetails onpage 101.
When protein and RNA are collected from the same slide in one collection (Spatial Proteogenomic assay), the
corresponding data analysis study includes protein and RNA data. Quality control and subsequent data analysis
steps are performed for each analyte separately, and datasets are distinguished by a unique symbol. If a protein
scan and an RNA scan are collected from separately, the readouts cannot be combined into one data analysis
study. (The study can be created, but analysis steps are blocked because the software does not see any probes
in common between the two probe kits.) Data can be exported for analysis with other software packages, such
asGeomxTools.
Segment QC
In the QC process, some basic metrics collected from the run
are compared to an expected range of values to determine the
overall quality of the data.
Select the QCbutton. Review the QC parameters and default
values selected for your study (Figure20). You may change the
selections and default values as appropriate based on the

.
Select the QCbutton. Review the QC parameters and default
values selected for your study (Figure20). You may change the
selections and default values as appropriate based on the
parameters of your study. Then, select Run QC. The software
flags segments not meeting these QC parameters.
Flagged segments are not removed automatically, but are
tagged for reference in subsequent data analysis steps.
Technical Signal QC
Technical signal QC assesses the quality of sequencing for
each segment.
Figure 20: QC parameters windowTheraw read threshold is the lowest number of raw reads allowed
for a segment.
Thepercent aligned reads sets the minimum (as a percentage) of
raw reads that align to target sequences.
Sequencing saturation sets the minimum percent of sequencing saturation allowed. The percent of
sequencing saturation is calculated as: 1 - (deduplicated reads/aligned reads) x 100. 100% sequencing
saturation indicates a representative sample, while 0% sequencing saturation indicates that all reads
were unique. Values below 50% may need to be resequenced.

Technical Background QC
Thetechnical background QCisaGeoMx DSP run control.
TheNegative probecountgeomean establishes thelevel oftechnical noise expected and flags
segments with signal below thatlevel. Thenegative probe count geomean willbecalculated separately
foreach probe kit.
TheNegative Probe count geomean isnotrelevant forProtein NGS andshould beun-checked.
TheNo-Template Control Count establishes thelevel atwhich counts intheNo-Template Control
(NTC) willbeflagged. TheNTC isused todetect contamination inthelibrary prep. With software v3.1,
users have theoption toexclude theNTC when setting upascan (refer totheGeoMx DSP Instrument
User Manual (MAN- 10152) .Uncheck theboxforNTC Count inTechnical Background QCifthescans in
thepresent study were collected without anNTC.
Minimum NucleiandSurface AreaCount
TheGeoMx chemistry isdesigned foranoptimal range ofnuclei andsurface areas. Thedefault value of
each ofthese QCparameters willautomatically fillwith theminimum recommended value forthe
GeoMx product used inthepresent run. Note, however, that notallslides willhave nuclei count,
particularly ifnoDNA channel was selected. These may besafely deselected ifranges orconsideration
arenotappropriate fortheparameters ofyour study.

Calculate LOQ
Thelimit ofquantitation (LOQ) issettobethenegative probe geomean multiplied bythegeometric
standard deviations ofthenegative probes. Youmay setLOQ tothevalue ofyour choice.
LOQ=GeoMean (Neg1-n)*GeoStdev (Neg1-n)LOQThreshold
Thisisaconfidence threshold rather than adetection threshold. Avalue below LOQ does notnecessarily
mean thatatarget isnotexpressed, butavalue above LOQ gives confidence thatitisexpressed. 2.0is
thedefault (and recommended) setting forhigh- confidence detection. Ifanexperiment included more
than oneprobe kit,theLOQ willbecalculated separately foreach probe kit.

FOVRegistration QC
This metric reports thepercentage offields ofview (FOVs) theDigital Analyzer orSPRINT instrument
captured. Atleast 75% ofFOVs should besuccessfully counted toobtain robust data. Afailure inthis
area may indicate atilted orsmeared cartridge, which canberemedied byrescanning (ProorMAX/FLEX
systems only; ideally within oneweek). However, consistently lowpercentages canindicate anissue
with theinstrumentation. Wells with consistently lowpercentages should notbeincluded infurther
analysis. Totroubleshoot FOV Registration QCresults:
oCheck alignment ofthecartridge intheinstrument –ifcrooked, reload andrescan.
oClean thebottom ofthecartridge with70%EtOH andawipe, reload, andrescan.
oRefer totheappropriate nCounter instrument user manual foradditional information.
Binding Density QC
Thismetric isameasurement (spots persquare micron) oftheconcentration ofbarcodes detected bythe
nCounter instrument. Theinstrument may notbeable todistinguish each probe from theothers ifthe
density istoohigh. Generally, asegment's binding density isproportional toitsquantity ofhybridized
probe tags. Very high binding density canresult indecreased linearity incounts andloss ofsensitivity.
Low binding density canresult inalossofdetectable signal from expressed targets. Thedefault Binding
Density ranges are:
onCounter ProorMAX/FLEX system: 0.1-2.25 spots persquare micron.

inding density canresult inalossofdetectable signal from expressed targets. Thedefault Binding
Density ranges are:
onCounter ProorMAX/FLEX system: 0.1-2.25 spots persquare micron.
onCounter SPRINT system: 0.1-1.8spots persquare micron.
Measurements outside ofthese ideal ranges may result inaBinding Density QCfailure, butshould be
checked toseehow much they deviate from theideal range. Large increased deviations canresult in
reduced FOV registration andmay represent asaturated cartridge. Large decreased deviations may
represent afailed lane. Wells exhibiting either type oflarge Binding Density deviation should bediscarded
from analysis. Some deviation may stillresult ingood quality data, butthismust beevaluated onacase-
by-case basis. Introubleshooting Binding Density QCresults youmay:
oCheck theinput amount. More sample input willresult inincreased Binding Density.
oConsider theexpression level ofthetargets. Ifthetargets arehighly expressed, Binding Density willbe
high because more molecules aretargeted inyour samples.
oConsider thesizeofthepanel. Ifapanel contains probes formore targets, then Binding Density will
usually behigher.

Positive Control Normalization QC
TheGeoMx HybCode Pack contains onespike- inpositive hybridization (pos- hyb) control probe perwell
forevaluating thequality ofthenCounter readout step. APositive Control Normalization factor is
calculated using these pos-hybcontrols. This normalization adjusts forvariations thatexist across lanes,
cartridges, and days and account fordifferences inuser technique, hybridization, magnetic bead
purification, complex- to-slide binding, andimaging. Robust expression ofthepos-hybcontrol probe is
expected.
Thedefault range ofacceptable values is0.3-3.0,butvalues outside thisrange may bedetermined tobe
acceptable based onempirical data. Very lowcounts forthepos-hybprobe incombination with alarge
Binding Density deviation may indicate issues with thehybridization step. Inaddition, large variations in
ROIorsegment sizewillcontribute toanincreased Positive Control Normalization range ofvalues; inthis
case, care should betaken toensure thepositive hybridization controls areabove 100counts before
positive control normalization.
AspartoftheQCprocess, data willbenormalized tothepositive controls intherun;thepositive controls
willberemoved from thedataset after thatpoint. Before running QC,itisrecommended thatyouview

eQCprocess, data willbenormalized tothepositive controls intherun;thepositive controls
willberemoved from thedataset after thatpoint. Before running QC,itisrecommended thatyouview
thepositive control results intheinitial dataset toensure thatpositive control counts areasexpected.
Minimum NucleiandSurface AreaCount
TheGeoMx chemistry isdesigned foranoptimal range ofnuclei andsurface areas. Thedefault value of
each ofthese QCparameters willautomatically fillwith theminimum recommended value forthe
GeoMx product used inthepresent run. Note, however, that notallslides willhave nuclei count,
particularly ifnoDNA channel was selected. These parameters may besafely deselected ifranges or
consideration arenotappropriate fortheparameters ofyour study.

Q3normalization rescales thegene expression data such that allsegments have similar gene expression
ranges. Itreduces variance from segment size, segment cellularity, andother technical factors. Underlying the
Q3method isanassumption that, despite biological variation, segments should have similar gene expression
count distributions. Thus, Q3isonly appropriate when aprobe panel islarge anddiverse, such asonethat
targets thewhole transcriptome. When probe panels aresmall andtargeted, orwhen segments aresmall and
only asmall fraction ofaprobe panel isdetected inthose segments, Q3istypically notanappropriate
normalization method. Additionally, Q3isperformed only with regard forassay signal, notassay background,
and canbecome biased ifsignal isnotadequately above background. This issue commonly arises when
segments aresmall.

Astudy with tissue microarray slides and1ROIperpatient should generally usethet-test. Astudy with tissue
sections andmultiple ROIs perpatient should generally usethelinear mixed model test.
lT-test(unpaired) :The unpaired t-test should beselected when comparing themeans oftwo
groups from independent observations. Forexample, anunpaired t-test would beused totest
whether theexpression ofaprobe inthetumor microenvironment (TME) issignificantly different
between tissue specimens originating from tumors which responded totreatment vs.tissue
specimens originating from tumors which didnotrespond totreatment. T-tests assume anormal
distribution.
Under thenullhypothesis, there isnotasignificant difference inthemean target probe expression
between thetwogroups.
lT-test(paired) :The paired t-test should beselected tocompare means between paired
observations, such asbetween segments originating from thesame ROI. Forexample, apaired t-
test would beused incomparing theexpression ofaprobe target intheTME before andafter
treatment formatched tissue samples. T-tests assume anormal distribution.
Under thenullhypothesis, there isnotasignificant difference inthemean target probe expression
between thetwogroups.
lMann-Whitney test:TheMann- Whitney U-testisanon-parametric testofthenullhypothesis. It

there isnotasignificant difference inthemean target probe expression
between thetwogroups.
lMann-Whitney test:TheMann- Whitney U-testisanon-parametric testofthenullhypothesis. It
does notrequire theassumption ofnormal distribution.
Under thenullhypothesis, thedistributions ofboth populations areequal.
Under thealternative hypothesis, thedistributions ofthepopulations arenotequal; arandomly
selected observation from onegroup willbegreater orsmaller than arandomly selected observation
from thesecond group. Thetestreturns theprobability thatthemedian expression ofatarget probe
inonegroup isgreater than theother.
lLinearMixedModel :Linear Mixed Models (LMMs) aredesigned tohandle data with repeated
measurements from thesame sampling unit. Forexample, inastudy where 6ROIs arecollected
from each sample, theROIs from anyonesample willallbeinfluenced bytheidiosyncratic biology of
thatsample, sharing atendency forhigher expression ofsome genes/proteins andlower expression
ofothers. LMMs explicitly model thistendency.
Specify atleast onerandom effect toadjust for;thedefault isScan_ ID.Choose random slope when
your fixed effect factor hasmultiple levels within each ofoneofyour random effect factors.
FORRESEARCH USEONLY.Notforuseindiagnostic procedures. 49

Asofsoftware v2.5.1, theLMM lowest level random effect isnolonger arequirement, asitwas inearlier
software versions. This change does notaffect theformula forLMM, since earlier software versions
automatically dropped thelowest level random effect.
Incontrast toLMM, thet-testisoften dramatically wrong indata with repeated measurements. Bytreating
multiple ROIs from thesame sample asindependent observations, thet-testinappropriately inflates the
sample sizeofthestudy. Forexample, given 100ROIs from onetreated andonecontrol sample, thet-test
willactasifthere are100independent samples from thetreated andcontrol groups, while aLMM will
correctly acknowledge thatthere isjustoneindependent sample from each group, with many repeated
measurements.
Unlike random intercept onlymodel, arandom slope model allows each group linetohave adifferent slope
andthatmeans thattherandom slope model allows theexplanatory variable tohave adifferent effect for
each group. Forexample, when each group hasdifferent ROI types andtheir effect isofinterest, the
variance ofgroup- specific ROItype effect needs tobeestimated viatherandom slope model toproperly
evaluate theglobal ROItype effect.
6.Select theMultiple testcorrection desired. Themethod chosen willbeused toadjust thep-values of
individual genes. Agene’s False Discovery Rate (FDR) reflects theproportion ofsignificant genes that

le testcorrection desired. Themethod chosen willbeused toadjust thep-values of
individual genes. Agene’s False Discovery Rate (FDR) reflects theproportion ofsignificant genes that
areexpected tobefalse discoveries. FDR canbeused asamore conservative and informative
alternative top-values.
lNone :thisselection isanoption toforgo calculating FDR.
lPermutation q-Value :thismethod requires alarge number oftests andmay notbethemost
effective method forGeoMx DSP analysis.
lBH:theBenjamini- Hochberg procedure isastandard method incontrolling FDR and isthe
recommended method forGeoMx DSPprotein analysis .Itassumes that thetests are
independent forasubset ofgenes/proteins, which usually holds forgene/protein expression data.
lBY:theBenjamini- Yekutieli procedure isanadjustment toBH.Itcontrols FDR inamore general
setting, butposes amore strict threshold thatleads tofewer significant calls. With theBYmethod,
it’svery unlikely toidentify anysignificantly differentially expressed genes inDSP analysis.

Workflows combine multiple data analysis steps intoasingle streamlined operation. Tenworkflows are
pre-loaded into theGeoMx DSP Data Analysis Suite. Their parameters canbecustomized toyour
specifications. Atthistime, itisnotpossible tocreate anew workflow from scratch.

normalization andcluster analysis onallremaining targets after QCandfiltering. This workflow also
produces anegative normalized dataset forcomparison.
lRNANGSWorkflow 2:Statistical testandpathway analysis. This workflow requires annotations and
modification ofthestatistical testparameters tocomplete fully. This workflow performs astatistical
testandpathway analysis onaQ3normalized dataset after QCandfiltering steps.

segment QCwith modified parameters andremoves failed segments during biological probe QC.Then
amodified filtering step keeps targets aslong asthey areabove LOQ inmore than 1%ofsegments.

remaining after themodified filtering.

Study Summary (NGS only)
For NGS readout: Study Summary is only available for studies with NGS readout.
The study summary (Figure 74)plots the total number of reads for each segment by each processing
step outcome (trimmed, deduplicated, etc.). It also contains dropdown fields with Segment Summary
(Figure 75),Probe Summary , andTarget Summary .
Figure 74: NGS Study Summary
Segment summary includes Q30 values. Q30 refers to the percentage of bases that get a Q score of at
least 30 (99.9% accuracy). Q score is the most common metric to assess the accuracy of a sequencing
platform (refer to UnderstandingIllumina Quality Scores Technical Note for more details).
The terms umiQ30 andrtsQ30 refer to the % of bases with Q≥30 specifically in the UMI and RTS_ID
portions of the reads, respectively.

differences between subsets ofanexperiment without making anyassumptions about theunderlying
statistical distribution; they arenon-parametric.

Principal Component Analysis (PCA) Plot
The PCA plot (Figure 91)depicts the first three principal components for the selected dataset along the x-
axis, y-axis, and z-axis of a three-dimensional plot.
Figure 91: PCA plotPCA is a dimensionality reduction technique for
visualizing variation within a dataset. Users should
view PCA when they want to get a sense of how
similar a segment is to the others. This technique
can be used to identify clusters of similar
segments. Each principal component (PC1- 3,
plotted as x,y,z axes by default) explains a certain
percentage of the variation in the data (PC1
explains the most variation, PC2 second, etc). It’s
also possible using PCA data to determine which
targets contribute to each PC, that is, which are
responsible for variation between segments. PCA
input data is scaled before analysis.
lClick on the plot and drag to rotate along the x, y,
or z axis to view the plot in different axes‘
perspectives.

viewer.

Algorithm Details
Algorithm Details
Bargraph: Bargraphs aredisplayed aslinear orlog2transformed values. When grouping: Median. Error
bars areeither theStandard deviation ofvalues inagroup, orstandard error.
Boxplots: Box plots depict subsets oftheexperiment through their quartiles. Box plots have lines
extending vertically from theboxes (whiskers) that indicate variability outside theupper and lower
quartiles. Outliers may beplotted asindividual points. These visualizations display differences between
subsets ofanexperiment without making anyassumptions about theunderlying statistical distribution;
they arenon-parametric.
Cluster: The cluster tool displays anunsupervised hierarchical clustering where theselected data
represents log-transformed and z-scored values. Clustering isperformed based oncorrelation to
determine theposition intheclustering dendrogram. Thecluster heatmap willploteach segment- probe
cellaccording todetermined position using color torepresent z-scores. When exporting data from the
visualization pane, youwillbeexporting onlythevalues yousee, which arez-scores.
Forestplots: The forest plot shows thedistribution ofratio values forindividual probes across all
segments orgroups ofsegments. Forest plots canonly begenerated from aratio dataset. Fold changes

e forest plot shows thedistribution ofratio values forindividual probes across all
segments orgroups ofsegments. Forest plots canonly begenerated from aratio dataset. Fold changes

95% confidence limits.

software v3.1 uses Reactome Database Build 78+ NCBI_ 08122021.

ForNGSreadout:
Biological ProbeQC
Grubbs testlooks foroutlier
probes, loworhigh, within a
target andROI. Probes that
areoutliers inaproportion of
ROIs above theuser- set
threshold areremoved from
allROIs andconsidered
"global" outliers. Probes that
areoutliers inaproportion of
ROIs below theuser- set
threshold areremoved only
from those ROIs inwhich
they areanoutlier andare
considered "local" outliers.
Thelimit ofquantitation
(LOQ) foreach AOIis
calculated as{user- defined
value} geometric standard
deviations above the
geometric mean ofthe
negative probes. Thisis
calculated after the
exclusion ofoutlier probes.FornCounter readout:
FOVRegistration QC
Each individual lanescanned onannCounter system isdivided intoafew
hundred imaging sections, called Fields ofView (FOV). Thesystem images
these FOVs separately, andsums thebarcode counts (representing the
GeoMx DSP probe counts) ofallFOVs. Finally, thesystem reports thenumber
ofFOVs successfully imaged asFOV Counted. Significant discrepancy
between thenumber ofFOV forwhich imaging wasattempted (FOV Count)
andforwhich imaging wassuccessful (FOV Counted) may indicate anissue
with imaging performance. Recommended percentage ofregistered FOVs
(i.e., FOV Counted over FOV Count) is75%. Lanes willbeflagged ifthis
percentage islower.
Binding Density QC

ssue
with imaging performance. Recommended percentage ofregistered FOVs
(i.e., FOV Counted over FOV Count) is75%. Lanes willbeflagged ifthis
percentage islower.
Binding Density QC
Theimaging unitonlycounts thebarcodes thatareunambiguously
distinguishable. Itdoes notcount barcodes thatoverlap within animage. Too
many overlapping codes intheimage, however, willresult inimage saturation
inwhich significant data losscould occur. Todetermine thelevel ofimage
saturation, thenCounter instrument calculates thenumber ofoptical features
persquare micron foreach laneasitprocesses theimages. Thisiscalled the
Binding Density. TheBinding Density isuseful fordetermining whether data
collection hasbeen compromised duetoimage saturation. Lanes willbe
flagged when Binding Density value isnotwithin therange of0.1to1.8(for
nCounter SPRINT Profiler) or2.25 (nCounter Pro/MAX/FLEX).
Positive controlnormalization QC
TheGeoMx HybCodes contain synthetic ERCC spike- ins.Counts forthese
spike- insmay beused tocorrect forplatform- associated sources oftechnical
variation (e.g., automated purification, hybridization conditions, etc.).
Normalization iscarried outusing ascaling approach. Thegeometric mean
count values ofthespike- insforeach segment aremultiplied with ascaling or
normalization factor tobring them tothereference value which issettothe
arithmetic mean ofthese geometric mean values.

ofthespike- insforeach segment aremultiplied with ascaling or
normalization factor tobring them tothereference value which issettothe
arithmetic mean ofthese geometric mean values. Lanes willbeflagged when
thisPositive ERCC normalization factor isoutside oftherange 0.3to3.

AICDA (Activation-induced cytidine deaminase) is essential for:

- Somatic Hypermutation (SHM): Introduces point mutations into the
 variable regions of immunoglobulin (Ig) genes, increasing antibody
 diversity.

- Class Switch Recombination (CSR): Facilitates the switching of
 antibody isotypes (e.g., IgM to IgG, IgA, or IgE), allowing
 antibodies to mediate different immune functions.

It acts by deaminating cytosine bases in DNA to uracil, which is
recognized and processed by DNA repair pathways, leading to targeted
mutations or recombination events.

AICDA is primarily expressed in germinal center B cells during

l, which is
recognized and processed by DNA repair pathways, leading to targeted
mutations or recombination events.

AICDA is primarily expressed in germinal center B cells during
activation and proliferation. It can be found in Activated B cells in
secondary lymphoid organs (e.g., lymph nodes, spleen, tonsils) + TLSs.
AICDA is not expressed in naïve or memory B cells or other immune cell
types under normal conditions.

**This is the folder for the 7_TLS project.**

**Members:** Ada Junquera, Zhihan Liang, Hanna Elomaa

**1. Management & Planning**

The Gantt chart is located here. Other guidelines and project tasks can
be allocated here.

**2. Methods & Experiments**

Here we have protocols and other experimental files.

**3. Data & Figures**

In the data folder, we have Xenium data and segmentation test results.

Manuscript ideas and figures are assigned to the figures folder.

**4. Meetings & Updates**

Meeting notes, personal notes, and presentation slides are here.

**5. Writing & Dissemination**

Conference abstracts, TLS project manuscript plan, and all other files
related to paper writing can be found here.

**6. Archive**

We have master\'s thesis and other outdated files here.

## Atlas of TLS and TILs in ovarian cancer {#atlas-of-tls-and-tils-in-ovarian-cancer .unnumbered}

Descriptive introduction from our cohort where we explore the presence
and basic metrics of distinct cancer types.

### QUESTIONS: {#questions .unnumbered}

- How do TLS and TIL characteristics vary across different ovarian
 > cancer histologies (e.g., HGSC, LGSC, clear cell carcinoma,
 > carcinosarcoma)?

- How does the characterization and spatial organization of TLS and
 > TILs correlate with clinical outcomes, specifically
 > progression-free survival (PFS) and overall survival (OS), in
 > these cancer patients?

## Exploration of the TME's link to TLS/TIL appearance {#exploration-of-the-tmes-link-to-tlstil-appearance .unnumbered}

In this section we will funnel the information from the prior atlas to
something more concrete, specific diagnosis (HGSC/Endometrioid), tissue
type (Omentum), molecular profile (HRP) and distinct therapy sequences
(PDS/IDS). We want to explore the patterns within the TME correlating
with presence of these distinct aggregates, as well as profiling the
spatial interactions of these structures.

### QUESTIONS: {#questions-1 .unnumbered}

- Do TILs and TLS-containing samples differ spatially and
 > functionally? Why do samples display heterogeneity in TLS and GC
 > presence?

 - *Based on preliminary analysis;* Is the presence of GC a
 > prognostic marker, and if so which are the mechanisms that
 > promote or impair their development

- Are TIL and TLS presence linked to specific microenvironmental
 > features or structural patterns? How might these patterns
 > influence the clinical outcomes?

 > DeepCell library (Python 3.8.20). Preprocessing included
 > per-channel normalization, gamma correction, and unsharp
 > masking to enhance contrast and edge definition. Large images
 > were divided into overlapping tiles, processed individually,
 > and reassembled with label harmonization using a custom untile
 > function. To exclude background regions, a Gaussian Mixture
 > Model (GMM) was applied to the mean intensities of each tile,
 > and the lowest-intensity cluster was automatically classified
 > as background. Segmentation focused on nuclear compartments
 > using DAPI and optional membrane markers, with all predictions
 > run with 1mpp (recommended for images of 0.65um/pixel)

## H) GeoMx {#h-geomx .unnumbered}

### Geomx Experiment {#geomx-experiment .unnumbered}

The experiment was done in Feb and March 2025. 30 samples were shared
with the EyeMT project and finally 28 samples passed QC for GeoMx.

Protocols are saved here
[[GeoMx]{.underline}](https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1JtjMpnAPUM6-O3FQ39PEuolsvUWy7eFB?usp=sharing)

#### In a nutshell {#in-a-nutshell .unnumbered}

Formalin-fixed paraffin-embedded (FFPE) tissue sections (5 µm) were
sectioned adjacent to tCycIF slides. These sections are then processed
according to the manufacturer's instructions for the GeoMx Digital
Spatial Profiler (DSP; Bruker Spatial Biology, MAN-10150-06). Briefly,
slides were baked overnight, deparaffinized, and rehydrated before
target retrieval in 1× Tris-EDTA (pH 9.0) using a steamer at \~99 °C,
followed by Proteinase K digestion at 37 °C to expose RNA targets.
Sections were then postfixed and hybridized overnight with the GeoMx RNA
Probe Mix containing UV-cleavable oligonucleotide tags. After two
stringent washes, customized morphology markers and nuclear stains were
applied to enable ROI visualization.

Slides were scanned on the GeoMx DSP instrument to acquire whole slide

tringent washes, customized morphology markers and nuclear stains were
applied to enable ROI visualization.

Slides were scanned on the GeoMx DSP instrument to acquire whole slide
fluorescence morphology images, and regions of interest (ROIs) were
selected for illumination. Upon UV exposure, oligonucleotide tags were
released from each ROI and aspirated into a collection plate for library
preparation. Libraries were PCR-amplified using GeoMx Seq Code primers,
pooled, purified, QCed and sequenced on Novaseq X 10B 100c following the
GeoMx NGS Readout User Manual (MAN-10153). FASTQ files were processed
using the GeoMx NGS Pipeline to generate digital count conversion (DCC)
files.

#### ROI selection {#roi-selection .unnumbered}

ROIs from adjacent t-CyCIF images were pre-selected based on TLS immune
markers: CD20, CD21, CD4, CD8a, BCL6, Ki67, and Vimentin.

For mature TLS regions, three Regions of Interest (ROIs) were selected:
one located at the germinal center, one in the T-cell and B-cell regions
adjacent to the germinal center, and one in the stroma region near the
mature TLS. These three ROIs were collectively referred to as a group of
ROIs, each marked with a specific numerical label to distinguish it from
the others. This ROI selection method was adopted for each selected
mature or near-mature TLS region. All mature or near-mature TLS regions

numerical label to distinguish it from
the others. This ROI selection method was adopted for each selected
mature or near-mature TLS region. All mature or near-mature TLS regions
in the sample were selected as much as possible, and ROIs belonging to
near-mature TLS were further subdivided in subsequent data processing.

In addition, five immune aggregate regions and corresponding five
Regions of Interest (ROIs) were selected for each sample. These immune
aggregates may be located in the early stages of TLS development.

The selection of ROIs referenced pre-selected ROIs on adjacent tcycif
slices by loading a tcycif image containing the pre-selected ROIs and
overlaying it onto the actual scanned geomx image in the geomx system.
However, due to slight deformations in adjacent slices, the actual
selected ROIs and the pre-selected ROIs were not entirely identical. The
final selected ROIs must actually correspond to TLS regions or LAgg
regions in the Geomx image. However, due to the poor staining effect of
CD20 on geomx sections, this selection relied more heavily on the
results of CD45.

and
[[https://imagej.net/plugins/bdv/warpy/warpy-extension]{.underline}](https://imagej.net/plugins/bdv/warpy/warpy-extension).

A separate QuPath project and folder were created for each sample. The
corresponding tcycif and geomx images were imported into the QuPath
project for each sample, then aligned using Warpy in FIJI, generating a
new overlapping image. Finally, the geomx import, transfer, and tcycif
export of the ROI were completed. Detailed steps and scripts are
available on GitHub and can be found in the slides mentioned above.

### GeoMx data analysis for TLS {#geomx-data-analysis-for-tls .unnumbered}

#### Data processing {#data-processing .unnumbered}

Received raw sequencing data from the FIMM sequencing center. In the
GeoMx spatial transcriptomics analysis, I first loaded the dataset
processed by the Iga, which included quality control (LOQ and GDR
filtering), Q3 normalization, and Harmony batch effect correction. I
then performed sample filtering and metadata integration by removing
"Agg_additional" AOIs, standardizing TLS status labels, merging clinical
information to include HRD/HRP status, and adding manually updated TLS
annotation labels. The resulting geomx_data object was saved as a
harmonized dataset, serving as the standardized input for downstream
analyses such as differential expression and spatial pattern analysis.

##### 1. Data Loading and Structure Check {#data-loading-and-structure-check .unnumbered}

 > Harmony.

 - Harmony batch-corrected matrix (harmony_batch_corr_q3_norm)

 - sample metadata (pheno)

 > MS-down)** scores per AOI.

##### Signature Trajectories Across TLS Maturation {#signature-trajectories-across-tls-maturation .unnumbered}

- Quantified the distribution of **GC, TB, and Stromal signature

- Observation

 - Increasing GC signature enrichment from stromal toward mature
 > TLS states.

 - Decreasing stromal scores in parallel.

 - Intermediate enrichment of TB-related signals in transitional
 > stages (Agg and Immature_GC).

- This trend supports a **progressive maturation axis** of TLS
 > development from stromal-rich aggregates to GC-rich organized
 > structures.

#### New TLS label {#new-tls-label .unnumbered}

##### Label Reassignment and Confidence Estimation {#label-reassignment-and-confidence-estimation .unnumbered}

- For each AOI, determine the top and second-highest signature scores.

# Label Reassignment and Confidence Estimation {#label-reassignment-and-confidence-estimation .unnumbered}

- For each AOI, determine the top and second-highest signature scores.

- Calculated a **confidence metric** = (top − second) / top.

- AOIs with confidence \< 0.5 were labeled as *"Intermediate"*,
 > suggesting ambiguous transcriptional states.

- Reassigned labels where appropriate, preserving "Agg" classification
 > for AOIs originally annotated as aggregates but enriched in TB or
 > Stromal genes.

- Compared **original (tls_status)** vs **new labels** using PCA/UMAP
 > visualizations and proportional bar plots.

##### Identification of Ambiguous or Misclassified AOIs {#identification-of-ambiguous-or-misclassified-aois .unnumbered}

- Flagged AOIs with inconsistent or ambiguous labels (e.g., GC→TB,
 > Agg→GC transitions).

- Compiled and exported lists (suspicious_labels_all.xlsx) for manual
 > spatial review.

- Highlighted candidate GC-like Agg regions and potential misannotated
 > GC ROIs based on clustering and expression heatmaps.

## J）Scellex BCR Pilot {#jscellex-bcr-pilot .unnumbered}

### SCellex technology {#scellex-technology .unnumbered}

SCellex develops a spatial single-cell gene expression analysis platform
based on visual barcodes.

Their core technology is a **visualized barcode-based Picowell
microarray**, combined with AI image analysis, enabling the direct
capture of RNA on tissue slices or single-cell suspensions while
preserving spatial location information.

- Random barcodes combined with image localization can achieve spatial
 > resolution capabilities close to those of a single cell.

The goal is to perform high-throughput multi-omics sequencing while
maintaining spatial context (i.e., cell location within the tissue).

The plan is to first test spatial methods with PolyA captured for Fresh
Frozen. Theoretically, this has the potential to capture RNA targets on
FFPE and combine them with a BCR/TCR specific RNA panel for spatially

methods with PolyA captured for Fresh
Frozen. Theoretically, this has the potential to capture RNA targets on
FFPE and combine them with a BCR/TCR specific RNA panel for spatially
localized BCR analysis. The technology platform supports multi-omics and
functional assays, and can be further integrated with proteomics in the
future.

samples to confirm successful RNA capture and compare efficiency. Check
if specific sequences can be amplified for BCR/TCR-related fragments (to

vector_chunks.text:
2 Abstract 
High-grade serous ovarian cancer (HGSC) is the most aggressive subtype of ovarian 
cancer. Most patients are diagnosed at an advanced stage, resulting in a poor 
prognosis. Although some targeted therapies have made good progress, immune 
checkpoint inhibitors (ICIs) still show limited efficacy in HGSC treatment. This is 
closely associated with the high heterogeneity and unique immunosuppressive tumor 
microenvironment (TME) of HGSC. As local immune centers in the TME, tertiary 
lymphoid structures (TLSs) contain diverse types of immune cells and play a crucial 
role in the anti-tumor immune process. Nonetheless, the spatial immune landscape of 
TLSs and key gene expression signatures affecting prognosis have not been fully 
elucidated. In recent years, spatially resolved gene expression profiling of clinical 
samples has provided insights into the transcriptome at high resolution. However, 
despite revealing a series of response mechanisms of anti-tumor immunity , these 
methods still have many limitations in non-single cell resolution, specific tissue 
sample types, and high cost. 
Here in this thesis, I establish ed Nano-Pick, a novel method based on precise 
microtissue extraction and the nCounter analysis system. Nano-Pick enables cost-
effective and rapid spatial gene expression profiling using formalin-fixed paraffin-
embedded (FFPE) samples.

vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
e extraction and the nCounter analysis system. Nano-Pick enables cost-
effective and rapid spatial gene expression profiling using formalin-fixed paraffin-
embedded (FFPE) samples. All TLSs-enriched FFPE slides were obtained from 
patient-derived HGSC samples at Helsinki University Hospital (HUH). With the help 
of tissue cyclic immunofluorescence (t-CycIF), a highly multiplexed imaging method, 
selected regions of interests (ROIs) were annotated in different zones of TLSs. These 
ROIs were accurately transferred to adjacent tissue sections, guiding the target region 
selection for microtissue extraction. An improved immunofluorescence staining 
method was used to successfully label cancer cells, macrophages, and immune cells 
without antigen retrieval. With the optimized combination of parameters, efficient and 
consistent precise picking for different cell types was achieved. mRNA extracted from 
the collected microtissues was validated by qPCR for the range of pre-amplification 
cycles, confirming its suitability for nCounter gene panel analysis. Therefore, Nano-
Pick offers the opportunity for higher-resolution spatial analysis and interactions of 
immune cell populations in the TME, especially in the TLSs. This low-cost, reliable 
and sensitive spatial gene expression profiling method has broad application prospects

immune cell populations in the TME, especially in the TLSs. This low-cost, reliable

5 
 1 Literature review 
1.1 Ovarian cancer 
Ovarian cancer is the most lethal type of gynecological malignant tumor. Globally, 
more than 310,000 new cases of ovarian cancer are diagnosed per year, leading to the 
death of over 200,000 women (WCRF International, 2020). Due to the difficulty of 
early diagnosis and high invasiveness, only 30% of ovarian cancer patients survive 
more than 5 years after first diagnosis (Baldwin et al., 2012). 
Ovarian cancer is a highly heterogeneous disease. It can be categorized into several 
histological subtypes based on diverse clinicopathological and molecular features. 
Epithelial ovarian cancer (EOC) is the most predominant type, which encompasses a 
range of specific subtypes including high -grade serous carcinoma (HGSC), low -grade 
serous carcinoma (LGSC), endometrioid carcinoma, clear cell carcinoma, and 
mucinous carcinoma (Kaku et al., 2003). EOC is believed to originate from abnormal 
epithelial cell proliferation in fallopian tubes (Labidi -Galy et al., 2017), while other 
subtypes of non -epithelial ovarian cancer (NEOC) arise from germ -cell, sex-cord, or 
stromal tissue (Kim et al., 2018). 
1.1.1 HGSC 
As the most common and aggressive subtype of ovarian cancer, HGSC has a notably 
poor prognosis, accounting for approximately 70% of all ovarian cancer deaths (Peres 
et al., 2019).

vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
s the most common and aggressive subtype of ovarian cancer, HGSC has a notably 
poor prognosis, accounting for approximately 70% of all ovarian cancer deaths (Peres 
et al., 2019). This is due to the advanced stage at which it is often diagnosed, as well 
as its rapid development and aggressive nature (Dao et al., 2016). Around 75% of 
HGSC patients are diagnosed at stage III or IV according to the FIGO staging system 
(Peres et al., 2019) , indicating that tumor cells have spread to distant lymph nodes and 
other tissues within the pelvi s (stage III), or distant metastasis are present (stage IV) . 
The new dualistic model of ovarian carcinogenesis categorizes HGSC as a type II 
carcinoma with high -grade histology and high proliferative activity , while other

vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
6 
 subtypes that develop from benign extra ovarian lesions are classified as type I 
carcinoma (Kurman & Shih, 2016). 
A key feature of HGSC is its high degree of genetic instability: more than 96% of 
cases harbor TP53 mutations (Kuhn et al., 2012 ). Also, genetic mutations in the 
homologous recombination repair (HRR) pathway are very common, such as 
alterations in BRCA1 or BRCA2 (Perets et al., 2013). HGSC is heavily dependent on 
the HRR to repair DNA double -strand breaks (DSBs) from exogenous anticancer 
treatments such as ionizing radiation. Compared with the non -homologous end -
joining (NHEJ) pathway, HRR uses homologous D NA templates to assist the repair, 
which significantly reduces the error rate (Ledermann et al., 2016). However, because 
of related genes’ copy number variation, around 50% of HGSC patients exhibit an 
HRR deficiency (HRD)(Fuh et al., 2020) . These HRD patients are more sensitive to 
platinum-based chemotherapy because they cannot repair DNA damage induced by 
chemotherapy drugs . HRD patients also benefit more from the synthetic lethality 
approach provided by PARP inhibitors (PARPi), although the molecular mechanisms 
affecting their susceptibility or resistance remain a subject of ongoing study 
(Konstantinopoulos et al., 2015). 
1.1.2 Clinical treatments 
The median age for diagnosis of o varian cancer is 63 , with patients usually ranging

vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
in a subject of ongoing study 
(Konstantinopoulos et al., 2015). 
1.1.2 Clinical treatments 
The median age for diagnosis of o varian cancer is 63 , with patients usually ranging 
from 55-74 years old (NIH, 2023). Given the invasive nature and high dissemination 
rate of HGSC , cytoreduction surgery is the preferred treatment . Primary debulking 
surgery (PDS) is typically performed for patients at earlier FIGO stages (I or II) , 
where tumor tissues are confined solely to the pelvic cavity and are completely 
removable . The objective of PDS is to eliminate as much visible tumor tissue as 
possible to achieve complete resection. A postoperative finding of tiny residual 
tumors, smaller than 1cm in diameter (R=0 or R<1), is classified as "optimal 
debulking", whereas the discovery of larger residual tumors (R>1) signifies 
"suboptimal debulking" (Hoskins et al., 1994). After surgery, patients often receive

vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
7 
 adjuvant chemotherapy to further eradicate tiny tumor cells that cannot be removed by 
surgery, thereby reducing the risk of relapse and prolonging survival (Matulonis et al., 
2016). Typical chemotherapy regimens involve a combination of platinum -based 
compounds and taxanes , such as carboplatin and paclitaxel (Du Bois et al., 2003). A 
standard course of chemotherapy consists of 6 cycles, which can be adjusted based on 
the patient’s condition and their response to the treatment. If severe side effects occur 
or the cancer tissue shows resistance to the initial medication, the chemotherapy 
regimen must be adjusted or paused. 
For patients diagnosed with advanced stage (e.g. stage IV), optimal cytoreduction is 
difficult to achieve and neoadjuvant chemotherapy (NACT) is recommended to 
reduce tumor burden before surgery (Gill et al., 2017). NACT typically consists of 3 
cycles of chemotherapy on the combination of platinum and taxanes, but some 
patients also receive NACT on an individualized basis ranging from 1 to 8 cycles 
(Kobal et al., 2018). The approach of neoadjuvant chemotherapy followed by interval 
debulking surgery (NACT -IDS) is gradu ally gaining acceptance in clinical settings, 
although its effectiveness compared to direct PDS remains a topic of debate. Results 
from several randomized trials indicate that patients at advanced stages undergoing

vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
ttings, 
although its effectiveness compared to direct PDS remains a topic of debate. Results 
from several randomized trials indicate that patients at advanced stages undergoing 
NACT-IDS have no significant differences in progression -free survival (PFS) and 
overall survival (OS) compared to those undergoing PDS directly, but adverse effects 
(such as hemorrhage, venous thromboembolism, infection) and mortality are 
significantly reduced (van der Burg et al., 1995; Rose et al., 2004; Vergote et al., 
2010; Qin et al., 2018). On the contrary , some authors claim that in their clinical trials 
enrolling stage IV ovarian cancer patients, NACT -IDS leads to significantly longer 
OS and PFS (Van Meurs et al., 2013; Vergote et al., 2018). In conclusion , many 
studies support that NACT -IDS shows improvements over PDS, especially for 
advanced HGSC patients with a high tumor burden (Kehoe et al., 2015; Gill et al., 
2017; Liu et al., 2020).

vector_chunks.text:
8 
 Despite the efforts of researchers over the past three decades, the cure rate, PFS , and 
OS of HGSC patients have not significantly improved. The 10 -year survival rate of 
patients with advanced HGSC is less than 20%, and 75% will develop platinum 
resistance and relapse within 18 months after treatment (Clifford et al., 2018). The 
mechanisms leading to chemotherapy resistance and relaps e have not yet been 
identified, some possible causes include metabolic reprogramming, oxidative stress, 
resistance of cancer stem cells, cell cycle regulation, and abnormal chronic activation 
of signal pathways including PTEN, KRAS , and mTOR (Yang et al., 2022). 
In recent years, new targeted therapies have shown promising effects in the treatment 
of HGSC. As mentioned before, some PARPi, such as olaparib and niraparib, have 
become efficient medications for patients with homologous recombination deficiency 
(HRD) especially those with BRCA1/2 mutations (Caruso et al., 2017; Poveda et al., 
2022). Another new targeted drug is bevacizumab, a monoclonal antibody that targets 
vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) to inhibit angiogenesis in ovarian cancer. 
The combinat ion of bevacizumab with after -surgery chemotherapy has been 
demonstrated to prolong the PFS of patients at III/IV stage HGSC (Oza et al., 2015; 
Pujade-Lauraine et al., 2014).

vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
at ion of bevacizumab with after -surgery chemotherapy has been 
demonstrated to prolong the PFS of patients at III/IV stage HGSC (Oza et al., 2015; 
Pujade-Lauraine et al., 2014). These new treatment methods have been widely used in 
clinical treatment, offering personalized and precise therapeutic options for some 
patients to achieve improved prognoses . 
However, immunotherapies, proven to be effective in treating other types of cancer 
like melanoma and non-small cell lung cancer, have not shown equivalent success in 
HGSC. In particular , some well-known immune checkpoint inhibitors (ICI) targeting 
programmed cell death protein 1 (PD-1) or programmed death -ligand 1 (PD -L1) only 
show limited effect in HGSC . Although large -scale studies in ovarian cancer patients 
have confirmed that high levels of CD3+ or CD8+ T cells are significantly associated 
with improved PFS and OS (Hwang et al., 20 12), suggesting substantial potential for 
anti-PD-L1/PD-1 therapy, it has yet to demonstrate notable efficacy in several initial 
clinical trials using PD-L1/PD-1 inhibitors such as avelumab, atezolizumab,

vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
9 pembrolizumab a nd nivolumab (Chardin & Lear y, 2021). A meta-analysis 
encompassing 15 clinical trials assessed the overall response rate (ORR) of PD-1/PD-
L1 inhibitors in ovarian cancer treatment, revealing that the ORR is merely 10% for a 
PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitor and around 30% with the combination of chemotherapy (Zhu, 
Yan & Wang, 2021 ). One explanation is the low expression levels of PD-L1 on both 
cancer cells and immune cells within the tumor microenvironment (TME) of ovarian 
cancer, with less than 25% of patie nts exhibiting more than 5% PD-L1+ immune cells 
(Moore et al., 2017). Other findings showed that the use of ICIs including PD-1, 
LAG-3, and CTLA-4 alone leads to compensatory upregulation of alternative 
checkpoint pathways, this way suppressing the activation of loca l T cells (Huang et 
al., 2017), which offe rs a possible rationa le for the observed lack of efficacy of single 
ICI in clinical tria ls. Therefore, the potential f or clinical trials exploring combination 
therapies of multiple ICI needs to be noticed. Consider ing the high heterogeneity and 
unique immunosuppressive TME of HGSC (Rodriguez et al., 2018; Male ki Vareki, 
2018), more research is needed to further understand the compensatory mechanisms 
activated following immune checkpoint blockade as well as regulations of the 
immune network within spec ial TME of HGSC.
