eeded to further understand the compensatory mechanisms 
activated following immune checkpoint blockade as well as regulations of the 
immune network within spec ial TME of HGSC. 
1.2 Tumor microenvironment 
The tumor microenvironment (TME) is a highly complex and dynamic micro-
ecosystem comprising tumor, stromal, and immune cells with high intra- and inter-
individual heterogeneit y. It can either promote or suppress immune responses through 
a series of cellular interactions during cancer progression and treatment. Over time, 
the TME co-evolves with the tumor, facilitating the emergence of diverse cancer cell 
subclones and contributing to individual tumor heterogeneity ( So et al., 2022). The 
TME also encompasses a variety of stromal cells, such as endothelial cells, 
fibroblasts, and activated cancer-associated fibroblasts (CAF) (Bussard et al., 2016). 
These cells play a crucial role in remodeling the extracellular matrix (ECM), altering 
pathways of cancer cell migration, and modifying intercellular communication (Wu &

vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
10 
 Dai, 2017). The TME also houses a diverse array of immune cells from both the 
innate immune system —including macrophages, dendritic cells (DC), and natural 
killer (NK) cells —and the adaptive immune system, comprising CD8+ cytotoxic T 
lymphocytes, CD4+ help er T cells, regulatory T cells (Treg), B cells, and plasma cells 
(Balkwill et al., 2012). 
The TME plays a key role in shaping the anti -tumor immune response. The activation 
of tumor immunity involves processes that enable the innate and adaptive immune 
systems to identify and eliminate tumor cells. Infiltrating into the tumor stroma, 
natural ki ller (NK) cells represent a critical component of the innate immune defense 
against tumors. Antigen-presenting cells (APCs) in the TME, such as dendritic cells, 
can capture tumor -specific antigens and present them to T cells to facilitate T cell 
activation and tumor -killing effects. Furthermore, B cells can recognize surface 
antigens of tumor cell s and differentiate into plasma cells, producing and secreting 
antibodies to promote the elimination of tumor cells. On the other hand , the TME also 
harbors immune cells like regulatory T cells (Tregs), myeloid -derived suppressor cells 
(MDSCs), and M2 macrophages, which contribute to the immunosuppressive network 
that regulates anti-tumor immu nity (Rodriguez et al., 2018).

vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
, myeloid -derived suppressor cells 
(MDSCs), and M2 macrophages, which contribute to the immunosuppressive network 
that regulates anti-tumor immu nity (Rodriguez et al., 2018). 
Compared with other cancers, ovarian cancer is distinguished by a particularly unique 
and highly heterogeneous TME with suppressive immune regulatory networks. The 
abundance of immunosuppressive cytokines within the TME impairs T cell cytotoxic 
functions, while the recruitment of regulatory cells such as Tregs and MDSC further 
suppresses the immune response (Jiang et al., 2015). Additionally, due to the chronic 
exposure to tumor antigens and immunosuppressive signals in the TME, exhausted 
CD8+ T cells also failed to contribute to anti-tumor immune responses. This 
exhaustion is mainly marked by the upregulation of inhibitory receptors like PD -1 and 
CTLA-4, a decrease in effective cytokine secretion, and diminished cytotoxic 
potential (Gholami et al., 2015). The suppressive nature of the TME hinders the

vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
11 immune system's capacity to effectively recognize and eradicate proliferating cancer 
cells, ultimately leading to tumor progression. 
1.2.1 Key cell populations and anti-tumor immunity 
Tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes (TI Ls) are a critical subset of immune cells that 
migrate from the bloodstream into the TME, where they play a central role in 
orchestrating immune responses against cancer cells. TILs mainly include CD8+ 
cytotoxic T lymphocytes (CTL), CD4+ helper T cells, Tregs, B cells, and plasma 
cells. Among these, CTL is considered to have strong and specific capabilities in 
targeting and eliminating tumor cells, serving as key players in anti-tumor immunit y. 
The process of this immune response initiation involves mature dendritic cells (DCs), 
which are responsible for tumor antigen recognition and capture . They then activate T 
cells by presenting processed peptides through major histocompatibility complex 
class I (MHC I) and class I ( MHC II) molecules (Chen & Mellman, 2013). Once 
activated, T cells can migrate and infiltrate into tumor tissues, where they recognize 
and eradicate cancer cells with the help of T cell receptors (TCR) and secreted cell 
lytic molecules like perforin, granzyme B, and IFNγ (Jiang et al., 2020). The antigens 
from destroyed cancer cells are subsequently recaptured by DCs, sparking a 
beneficial cycle that activates more T cells.

vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
, granzyme B, and IFNγ (Jiang et al., 2020). The antigens 
from destroyed cancer cells are subsequently recaptured by DCs, sparking a 
beneficial cycle that activates more T cells. During this process, CD4+ helper T cells 
can secrete cytokines to enhance CD8+ T cell activation and proliferati on. They also 
facilitate the recruitment and maturation of additional DCs into the TME through high 
levels of CCL5 secretion, ensuring sustained long-term anti-tumor immunity (Nesbeth 
et al., 2010). 
Stromal or intraepithelial B lymphocytes are also identified in many clinical samples 
from EOC patients. Their presence is generally considered to be associate d with a 
favorable prognosis, but depends on different tumor stages (Milne et al., 2009). A 
higher density infiltration of B cells, plasma cell s, and CD8+ T cells is correlated with 
improved outcomes in HGSC patients (Kroeger et al., 2016). Furthermore , evidence 
suggests that the co-localization of B lymphocytes with CD8+ TILs within the TME

vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
12 
 improves patient survival rate, compared to the samples with the presence of CD8+ 
TILs alone (Nielsen et al., 2012). This advantageous effect might be attributed to the 
pathway wherein IFN γ secreted by B cells aids in the polarization of CD4+ Th cells 
towards Th1, thereby facilitating T cell expansion through the presen tation of tumor -
associated antigens (TAA) (Sarvaria et al., 2017). Investigations into TILs within 
recurrent metastases of HGSC patients reveal that B cells predominantly infiltrate 
lymphoid structures in the stromal areas of metastatic tumors. These B cells are 
capable of producing tumor -specific IgG and exhibit robust memory responses to 
TAA, thereby supporting anti -tumor immune responses (Montfort et al., 2017). 
To prevent cytotoxic T cells from attacking the body's own tissues, the TME also 
encompasses an extensive immunosuppressive network with immune regulatory cells. 
Regulatory T cells (Treg), in particular, can regulate immune responses against self -
tissue antigens and suppress T cell activity, primarily through the secretion of 
cytokines such as IL -10, IL-35, and TGF β (Facciabene et al., 2012). Within the TME 
of ovarian cancer, the chemokine CCL22, secreted by tumor cells, TAMs , and DCs, 
recruits more Foxp3+ Treg cells and induces them to activate immunosuppressive

vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
. Within the TME 
of ovarian cancer, the chemokine CCL22, secreted by tumor cells, TAMs , and DCs, 
recruits more Foxp3+ Treg cells and induces them to activate immunosuppressive 
activity under hypoxic conditions in the TME, aiding in the immune escape of tumor 
cells (Fialová et al., 2013; Rodriguez et al., 2018). Research has linked the 
accumulation of FOXP3+ Treg cells wit h increased mortality risk, advanced disease 
stage, and poor response to tumor reduction efforts in ovarian cancer patients (Barnett 
et al., 2010; Facciabene et al., 2011). In mouse models, the depletion of Treg cells 
significantly increased the secretion levels of IL -6 and TNF α and enhanced the 
infiltration of CD8+ T cells, resulting in better anti -tumor immune activation (Chen et 
al., 2012). Besides, the presence of myeloid -derived suppressor cells (MDSCs), along 
with suppressive cytokines such as TGF -β and the expression of various T cell 
inhibitory checkpoint receptors (CTLA -4, PD-1, LAG-3, TIM-3, TIGIT) all 
contributes to this comprehensive redundant inhibitory network of endogenous anti -
tumor immunity. This network poses a significant challenge to elic iting effective anti -
tumor responses through cancer immunotherapy.

vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
13 
 Given the significant variability in TILs infiltration among individuals and its critical 
role in indicating clinical treatment options and prognosis, the academic community 
categorizes cancer immunophenotypes into three major types: immune desert 
(absence of immune cells within the TME), immune -excluded (tumor areas are 
surrounded by immune cells but lacking effective immune cell infiltration), inflamed 
tumors (active TILs infiltration and anti -tumor immune responses) (Chen & Mellman, 
2017). Ovarian tumors classified as immune desert are typically non -inflammatory 
and exhibit a limited response to immune checkpoint inhibitors like anti -PD-1/anti-
PD-L1. Therefore, it is necessary to find therapeutic strategies to induce the activation 
of immune cells and inc rease the infiltration of TILs in the TME during clinical 
treatment. The TME of inflamed tumors contains a large number of immune cells, 
especially T cells, and therefore benefit from treatment with checkpoint inhibitor 
antibodies and monoclonal antibodies targeting tumor -associated antigens. It's 
important to acknowledge, however, that inflamed tumors often harbor exhausted T 
cells and large numbers of Treg cells in their TME, leading to immune evasion and 
immunosuppression (Paijens et al., 2021). In the c ase of the immune -excluded

vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
bor exhausted T 
cells and large numbers of Treg cells in their TME, leading to immune evasion and 
immunosuppression (Paijens et al., 2021). In the c ase of the immune -excluded 
phenotype, where immune cells are predominantly located in the peritumoral stroma, 
anti-angiogenic therapies are more effective in enhancing the treatment efficacy based 
on the tumor's immunophenotype (García -Martínez & Pérez -Fidalgo, 2020). 
The extent and composition of different types of TILs infiltration have been proven to 
possess prognostic significance in ovarian cancer according to clinical research 
findings. An analysis of 186 fresh frozen ovarian cancer samples from patients at 
advanced stages revealed that the presence of CD3+ TILs within tumors correlated 
with a significantly higher five -year survival rate of 38%, compared to mere 4.5% in 
patients lacking TILs (Zhang et al., 2003). This positive association persisted among 
patients w ho had undergone tumor debulking and received platinum -based 
chemotherapy (Zhang et al., 2003). Further studies showed that infiltration by CD8+ 
TIL cells is indicative of a favorable prognosis in ovarian cancer, suggesting a more 
pronounced beneficial eff ect of CD8+ TILs over CD3+ TILs (Clarke et al., 2009; Sato

vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
14 
 et al., 2005). Additionally, Sato et al. also observed that the subgroup of patients with 
a higher ratio of CD8+/CD4+ TILs exhibited improved overall survival, which is 
possibly due to the negative influence of CD4+ FOXP3+ Tregs. However, some 
findings indicate that a substantial proportion of activated CD4+ T cells is 
significantly associated with improved median overall survival and survival rate in 
EOC patients (Tsiatas et al., 2009; Le Page et al., 2012). This can be attributed to the 
role of CD4+ T cells in facilitating the recruitment and activation of dendritic cells 
(DCs). Another large -scale meta -analysis further solidified the correlation between the 
abundant presence of intraepithelial CD8+ TILs in EOC patient samples and better 
survival outcomes, irrespective of different tumor grades, stages, or histological 
subtypes (Hwang et al., 2012). Although most tumor cells express antigens that can be 
recognized by CD8+ T cells, various mechanisms allow these tumor cells to neutralize 
anti-tumor immune re sponses in various ways and achieve immune evasion (Brichard 
& Lejeune, 2007). Therefore, amplifying the functional response of CD8+ TILs in the 
existing TME and enhancing the recruitment of more effector T cells to the TME are 
critical strategies in cance r immunotherapy. 
Tumor-associated macrophages (TAMs) represent a predominant macrophage

vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
ing the recruitment of more effector T cells to the TME are 
critical strategies in cance r immunotherapy. 
Tumor-associated macrophages (TAMs) represent a predominant macrophage 
population in the TME and are closely related to tumor progression and metastasis. 
Originating either from the infiltration of tissue -resident macrophages or the 
recruitment of circulat ing myeloid monocytes in the blood (Ghosn et al., 2010), TAMs 
can differentiate into two distinct subtypes, M1 macrophages (anti -tumor & pro -
inflammatory) and M2 macrophages (pro -tumor & anti -inflammatory). Interferon -
gamma (IFN γ), secreted by CD4+ and CD8 + T cells, can activate the polarization 
from monocytes into M1 macrophages. These macrophages are characterized by their 
production of immunostimulatory cytokines such as IL -12 and TNF -α, which 
contribute to the suppression of tumor progression and the en hancement of anti -tumor 
immune responses (Sica et al., 2008). On the contrary, in the presence of 
immunosuppressive cytokines like IL -4 and IL-13, monocytes are inclined to 
differentiate into M2 macrophages (Van Dalen et al., 2018). M2 macrophages

vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
15 
 participate in the process of angiogenesis and extracellular matrix remodeling to 
further promote the development of an immunosuppressive microenvironment by 
releasing T cell regulatory factors such as CCL -22, thus aiding in the dissemination of 
cancer cel ls (Sica et al., 2008; Curiel et al., 2004). 
Research on EOC patients has revealed a significant infiltration of TAMs in both 
tissue and ascitic fluid, with a dominance of the M2 TAMs subtype that expresses 
CD163 and CD206 (Takaishi et al., 2010). EOC cells can express the co -inhibitory 
molecule B7 -H4 with M2 TAMs to suppress t he proliferation and cytotoxicity of T 
cells (Kryczek et al., 2007). This interaction can accelerate the recruitment of 
additional Treg cells to the tumor vicinity, further forming an immunosuppressive 
microenvironment (Kryczek et al., 2007). Clinical investigations have correlated 
heightened levels of M2 TAMs expression with tumor progression and poor overall 
survival rates (Kawamura et al., 2009; Zhang et al., 2014). Moreover, studies indicate 
a positive association between the M1/M2 and M2/TAMs ratios in ovarian tumors and 
both PFS and OS, though the overall density of TAMs does not hold prognostic 
significance (Lan et al., 2013; Zhang et al., 2014). While the M1/M2 macrophage 
dichotomy offers prognostic insights, recent transc riptome analysis has unveiled the

vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
prognostic 
significance (Lan et al., 2013; Zhang et al., 2014). While the M1/M2 macrophage 
dichotomy offers prognostic insights, recent transc riptome analysis has unveiled the 
complexity and heterogeneity within TAMs populations (Xue et al., 2014). This 
means that the entire population of macrophages spans a continuous spectrum of 
functional states, with M1 and M2 representing the extremes at it s two ends (Ostuni et 
al., 2015). In summary, TAMs subpopulations, varying in polarization, critically 
influence the tumor microenvironment's dynamics and the clinical prognosis of EOC, 
highlighting the intricate interplay within the TME. 
As a major component of the stromal region, cancer -associated fibroblasts (CAFs) 
also have unique roles in ovarian cancer progression. Recent studies have illuminated 
that CAFs can be activated by substances like lysophosphatidic acid produced by 
ovarian cancer cells (Jeon et al., 2008), and they can subsequently promote tumor 
growth and metastasis through mechanisms such as the Hedgehog signaling pathway

vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
16 
 and angiopoietin expression (Heller et al., 2012; Gilead et al., 2004). 
Immunohistochemistry analysis has revealed a higher prevalence of CAFs in the TME 
of ovarian cancer compared with benign ovarian tissues (Zhang et al., 2011). 
Moreover, α-smooth muscle actin (α-SMA) expressed by CAF is significantly 
increased in ovarian cancer patients with distal lymph node and omental metastasis 
(Zhang et al., 2011). Considering the dynamic bidirectional interactions and intricate 
relationships between CAFs and tumor cells, CAFs are another promising therapeutic 
target in the TME. Several non -specific receptor tyrosine kinase inhibitors targeting 
different fibroblast growth factor receptor (FGFR) subtypes, such as lucitanib, 
dovitinib, and nintedanib, have been authori zed for clinical trials in cohorts of 
advanced patients to assess their effectiveness both as monotherapies and in 
combination with chemotherapy. Notably, nintedanib has been shown to significantly 
improve PFS in the combination treatment with carboplatin and paclitaxel 
chemotherapy (Du Bois, 2013). These trial results demonstrate the potential of 
targeting the tumor stroma as a strategy to modulate the cancer TME and ultimately 
inhibit cancer progression. 
Considering the close connection between the TME and tumor progression, several 
treatments targeting the TME have been studied and developed.

vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
imately 
inhibit cancer progression. 
Considering the close connection between the TME and tumor progression, several 
treatments targeting the TME have been studied and developed. Immune checkpoint 
inhibitors represent a significant advancement in immunotherapy in recent years. 
These engineere d molecules are designed to accurately block inhibitory checkpoint 
molecules, such as PD -1, PD-L1, CTLA -4, etc., thereby activating or restoring T cell 
immune functions (Chardin & Leary, 2021). However, as mentioned above, despite 
their success in treating solid tumors like melanoma, ICIs such as PD -1/PD-L1 
inhibitors have not been achieved as expected in the treatment of HGSC. This 
discrepancy can be attributed to the distinctive heterogeneity of the TME in ovarian 
cancer (Rodriguez et al., 2018). Given th at inhibiting the interaction between PD -1 
and its ligands has demonstrated the capability to drive antitumor activity in 
preclinical models (Topalian et al., 2012), PD -1/PD-L1 targeted ovarian cancer 
therapy is still a valuable area of research. Consequen tly, exploring combination

vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
17 
 therapies that involve multiple ICIs or integrating ICIs with other targeted therapies 
represents a promising direction for clinical research, potentially leading to significant 
breakthroughs in the future. 
In addition, other therapeutic therapies targeting the TME of ovarian cancer include 
using VEGF receptor inhibitors to interfere with tumor angiogenesis, using CCL2 
inhibitors to block TAM recruitment, and using small molecules such as TLR -7 
agonists to re polarize M2 macrophage into anti -tumor M1 macrophages (Yang et al., 
2020). Cancer vaccines represent another innovative approach, which is designed to 
stimulate the immune system to recognize and attack cancer cells by immunogenic 
tumor antigens. Adoptive cell therapy (ACT), which involves the selection and 
expansion of antigen -specific T cells ex vivo, and the reperfusion into the patient, 
offers a personalized treatment avenue for ovarian cancer. These new therapies are 
promising but have not yet achieved stable and widespread positive outcomes in 
clinical responses (Odunsi, 2017). 
1.2.2 Spatial tumor microenvironment 
In previous research, traditional single -cell omics technologies such as scRNA -seq 
allow researchers to deeply explore the composition of cell types and differential gene 
expression in the TME of ovarian cancer, which leads to an acknowledgment of the

vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
NA -seq 
allow researchers to deeply explore the composition of cell types and differential gene 
expression in the TME of ovarian cancer, which leads to an acknowledgment of the 
TME's complex alterations during patient treatment and its heterogeneity across 
different individuals. However, the absence of spatial information hinders our 
understanding of the actual spatial cellular organization and their interactions in the 
TME of ovarian cancer. This also leads to some controversies observed in prior 
research, su ch as conflicting results on the role of activated CD4+ T cells in tumor 
progression across different cohorts, and inconsistent prognostic significance of the 
proportion of different types of TIL infiltration (Sato et al., 2005; Tsiatas et al., 2009; 
Le Page et al., 2012). 
In recent years, with the development of single -cell and spatial technologies, spatial 
transcriptomics has become a new revolutionary method to research spatial TME.

vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
18 
 Now the significance of the TME extends beyond merely the type and quantity of 
cells. Their spatial distribution and the proximity between different cell populations 
also have a crucial impact on cell -cell interactions and the outcomes of immune 
responses (Tian et al., 2023). Furthermore, the structure of the ECM, the presence of 
oxygen and nutrient gradients, and the concentration gradients of various signaling 
molecules all affect the effectiveness of the anti -tumor immune response in the spatial 
dimension. A spatial analysis of ovarian cancer TILs has demonstrated significant 
correlations between the proximity of TAMs, B cells, CD4 T cells, and patient overall 
survival, positioning the spatial interactions of immune cells as a novel predictor of 
treatment outcomes (Steinhart et al., 2021). In another study using samples from 
patients with recurrent ovarian cancer treated with a combination of PARPi and anti -
PD1, the researchers conducted single -cell spatial clustering analysis and found that 
exhausted CD8+ T-cells are associated with PD -L1+ macrophages, PD -L1+ dendritic 
cells and PD -L1+ tumor cells (Färkkilä et al., 2020). Interestingly, higher attraction 
between exhausted CD8+ T cells and PD -L1+ macrophages is significantly associated 
with better treatment response outcomes (Färkkilä et al., 2020). Such research on the

vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
raction 
between exhausted CD8+ T cells and PD -L1+ macrophages is significantly associated 
with better treatment response outcomes (Färkkilä et al., 2020). Such research on the 
spatial interaction of PD -1/PD-L1 in the TME can further reveal the molecular 
mechanism of PD -1/PD-L1-mediated immunosuppression mechanisms, thereby 
providing predictions and guidance on clin ical immunotherapy outcomes of ICI 
combinations such as PARPi/anti -PD1. This evidence further underscores the notion 
that the spatial characteristics as well as the spatial interactions among various 
immune cell subpopulations in the TME are highly related to immunotherapy 
response, exhibiting the value of spatial transcriptomics research. 
1.3 Tertiary lymphoid structure 
The tertiary lymphoid structure s (TLSs) are spontaneously formed, lymph node -like 
aggregates of diverse immune cells within non -lymphoid tissues. These structures 
emerge in areas where chronic inflammatory responses are sustained due to infection 
or disease, including cancer and autoimmune disease (Schumacher & Thommen,

vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
19 
 2022). Widely present in the TME, TLS s contain TIL cell clusters with well -
characterized structures, including mature dendritic cells, abundant T cells, and B cell 
areas containing germinal centers (GC) (Kang et al., 2021). TLS s play a crucial role 
in initiating and sustaining local adaptive immune responses within tumor sites, 
thereby enhancing the effect of anti -tumor immunity and potentially improving 
patient prognosis (Dieu -Nosjean et al., 2014). While the precise mechanisms through 
which TLS s contribute to anti-tumor immunity are still being explored, strategies 
aimed at increasing TLS formation in the TME and utilizing these structures to 
promote lymphocyte infiltration have shown great potential in cancer treatment 
(Sautès-Fridman et al., 2019). 
1.3.1 TLS in the immune system 
The human immune system is a complex dynamic network composed of various 
immune cells and organs, mainly including two branches: innate immunity and 
adaptive immunity. Innate immunity serves as the immune system's initial line of 
defense, characterized by its rapid and nonspecific immune responses. Key players in 
this cellular network of innate immunity include macrophages, neutrophils, DC, and 
NK cells. Adaptive immunity can generate targeted immune responses against 
specific pathogens or antigens with imm une memory. B lymphocytes and T

vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
ophages, neutrophils, DC, and 
NK cells. Adaptive immunity can generate targeted immune responses against 
specific pathogens or antigens with imm une memory. B lymphocytes and T 
lymphocytes are the principal cellular components that constitute adaptive immunity. 
They perform their immune roles through the secretion of specific antibodies and the 
mediation of cytotoxic T -cell activities, respectivel y (Clark & Kupper, 2005). 
Different organs are involved in the immune system. The bone marrow and thymus 
are classified as primary lymphoid organs, serving as crucial nests for the 
proliferation and maturation of numerous immune cells. B lymphocytes are developed 
and matured in the bone marrow, while T lymphocytes also originate in the bone 
marrow and migrate to the thymus for their maturation process. Secondary lymphoid 
organs (SLOs) include lymph nodes, spleen, and mucosa -associated lymphoid tissue 
(MALT). SLOs act as hubs for interactions between innate and adaptive immune

vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
20 
 cells, facilitating the stimulation and regulation of adaptive immune responses (Qi et 
al., 2014). Within these SLOs, mature lymphocytes encounter antigens presented by 
antigen-presenting cells (APC), such as lysosomal associated membrane protein+ 
dendritic cells (DC -LAMP+) (Sautès -Fridman et al., 2019). Upon activation by these 
antigens, the lymphocytes proliferate and differentiate into effector T cells and B 
memory cells, orchestrating a robust adaptive immune response against cancer 
(Sautès-Fridman et a l., 2019). 
Tertiary lymphoid structures are local immune centers that develop de novo in 
multiple tissues in response to inflammatory signals, cytokines, and chemokines. 
Similar to the stromal structure formed by follicular reticular cells (FRC) in SLOs, the 
establishment of TLS s relies on a dense stromal network, which assists in embedding 
TLSs within stromal, peritumoral, and intratumoral areas (Barone et al., 2016). As an 
ectopic lymphoid structure, TLS has a center region where CD20+ B cells aggregate 
and peripher al compartments populated by CD3+ T cells, mirroring the anatomy 
architecture of lymphoid follicles in SLOs (Schumacher & Thommen, 2022). In 
addition to lymphocytes, TLS s contain numerous mature dendritic cells and follicular 
dendritic cells (FDC) derived from mesenchyme (Kang et al., 2021). Both TLS s and

vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
022). In 
addition to lymphocytes, TLS s contain numerous mature dendritic cells and follicular 
dendritic cells (FDC) derived from mesenchyme (Kang et al., 2021). Both TLS s and 
SLOs also contain CD68+ macrophages dispersed in follicles, which are responsible 
for clearing apoptotic cells and regulating immune responses (Baratin et al., 2017). 
Moreover, the high endothelial venules (HEVs) found abundantly within TLS s not 
only provide nutritional support but also enhance the migration of B lymphocytes and 
T lymphocytes from the bloodstream into specific TLS s (Dieu‐Nosjean et al., 2016). 
The migration of lymphocytes into SLOs is caused by the lymphoid chemokines 
including CCL19, CCL21, and CXCL13 (Dieu‐Nosjean et al., 2016). These 
chemokines are also highly expressed in the germinal center B cell regions and T cell -
enriched region in TLS s, thereby attracting naive and central memory T cells, as well 
as naive B cells, to join TLS s to complete differentiation and maturation (de 
Chaisemartin et al., 2011). The presence of similar structures, cellular compositions,

vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
21 
 and chemokine expressions enables TLS s to effectively bridge the innate and adaptive 
immune systems within the TME. This facilitates the establishment of a 
comprehensive immune network within or around the tumor, paralleling the immune 
functions of SLOs and enhancing durable antitumor immune r esponses. 
1.3.2 Development and organization of TLS 
It was initially believed that the formation of TLS s and the construction of SLOs 
followed similar mechanisms, that is, involving the stimulation of lymphotoxin 
signaling and the local high -level expression of certain key adhesion molecules and 
lymphoid chemokines (Li et al., 2023). However, while lymphoid tissue inducer (LTi) 
cells and the lymphotoxin signaling pathway are essential in the generation of SLOs 
(Mebius, 2003), emerging research suggests that the formation of TLS s does not 
strictly require true LTi cells. Instead, various immune cells including M1 
macrophages, CD8+ T cells, Th17 helper cells, and B cells can fulfill the role of LTi 
cells under certain conditions to initiate the multi -step physiological process of the 
formation and maturation of TLS s (Peske et al., 2015; Furtado et al., 2014). Intricate 
interactions among immune cells, stromal cells, and various cytokines are then 
involved in developing mature TLS s with immune functions.

vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
2015; Furtado et al., 2014). Intricate 
interactions among immune cells, stromal cells, and various cytokines are then 
involved in developing mature TLS s with immune functions. 
The formation of TLS s comprises six stages: localization and stromal cell activation, 
LTi cells aggregation, mutual feedforward activation between LTi cells and lymphoid 
tissue organizer (LTo) cells, HEV expansion and lymphocyte recruitment, 
compartmentalization of T cells and B Cells, GC generation and B cell differentiation 
(Li et al., 2023). The initiation of TLS formation is triggered by a cascade of 
inflammatory signals, including cytokines such as TNF -α, IL-6, IL-7, IL-17 and 
chemokines such as CCL19, CCL21, CXCL12, CXCL13 (Kang et al., 2021). Initially, 
LTi cells or immune cell subsets assuming LTi cells’ roles interact with stromal cells 
under the influence of IL -7 and IL-17, establishing the initial TLS formation site 
(Cupedo et al., 2009; Wang et a l., 2021). This process relies on cytokines such as IL -
13, IL-17, and IL -22 secreted by innate lymphoid cells (ILCs), T cells, and stromal

vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
22 
 cells. These cytokines can activate fibroblasts to upregulate cell adhesion molecules 
and secrete extracellular matrix components, thus forming a supportive matrix 
network essential for TLS construction (Nayar et al., 2019). 
Subsequently, under the induction of IL -7, CXCL13, and VEGF -A, LTi cells 
congregate at the nascent TLS site (Cupedo et al., 2009; Van De Pavert et al., 2009; 
Delvecchio et al., 2021). LTi cells can produce lymphotoxin α-1β-2 (LTα1β2) and 
activate LTo cells via LTβR receptors (Meier et al., 2007). LTi cells, in turn, further 
receive stimulation from IL -7 secreted by LTo cells leading to increased production of 
LTα1β2. This interaction creates a continuously robust and self -reinforcing positive 
feedback loop (Meier et al., 2007). These signaling molecules, together with VEGF -
C, promote the expansion of the HEV network (Shikhagaie et al., 2017). Next, LTo 
cells will establish a gradient of several chemokines including CCL19, CCL21, 
CXCL12, and CXCL13. These che mokines direct the migration of lymphocytes such 
as CCR7+ naive T cells, central memory T cells, CXCR5+ B cells, and T follicular 
helper (Tfh) cells from HEVs into the developing TLS s (Hill, 2019). Such recruitment 
is crucial for the subsequent organizatio n and compartmentalization of immune cells

vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
cular 
helper (Tfh) cells from HEVs into the developing TLS s (Hill, 2019). Such recruitment 
is crucial for the subsequent organizatio n and compartmentalization of immune cells 
into micro -functional districts within the TLS s, with distinct areas for B cells and T 
cells (Li et al., 2023). Ultimately, influenced by gradients of type I interferons (IFN) 
and CXCL13, GCs are formed to help B cells begin the differentiation process 
(Sjöstrand et al., 2016). 
Given such multi -step development and maturation process of TLS s, it is necessary to 
define different maturity stages of TLS s for cancer research. Researchers have 
identified three distinct maturation stages of TLS s based on the structural similarity 
between TLS s and SLOs in various cancers such as non -small cell lung cancer 
(NSCLC), hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC), and colorectal cancer (CRC) (Siliņa et 
al., 2018; Calderaro et al., 2019; Posch et al., 2018). In stage I, only dense aggregates 
of lymphocytes are present, comprising maturing B cells, CD8+ T cells, and other T 
cell subsets like T follicular helper (Tfh) cells, but without clear segregation of T cells

vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
23 
 and B cells; stage II is referred to as primary follicle -like TLSs, which contains FDC 
but lacks mature GC; stage III is the fully mature secondary follicle -like TLSs, which 
has GC and clear T/B lymphocyte compartmentalization (Schumacher & Thommen, 
2022). Unfortunately, no study has yet clearly defined the different maturation stages 
of TLSs in ovarian cancer, highlighting a gap in the current ovarian cancer research 
landscape. 
HEV are specialized blood vessels typically found in lymph nodes in SLOs. Driven 
by the secretion of pro -angiogenic factors (VEGF -C, LTα1β2, IL-7) that promote 
angiogenesis and vascularization, they develop and expand to form a HEV network 
during the devel opment of TLS s (Li et al., 2023). This HEV network not only boosts 
the recruitment of immune cells to the de novo TLS s region but also ensures its 
continued development to facilitate long -term tissue settlement within the target 
tumor area with sustained i mmune surveillance (Dieu -Nosjean et al., 2014). 
Collectively, these specialized cell zones and structures such as lymphoid follicles and 
HEVs enable mature TLS s to fulfill immune functions within the TME. 
It is noteworthy that the complex interactions between TLS and the TME during TLS 
development continuously coordinate the recruitment, aggregation, and clustering of

vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
n the TME. 
It is noteworthy that the complex interactions between TLS and the TME during TLS 
development continuously coordinate the recruitment, aggregation, and clustering of 
immune cells necessary for TLS maturation, thereby shaping those TMEs with many 
TLSs to exhibit unique cytokine and chemokine gene expression profiles (Li et al., 
2023). This process occurs continuously and dynamically within the TME, 
underscoring the key role of dynamic TME in affecting the initiation and persistence 
of local immune responses. For example, in the TME containing multi-TLSs, 
cytokines such as IFN -γ, IL-21, and IL -17 are upregulated, fostering an environment 
conducive to the differentiation and proliferation of plasma cells and cytotoxic CD8+ 
T cells, which significantly impacts the local immune landscape within the TME (Li 
et al., 2023).

vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
24 
 1.3.3 Immune functions of TLS 
The mature TLS s mirror the compartmentalized structure and functional attributes of 
lymph nodes found in SLOs. In mature TLS s areas, there are B -cell zones featuring 
germinal centers, T -cell zones, and HEV that facilitate the efficient migration of 
lymphocytes from the bloodstream into the tissue (Kang et al., 2021). Additional 
immune cells such as FDC, macrophages, and NK cells are a lso present (Kang et al., 
2021). FDC plays crucial roles in antigen presentation, the promotion of GC 
formation, and the facilitation of B c ell differentiation (Kang et al., 2021). 
Intrafollicular CD68+ macrophages are also involved in antigen presentation and have 
the function of clearing apoptotic cells, which is similar to their role in SLO 
(Schumacher & Thommen, 2022). In addition to exert ing their innate immune 
functions, NK cells can also secrete IFN -γ and promote B cell activation (Li et al., 
2023). GC is the key hallmark of a mature TLS. They are instrumental in supporting 
B cells through their processes of proliferation, somatic hyperm utation, and 
differentiation to produce high -affinity antibodies and exhibit active humoral anti -
tumor responses (Siliņa et al., 2018). 
Previous studies have found that TLS s often appear in TME in response to 
inflammation caused by tumor stimulation and they can promote immune activation.

vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
Siliņa et al., 2018). 
Previous studies have found that TLS s often appear in TME in response to 
inflammation caused by tumor stimulation and they can promote immune activation. 
The variety of cell types TLS s contain, including immune cells, fibroblasts, and 
endothelial cells, can affect the characteristics and metabolism of the TME through 
cell-cell interactions and the secretion of different cytokines, ultimately influencing 
cancer progression and treatment response (Kang et al., 2021). For instance, TLS s 
have been shown to sign ificantly contribute to tumor adaptive immunity by enhancing 
T cell activation, assisting in antigen presentation, and responding to cytokines in the 
TME, which can generate an increased number of plasma cells and cytotoxic CD8+ T 
cells (Sautès -Fridman, et al., 2019). Therefore, TLS density is closely related to the 
density of CD8+ T cells and CD4+ T cells in lung tumors (Goc et al., 2014). Another 
research suggests that memory B lymphocytes located in TLS s of omental metastases

vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
25 
 of ovarian cancer have high clonality and can produce a variety of chemokines that 
attract DCs and NK cells (Montfort et al., 2017). This in turn reflects the recruitment 
of innate immune cells by memory B lymphocytes in TLS s, thereby promoting and 
accelerating the cycle of anti -tumor immune responses. 
In addition to T lymphocytes and B lymphocytes, the presence of TLS s is closely 
associated with increased infiltration of other immune cell subsets in the TME, such 
as dendritic cells (conventional DCs and plasmacytoid DCs), macrophages (M1 and 
M2), follicular T cells (follicular helper T cells and follicular regulatory T cells) 
(Zhang et al., 2024). The research revealed that tumor tissues harboring TLS s have 
higher levels of diverse immune infiltration compared to those tumor regions lacking 
these structu res, which is often linked to better prognosis (Lin et al., 2020). This is 
confirmed by mouse melanoma models from which they found TLS s can drive tumor -
specific adaptive immune responses alone in the absence of SLO, with significantly 
increased T lymphocyte infiltration and suppressed tumor progression (Schrama et al., 
2001). Importantly, TLS s provide space for the key synergy of multiple immune cells, 
including B cells presenting antigen peptides to CD8+ T cells through MHC I

vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
chrama et al., 
2001). Importantly, TLS s provide space for the key synergy of multiple immune cells, 
including B cells presenting antigen peptides to CD8+ T cells through MHC I 
molecules, Tfh cells activati ng GC B cells and promoting immunoglobulin antibody 
production, etc., thus promoting comprehensive anti -tumor immune function (Zhang 
et al., 2024). Indeed, the presence of mature TLS s is indicative of a more favorable 
and immunologically active TME, and the anti -tumor immune response can be 
enhanced to inhibit tumor growth (Li et al., 2023) . 
1.3.4 Clinical significance of TLS 
Numerous studies have deeply explored the prognostic value of TLS s in tumor 
treatment. TLS presence is associated with a favorable prognosis across various 
cancer types, irrespective of the patient’s tumor stage (Sautès -Fridman et al., 2019). 
Functioning as a critical site within TME, TLS s play a pivotal role in initiating and 
maintaining local and systemic adaptive immune responses against tumors (Dieu‐
Nosjean et al., 2016 ). Clinical research in several different tumor types has confirmed

vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
26 
 that the presence of TLS is associated with the effectiveness of ICI and better survival 
outcomes (Petitprez et al., 2020; Cabrita et al., 2020; Helmink et al., 2020;). In 
HGSOC, recent evidence points to a correlation between the presence of TLS and 
improved patient outcomes, including longer overall survival (OS) and progression -
free survival (PFS) (Zhang et al., 2023; Lu et al., 2023). 
The density, maturation status, and spatial location of TLS s are key factors 
influencing prognostic values in cancer treatment. Several authors confirmed that 
higher TLS s density in the TME is associated with better prognosis in most tumors 
(Sautès-Fridman et al., 2016). Different mature statuses of TLSs lead to distinct 
cellular compositions as well as spatial cell -cell interactions, which are highly 
correlated with their effects on antitumor immune response and prognosis (Zhang & 
Wu, 2023). Previous studi es have shown that HCC and CRC patients with more 
mature TLS s differentiation have a lower risk of recurrence compared with patients 
with only lymphoid aggregates (Posch et al., 2018; Calderaro et al., 2019). 
Differences in TLS s location can also affect the prognosis of cancer patients. 
Compared with HCC patients with intratumoral TLSs, those with only peritumoral 
TLSs have a significantly higher risk of recurrence (Calderaro et al., 2019).

vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
of cancer patients. 
Compared with HCC patients with intratumoral TLSs, those with only peritumoral 
TLSs have a significantly higher risk of recurrence (Calderaro et al., 2019). 
Differential gene expression, cellular interactions, and spatial localization in TLS s 
also hold distinct prognostic implications. It is indicated that the presence of CD8+ 
TILs in TLS s alone cannot predict the prognostic effect, but its co -presence with 
plasma cells, CD20+ TILs, and CD4+ TILs is highly associated with positive anti -
tumor immune effects, suggesting synergy among these lymphocyte subpopulations in 
TLSs (Kroeger et al., 2016). Additionally, unique gene expression signatures of TLS -
related plas ma cells in ovarian cancer also show positive prognostic value (Kroeger et 
al., 2016). Another research used a mouse model to demonstrate that tumor -specific B 
and Tfh cells can cooperate to promote anti -tumor CD8+ T cell immune response (Cui 
et al., 2021). Analysis of genes involved in TLS s in the database revealed the 
prognostic value of some gene signatures such as CCL19, CXCL10, and CXCL13 for

vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
27 
 ovarian cancer, primarily by increasing immune infiltration and restricting immune 
escape (Hou et al., 2023). 
The generation and maturation of TLS can be induced through various methods, 
offering the potential to bolster anti -tumor immune responses. In mouse models, 
tissue-specific expression of some cytokines, such as lymphotoxin, TNF α, and 
CXCL13 can trigger TLS development locally (Schumacher & Thommen, 2022). 
ICIs, vaccines, and neoadjuvant chemotherapy have also been reported to increase the 
number of TLS s in TME (van Dijk et al., 2020; Maldonado et al., 2014; Remark et al., 
2016). However, it's crucial to note that while enhancing TLS function may curb 
tumor progression, it could also heighten autoimmune responses and worsen 
autoimmune toxicity (Schumacher & Thommen, 2022). Further clinical trials are 
necessary to validate the safety of such approaches and thoroughly assess associated 
risks with TLS induction. 
Importantly, the immune cell composition of TLS s exhibits noticeable heterogeneity 
across different cancer types and among individual patients (Sautès -Fridman et al., 
2019). This variability highlights the significance of developing and implementing 
clinical precision medicine approaches tailored to the unique im mune landscape of 
each patient. 
1.4 Methods enabling the spatial exploration of the TME

oping and implementing 
clinical precision medicine approaches tailored to the unique im mune landscape of 
each patient. 
1.4 Methods enabling the spatial exploration of the TME 
Visualizing and characterizing the spatial interactions between immune cells and 
tumor cells in the cancer TME is crucial for discovering new prognostic markers and 
therapeutic targets. Traditional techniques utilizing patient -derived material s can 
improve the understanding of cancer biology . Formalin-fixed paraffin -embedded 
(FFPE) samples are the most accessible clinical samples due to their ability to 
preserve tissue structure and cell integrity over long periods. Hematoxylin and eosin 
(H&E) staining, rou tinely employed by pathologists for diagnosis and prognostic 
assessment, offers valuable insights into tissue morphology. Recently, deep learning 
models based on H&E whole -slide images (WSIs) have emerged to analyze TME

vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
28 
 features and predict treatment response, but it is still difficult to identify cell types 
with limited information (Yamashita et al., 2021). Besides, traditional methods such 
as immunohistochemistry (IHC) can only achieve low -plex imaging and lack the 
ability to simultaneously explore multiple targets in the TME (Tan et al., 2020). 
On the other hand, recent advancements in multi -omics technologies have facilitated 
large-scale genome, transcriptome, and proteomics analysis of the TME, offering 
novel insights. However, conventional sequencing approaches such as whole genome 
sequencing, bulk RNA sequencing, and single -cell RNA sequencing cannot retain 
spatial information, restricting the understanding of cell interactions and immune 
functions in the TME. To uncover the spatial molecular and functional landscape of 
the TME and resolve gene expression variances at the single -cell level, innovative 
methods and technologies need to be employed to enable spatial exploration across 
the entire ti ssue slide. 
1.4.1 Highly multiplexed imaging 
Highly multiplexed imaging uses antibodies or nucleic acid probes to simultaneously 
detect and visualize multiple molecular targets in sample s to study their spatial 
distribution and complex interactions at high resolution . Mass cytometry imaging

vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
ultaneously 
detect and visualize multiple molecular targets in sample s to study their spatial 
distribution and complex interactions at high resolution . Mass cytometry imaging 
(MCI) uses antibodies conjugated to heavy metal isotopes to detect specific molecular 
targets. While it offers exceptionally high subcellular resolution, its application is 
mainly confined by the size of the target area, limiting sele ction to a small region of 
interest (ROIs) in tissue sections (Giesen et al., 2014). Consequently, there's a 
pressing need for multiplexed methods capable of simultaneously labeling multiple 
molecules in a larger area to achieve whole-tissue exploration. 
Multiplex immunohistochemistry (mIHC) utilizes chromogenic or enzymatic 
reactions to visualize multiple protein markers in tissue samples , while m ultiplex 
immunofluorescence (mIF) enables the labeling of protein targets using specifically 
binding antibodies conjugated with fluorophores (Tan et al., 2020). However, limited 
by the spectral overlap of fluorophores and the wavelength range of visible li ght,

vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
29 
 these two methods can only visualize up to 5 different targets in a single cycle. 
Nevertheless, by deactivating, blocking, or stripping labeled antibodies, these two 
techniques can achieve consecutive rounds of iterations, ultimately labeling up to 30 
targets (Lewis et al., 2021). 
In recent years, novel methods have been developed aiming for gentler removal of 
antibody labels to replace the inactivation of the antibodies themselves in traditional 
methods, thereby minimizing tissue damage and expanding the number of available 
cycles. Tissue cyclic immunofluorescence (t -CycIF) employs fluorophore 
photobleaching to eliminate the previous fluorophore signal, which greatly reduces 
the damage to tissue integrity caused by cycles of fluorescent staining and imaging. 
Leveraging a broad array of commercial antibodies against diverse protein targets, t -
CycIF enables very reliable and high -resolution highly multiplexed imaging of FFPE 
slides, accommodating up to 60 different markers (Lin et al., 2018). Another 
approach, co -detection by indexing (CODEX), utilizes primary antibodies conjugated 
to DNA barcodes for staining, with subsequent DNA hybridization facilitating the 
visualization of fluorescent signals (Black et al., 2021). After imaging, reporter 
molecules can be chemically stripped, allowi ng imaging of up to 60 labels. While this

vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
ng the 
visualization of fluorescent signals (Black et al., 2021). After imaging, reporter 
molecules can be chemically stripped, allowi ng imaging of up to 60 labels. While this 
method allows the scanning of entire tissue sections, its drawbacks include high 
reagent costs and limitations in antibody availability (Lewis et al., 2021). 
1.4.2 Spatial gene expression profiling 
Highly multiplexed imaging enables visualization of the distribution and abundance 
of protein targets within tissue sections, while transcriptome analysis can reveal 
differential gene expression and diverse cellular functional states. As technology 
progresses, spatially resolved gene expression profiling has become attainable. Image -
based in situ multiplex mRNA detection began with the emergence of single -molecule 
fluorescence in situ hybridization (FISH) in 2008, which employs oligonucleotide 
probes conjug ated with fluorescent tags to identify specific sequences in fixed cells or 
tissues, thereby labeling mRNA molecules (Raj et al., 2008). In 2014, sequential FISH

vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
30 (seqFISH) enabled multiple rounds of conti nuous hybridization and imaging, thus 
increasing the number of detectable genes (Lubeck et al., 2014) . To reduce the errors 
accumula ted from repeated mRNA detection, multiplex error-robust FISH 
(MERFISH) was developed in 2015, which uses a combination of tags, including an 
RNA-binding target sequence and a fluorophore-tagged read sequence (Chen et al., 
2015). MERFISH successfully achieves multiplex analysis of the copy number and 
spatial location of up to 1000 RNAs in multiple single cells, but it exhibits limited 
capability in capturing low-abundance transcripts (Walsh & Quail, 2023). Moreover, 
these in situ transcriptomic technologies fall short in capturing and analyzing the 
entire transcriptome profile, posing challenges fo r achieving high-throughput analysis 
at the whole tissue section level. 
In 2019, two mature commercial platf orms emerged for transcriptomic analysis: 
Visium and Ge oMx. Visium Spatial Gene Expression (10x Genomics) is based on in 
situ array that can detect tissue areas of up to 6 mm × 6 mm, with each spot offering a 
resolution of approximately 100 μm (2-10 cells) (Ståhl et al., 2016). The V isium 
workflow begins with H&E staining and imaging of fre sh frozen sections . Followed 
by tissue permeabilizatio n, beads with spatially barcoded oligonuc leotides are used to

vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
sium 
workflow begins with H&E staining and imaging of fre sh frozen sections . Followed 
by tissue permeabilizatio n, beads with spatially barcoded oligonuc leotides are used to 
achieve efficient capture of mRNA and preserve spatial information. Collected mRNA 
undergoes reverse transcription and NGS to generate spatial transcriptomics data, 
providing a high-resolution view of the transcriptome (Ståhl et al., 2016) . Visium has 
good sensitivity and can capture a wide range of genes with low-level transcript 
expression, making it suitable for FFPE sections as well (Nerurkar et al., 2020). Since 
each measurement site contains multiple cells, whole transcriptome reads of Visium 
require deconvolut ion using algorithms to determine cell type (Seferbekova et al., 
2023). Widely utilized in spatial transcriptomic analyses of various cancers such as 
breast cancer, prosta te cancer, and malignant mela noma, Visium has unveiled 
unprecedented intra- a nd inter-tumor heterogeneit y, along with distinctive gene 
expression profile s (Nerurkar e t al., 2020). A recent study leverage d Visium spatial 
transcriptomics to successfully identify multiple tumor subclones with distinct copy

vector_chunks.text:
31 
 number variations in a single HGSC section, showcasing varied infiltration patterns 
and cellular interactions (Denisenko et al., 2024). 
On the other hand , GeoMx Digital Spatial Profiler (NanoString) utilizes probes with 
photocleavable barcodes to bind to corresponding mRNA in FFPE tissue . It then 
illuminates the selected ROIs with UV light to release all oligonucleotide probes in 
this region for quantitative NGC sequencing analysis (Merritt et al., 2020). Tumor and 
immune cell specific antibodies are used in this process to morphologically label 
tissues to assist in ROIs selection. NanoString provides probe sets for both the 1,833 -
gene canc er transcriptome atlas (CTA) genome and the 18,000 -gene human 
transcriptome, thus enabling large -scale spatial transcriptomic analysis (Moses & 
Pachter, 2022). GeoMx has been used to study TME heterogeneity, biomarkers of ICI 
treatment, and TLS mechanisms in melanoma (van Dam et al., 2022; Toki et al., 
2019). GeoMx is highly sensitive and customized individual areas of illumination 
(AOIs) in a single ROIs can be generated using morphological markers, which 
enables gene expression analysis for specific cell types or spatial locations. However, 
it's important to note the subjectivity inherent in ROIs and AOI selection. In addition, 
while targeted methods enhance detection sensitivity, they are limited by the number

r, 
it's important to note the subjectivity inherent in ROIs and AOI selection. In addition, 
while targeted methods enhance detection sensitivity, they are limited by the number 
of genes in the probes combination set and their high cost (Wang et al., 2021). 
Released in 2022, the spatial molecular imaging system CosMx (NanoString) aims to 
achieve high -resolution spatial analysis of RNA and proteins in tissues at the single -
cell or subcellular level. CosMx utilizes an innovative approach by combining an in 
situ hybridization (ISH) probe with a fluorescent readout probe, known as a reporter, 
to capture RNA within sample tissue. The distinct fluorescent sequence of the reporter 
enables precise spot detection at high resolution. This system is capable of performing 
multiple cycles of fluorescent molecular barcode hybridization with high sensitivity 
and low error rates at the single -cell level (He et al., 2022). CosMx has a large 
scanning area (16 –375 mm2) for tissue sections and eliminates the need for additional 
tissue permeabilization. Serving as an automated integrated platform, CosMx enables

vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
32 
 three-dimensional super -resolution localization of FFPE sections containing up to 2 
million cells, coupled with cell segmentation based on morphological antibodies (He 
et al., 2022). Preliminary studies have demonstrated CosMx’s efficacy in showing the 
co-localization of B cells and Tfh cells in GCs in lymph nodes, as well as IL18 
signaling between B cells and macrophages (Hether et al., 2022). These findings 
underscore CosMx's ability to unravel immune processes in small tissue structures . 
Pick-seq (Rarecyte) is a new method for in -depth spatial transcription analysis of 
tissue sections, particularly FFPE samples. It mainly involves micro -region extraction 
and subsequent gene expression profiling or sequencing. Using microneedles with a 
diameter of 25 or 40 µm, microtissue regions containing 5 -20 cells can be isolated 
from tissue sections and collected, thereby preserving spatial information for 
subsequent transcriptome or proteomics analysis (Podyminogin et al., 2019 ). 
Microtissue extractio n relies on low -plex immunofluorescence staining with 
commercial antibodies on adjacent tissue sections, followed by whole -slide imaging 
by CyteFinder®. Through the usage of this instrument, it is possible to capture cells 
from any area of a whole tissue section for whole transcriptome isolation, 
amplification, and sequencing.

vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
®. Through the usage of this instrument, it is possible to capture cells 
from any area of a whole tissue section for whole transcriptome isolation, 
amplification, and sequencing. Previous studies have applied pick -seq for spatial 
transcriptomic analysis of melanoma and breast cancer (Maliga et al., 2021; Ericson et 
al., 2020), showing its efficacy in exploring spatial gene expression. However, to date, 
there have been no studies that conduct pick-seq and perform rapid gene expression 
analysis through non -sequencing methods. This presents an area ripe for exploration 
to further assist with spatial gene expression profiling.

 2 Introduction 
The clinical treatment of ovarian cancer faces significant challenges due to its high 
mortality rate and limited treatment options. Most patients are diagnosed with ovarian 
cancer at advanced stages, resulting in poor overall survival. Current standard 
treatments including p rimary debulking surgery and NACT fall short in efficacy , 
lacking consistent positive outcomes across all cases. In this context, new treatments 
using targeted therapies and ICIs have shown promising clinical results but have 
limited improvement in overall survival. Considering the suppressive immune 
network and functionally restricted TILs in the TME of ovarian cancer, it is necessary 
to understand more the role of adaptive immunity in anti -tumor immune response s 
with spatial characteristics . This will reveal their impact on clinical therapeutic effects 
and help improve current ICI treatments . 
TLSs are widely present in the TME of ovarian cancer but exhibit notable inter -
individual heterogeneity. While prior research has explored the predictive power of 
TLSs in tumor progression and patient prognosis, many anti -tumor immune 
mechanisms underlying these processes remain unclear. This knowledge gap indicates 
that TLS is a promising research field that should be further explored in mechanisms 
of its formation, maintenance, and interaction with TME.

vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
nclear. This knowledge gap indicates 
that TLS is a promising research field that should be further explored in mechanisms 
of its formation, maintenance, and interaction with TME. Moreover, it is still under 
debate whether the presence and abundance of TLSs favor patients’ positive immune 
responses and prognoses (Kang et al., 2021). Given such a double -edged sword role 
of TLS in anti -tumor immunit y, analyzing spatially resolved gene expression in 
mature TLS areas of clinical samples holds significant potential and value. 
New highly multiplexed imaging methods, such as t-CycIF, have significantly 
advanced the spatial analysis of the TME in ovarian cancer across entire tissue slides. 
Combining t -CycIF and high -resolution imaging, spatial image analysis at the single -
cell level has been achieved. This innovative approach has led to the identification of 
positive prognostic markers for the combination therapy of PARP inhibitors and PD -1 
inhibitors in platinum -resistant patients (Färkkilä et al., 2020), as well as the

vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
34 
 discovery of selective spatial attraction and interactions among immune cell 
subpopulations in TLS s in the TME of ovarian cancer (Sarkkinen et al., 2023). These 
findings underscore the crucial role of integrating the spatial dimension in cancer 
immunology research. 
While t-CycIF offers valuable insights into cell phenotype and neighborhood 
interactions with the help of validated antibodies, it lacks the ability to provide deeper 
molecular -level gene expression analysis. Spatial transcriptomics techniques such as 
GeoMx and Visium address this limitation by capturing mRNA and enabling 
sequencing analysis, allowing for the integration of cellular and molecular features 
with spatial resolution. Furthermore, pick -seq offers the possibility for microtissue 
collection of distinct cell populations and consequent high-throughput spatial analysis, 
which is particularly crucial for studying microstructures like TLS s in the TME . 
However, despite its tremendous research potential , there are still many issues 
regarding picking efficiency , the need for high tissue quality, and high costs for 
sequencing analysis . Therefore, it is necessary to develop a novel method using pick -
seq to achieve spatially resolved gene profiling with high resolution and low costs . 
This method will comprehensively enhance the investigation of anti-tumor immunity

vector_chunks.text:
ng pick -
seq to achieve spatially resolved gene profiling with high resolution and low costs . 
This method will comprehensively enhance the investigation of anti-tumor immunity 
mechanisms in the TME of ovarian cancer. 
2.1 Significance and challenges 
The optimized picking in this study can achieve precise and efficient extraction of 40 
μm diameter microtissues targeting different cell types with accurate spatial locations. 
When it is applied to the TLS s of ovarian cancer on FFPE slides and combined with 
the Nanostring nCounter Panel for gene expression profiling, this novel method has 
great potential to reveal the mechanism s of anti-tumor immune responses and 
potential key regulation genes cost -effectively . Given that FFPE slides can be stored 
for long periods and are easily available, it is possible now to implement spatially 
resolved large -scale transcriptome or genome analysis with large clinical sample 
cohorts across time.

vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
35 
 Currently, there are several key challenges for this new method. While techniques like 
pick-seq and Visium have been successfully utilized for fresh frozen samples, 
adapting a new technique for application on FFPE samples remains unexplored. 
Given the typically inferior RNA quality in FFPE slides, a range of obstacles must be 
addressed to guarantee both the yield and integrity of extracted mRNA. Due to the 
extremely low amount of mRNA yield obtained through pick-seq, the current 
approach relies solely on co stly and time -consuming sequencing methods after pre-
amplification . There is no method to use gene panels for fast targeted immune 
analysis. Besides, a lthough the nCounter PanCancer gene panel can detect gene 
expression levels for up to 770 genes, the lack of spatial information obscures key 
differentially expressed genes. Therefore, n ew protocols need to be designed to ensure 
that spatial information is preserved during microtissue extraction for subsequent 
transcriptomic data analysis. 
2.2 Research aims 
The primary research aim of this master's thesis is to establish a novel method called 
Nano-Pick that enables efficient and consistent operation of optimized pick-seq and 
verifies its suitability for Nanostring nCounter gene panel analysis. The following 
goals will be pursued and achieved:

cient and consistent operation of optimized pick-seq and 
verifies its suitability for Nanostring nCounter gene panel analysis. The following 
goals will be pursued and achieved: 
1: Improve ROIs selection in pick -seq experiments through TLSs annotation from t -
CycIF images 
1.1: Utilize t -CycIF stainings to identify and annotate TLSs regions in the TME of 
ovarian cancer 
1.2: Optimize antibody staining protocols to achieve successful cell type 
recognition for pick-seq 
2: Implement strategies to optimize microtissue extraction and access its compatibility 
for nCounter gene panel analysis 
2.1: Adjust parameters to achieve efficient extraction for different cell groups

 2.2: Transfer TLSs annotations from t -CycIF to guide precise picking on adjacent 
slides 
2.3: Establish a standardized protocol for mRNA extraction and purification after 
microtissue extraction 
2.4: Verify the feasibility and pre -amplification requirements for Nanostring 
nCounter gene panel analysis using qPCR

3.1.2 Patients and specimens 
All FFPE tissue samples are obtained from the ONCOSYS -Ova prospective study or 
the Helsinki University Hospital (HUH) tissue block collection. These blocks are 
taken from interval debulking surgery after NACT treatment, or from primary 
debulking surgery of HGSC patients. All samples utilized in this study, along with 
their corresponding clinical data, have received approval for research purposes from 
the ethics committees of the Women's Clinic, Helsinki University Hospital (HUH), 
and Helsinki Biobank. Patie nt IDs and other personal information are handled with 
utmost security and confidentiality on the HUH server, utilizing the HUH Datalake 
infrastructure provided by the Helsinki Biobank. Access to and extraction of clinical

led with 
utmost security and confidentiality on the HUH server, utilizing the HUH Datalake 
infrastructure provided by the Helsinki Biobank. Access to and extraction of clinical 
data are strictly limited to auth orized personnel only. In the publicly published results, 
each patient is assigned a randomized publication ID to minimize the possibility of 
patient identification. 
In this study, 17 samples with multiple TLSs are selected from 17 patients. Details of 
these samples are shown in Table 3.

3.2 Methods 
3.2.1 Sample sectioning 
Tissues are processed and embedded into paraffin blocks at the Tissue Preparation and 
Histochemistry Unit (TPHU) within the Anatomy Department at Biomedicum. Tissue 
sections with 5 μm thickness are cut from FFPE blocks at the histopathology services 
of the Institute for Molecular Medicine Finland (FIMM). For each sample, 7 adjacent 
tissue sections are obtained and then the best three adjacent slides are chosen for t -
CyCIF and microtissue picking ( Figure 1). 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Figure 1 : The schematic diagram of sample sectioning and selection .

 3.2.2 t-CycIF protocol 
3.2.2.1 Deparaffinization, rehydration, antigen retrieval and pre -staining 
First, paraffin around the tissue needs to be completely removed from all sectioned 
samples. The drying oven is preheated to 56℃. All the selected slides are placed 
vertically on the plastic slide holder at a certain distance and then baked in the drying 
oven for 30 minutes. After that, all FFPE slides were submerged in xylene s olution 
three times for 5 minutes each to remove paraffin. 
Next, all slides were put in a series of ethanol solutions with gradually decreasing 
concentrations (100% ethanol, 90% ethanol, 70% ethanol, 50% ethanol, 30% 
ethanol), once for each concentration lasting 3 minutes. This process can gradually 
rehydrate samp les. Finally, wash twice in 1xPBS for one minute each to remove any 
residual ethanol. 
In order to fully retrieve the cross -linked antigen molecules after being treated with 
FFPE, all slides need to be placed in a glass container filled with 250 ml of freshly 
made 10M citric acid solution (pH=6) and heated in the microwave to 100℃. After 
the heating process, the slides are allowed to cool down slowly at room temperature 
for 20 minutes. Then, Odyssey blocking buffer was added to the tissue. All slides 
were incubated at room temperature for 30 minutes in a moist incubation chamber to

vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
temperature 
for 20 minutes. Then, Odyssey blocking buffer was added to the tissue. All slides 
were incubated at room temperature for 30 minutes in a moist incubation chamber to 
prevent ev aporation and tissue dehydration. 
Next, use 1:5000 Hoechst and 1:1000 goat -anti-rabbit secondary antibodies for pre -
staining for 1 hour at room temperature. When finished, wash three times with 1xPBS 
for three minutes each time to remove residual antibodies. To remove background 
signals, the antibodies bound to the tissue during pre -stain were bleached to inactivate 
their fluorophores (see 3.2.2.3) for 1.5 hours. Finally, the slides are washed four times 
in 1xPBS for 3 minutes each to completely remove residual solution from the 
bleaching p rocess.

vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
