41 
 3.2.2.2 Antibody staining 
To identify the TLS s regions and analyze the different types of immune cells in the 
TME, an antibody panel was designed and applied in 5 cycles of t -CycIF experiments 
(Table 4). In the first cycle, an unconjugated BCL6 antibody (rabbit) was used with 
overnight incubation at 4°C. Then on the following day, samples are incubated by 
goat-anti-rabbit secondary antibodies conjugated with AlexaFluor -488 (1:2000) and 
Hoechst (1:10000) in Odyssey buffer for 1 hour at room temperature. After staining, 
the slides were washed three times with 1xPBS, three minutes each. Then 70µl of 
70% glycerol was added and each slide was covered with a coverslip, ready for 
scanning and imaging using Rare cyte CyteFinder® II with a magnification of 20X . 
The second to fifth cycles all used primary antibodies conjugated with either Alexa 
Fluor or eFluor. In each cycle, the slides were incubated overnight at 4°C with the 
corresponding antibodies, and then scan ned in the next day after washing and 
mounting. 
3.2.2.3 Fluorophore inactivation (bleaching) 
Fluorophore inactivation is required between each staining cycle to ensure the 
removal of all fluorescent markers from the previous cycle. The solution used for 
bleaching needs to be prepared freshly containing 40ml 1x PBS, 7.5ml 30% H2O2 
and 2.5ml 480 mM NaOH.

vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
val of all fluorescent markers from the previous cycle. The solution used for 
bleaching needs to be prepared freshly containing 40ml 1x PBS, 7.5ml 30% H2O2 
and 2.5ml 480 mM NaOH. This 50ml bleach solution can be used for up to 7 slides. 
The slides are placed into a plastic container with the bleach solution. Two large -area 
LED light panels are then used to photobleach the tissues double -sided at room 
temperature. The total bl eaching time of each cycle is 90 minutes, of which the 
bleaching solution needs to be replaced every 30 minutes following pre -stain, and 45 
minutes for other cycles.

3.2.3 Images acquisition, processing , and visualization 
3.2.3.1 Images acquisition 
Images were acquired using a Rarecyte CyteFinder ® II high-throughput scanner. This 
is an automated microscope platform for digital imaging of slides, inside containing 
the slide holder and multiple microscope lenses with different magnifications (4X, 
10X, 20X, 40X). This imaging system also consists of a series of software connected 
to the scanner, including RareCyte Instrument Controller (operates the microscope), 
CyteHub (control center for scanning and image analysis), CyteFinder (set up and 
monitor the scan progress), and Imager (for real real -time imaging and picking 
control). Up to four antibodies can be scanned simultaneously in one cycle and they 
should be located in different channels (488nm, 555nm, 647nm, 750nm). Hoechst 
33342 is consistently used in each cycle for nuclear staining. CyteFinder® II can scan 
two slides together within 30 minutes with a magnification of 20X, offering excellent 
scanning efficiency and well -focused high -quality images.

 The imaging process begins by placing the prepared slide upside down on the slide 
holder because all microscope lenses are inverted under the slide holder. Before 
scanning, an essay suitable for this experiment needs to be set up, including the 
antibodies used in each channel, the appropriate exposure time, and a series of other 
scanning-related parameters. When scanning a slide for the first time, a pre -scan 
needs to be conducted to identify the tissue area, allowing for manually selecting the 
appropriate scanning range and adding focusing points at different locations in 
CyteHub. The selected scan range and focusing points are stored in the CyteHub 
system. Starting from the second cycle, the pre -defined same area saved for each slide 
can be automatically s elected and scanned, which ensures the overlay of several 
images from different cycles. 
After successfully focusing on the tissue slide, scanning is performed automatically 
according to a pre -designed whole -area grid containing hundreds of micro -regions, 
called tiles. During scanning, all channels of a tile are imaged in sequence before 
moving to the next tile. These tiles have partially overlapped areas with each other, 
which facilitates the stitching process later. Upon completion of each scanning cycle, 
the raw data containing all tiles is stored in the corresponding rcpnl file (Figure 2).

vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
, 
which facilitates the stitching process later. Upon completion of each scanning cycle, 
the raw data containing all tiles is stored in the corresponding rcpnl file (Figure 2). 
3.2.3.2 Images processing and visualization 
To assemble all tiles into a high-dimensional image, an open-source software package 
ASHLAR (Alignment by Simultaneous Harmonization of Layer / Adjacency 
Registration) is used for the stitching and alignment process of all images from 
different cycles (Figure 2). ASHLAR 's workflow mainly consists of three steps: 
stitching together adjacent tiles in each imaging cycle; checking the relative positions 
of the same tiles across different cycles compared to the first cycle , and then 
registration ; output a final ome.tiff image containing all channels and all cycles . This 
process of stitching and registration is based only on the nuclear staining of the 
Hoechst channel because the nuclei have clear position s and boundaries to be 
identified easily. Similar to a pyramid structure, t he ome.tiff image uses an image

vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
44 stacking method that contains multiple layers of images with different resolutions. 
This optimizes the speed of visualization and image processing of such high-
dimensional images, as only a partial image within the field of view of a single 
resolution is loaded at each moment. 
Figure 2 : The schematic diagram of image processing and visualization . 
Napari, a fast and interactive viewer for multi -dimensional images in Python, is used 
to visualize the stitched images ( Figure 2). It can help view ome.tiff images and allow 
for the overlay of specific local area annotations or cell segmentations. The antibody 
information of all channels is pre -stored in a txt file and loaded into Napari alongside 
the image to display the antibody name corresponding to each channel. Adjusted 
contrast limits for each channel can be saved and recalled when the image is reloaded. 
3.2.4 TLS s identification and ROIs selection 
All TLSs are identified based on the high expression of CD20, CD4, and PD1 in t -
CycIF images. The contrast limits for different markers were adjusted and saved in 
Napari for optimized visualization. Round or oval aggregation centers with very 
strong CD20 e xpression were identified as GCs. Mature TLSs also have abundantly 
surrounded CD4+ T cells with high expression of PD1 on partial cells. Aggregated

vector_chunks.text:
45 areas of CD20+ B cells that lack CD4 and PD1 expression and have no obvious T/B 
cell compartmental structure are immature TLSs. 
Rectangle shapes are used in Napari to mark regions of all mature TLSs including 
adjacent tumor cells. All added location areas are stored separately for each sample. A 
custom Python script is applied to automatically crop and export all TLSs images for 
further TLSs annotation and ROIs selection. 
Next, annotations of three different zones are marked in each TLS region using 
QuPath. Since the picking targets are quite small, these annotations do not have to be 
fixed shapes and the only goal is to optimally label different cell types and 
interactions . First, check all TLSs images and only choose those mature TLSs with 
clear GC. Then the red line was drawn to mark the outer ring area of GCs without the 
center of only CD20+B cells. A large number of PD1+CD4+T cells and B cells are 
located in these T –B cell zones, serving as the primary site where lymphocytes 
coordinate immune functions within TLSs. Myeloid – T cell zones around GCs were 
then annotated with the green line, which contains abundant CD4+T cells, CD8+T 
cells, macrophages, DCs, and B cells. Th ese zones are identified as key areas where 
immune responses such as antigen presentation occur between myeloid cells and T 
cells with rich cell -to-cell interactions.

vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
cells. Th ese zones are identified as key areas where 
immune responses such as antigen presentation occur between myeloid cells and T 
cells with rich cell -to-cell interactions. Surrounded tumor zones were finally 
annotated with yellow lines, which also contain small numbers of cytotoxic T cells, 
macrophages, and DCs. 
Based on all annotations of TLSs, five ROIs were selected in each different zone in a 
single TLS area. The aim is to select ROIs with cell -cell interactions so that further 
gene expression analysis can be done to make discoveries. The diameter of all ROIs is 
40µm, which is consistent with the diameter of the microneedle used in the actual 
operation. The minimum distance between adjacent ROIs is 40µm to reserve enough 
space for the wiggling process during the picking. All selected ROIs and their 
coordinates were stored in the Rarecyte CyteHub® system, waiting to be transferred 
to the adjacent picking samples.

46 
 3.2.5 Microtissue extraction 
3.2.5.1 Antibody staining without antigen retrieval 
FFPE slides adjacent to t -CycIF slides were selected for microtissue extraction. These 
slides also need to undergo a similar slide preparation process as the t -CycIF 
experiment, including deparaffinization in xylene and rehydration in ethanol series 
with gradually decreasing concentrations. To bett er protect the mRNA in the FFPE 
sample, all ethanol series are prepared with DEPC-treated water on ice with 0.0025% 
RNasin Plus. Antigen retrieval will not be performed next. In the process of antigen 
retrieval in FFPE samples, conventional methods need to use heat-induced antigen 
retrieval methods (HIER) to remove protein cross -links and expose antigens, which 
can significantly improve the efficiency of antibody binding and staining results 
(Krenacs et al., 2010). However, extracting mRNA from FFPE samples requires 
avoiding high -temperature processing to preserve the integrity of the mRNA as much 
as possible. Therefore, the antigen retrieval step was skipped, while the staining steps 
were optimized with reliable antibodies to compensate for the loss of stai ning results 
as much as possible. 
The blocking was reduced to 10 minutes at room temperature with Odyssey buffer 
containing 0.1% RNasin Plus. All samples were then directly stained by Hoechst and

h as possible. 
The blocking was reduced to 10 minutes at room temperature with Odyssey buffer 
containing 0.1% RNasin Plus. All samples were then directly stained by Hoechst and 
pre-conjugated antibodies including Iba1, PanCK, and CD45 in the real picking 
experiment . These antibodies are validated from a test experiment with a designed 
antibody panel (Table 5). 0.1% RNasin Plus was always added to the antibody mix. 
The slides are incubated with antibodies at room temperature for 10 minutes and then 
moved to the 4°C fridge overnight.

47 3.2.5.2 Scan images and ROI s transfer 
On the next day, the slides were washed in 1xPBS with 0.0025% RNasin Plus for five 
minutes. All PBS used in this experiment is made with sterile 10xPBS stock and 
DEPC-treated water. Then 50µl of 50% glycerol was added and each slide was 
covered with a coverslip . Scan the slides using Rarecyte CyteFinder® II with a 
magnification of 10X . All images automatically undergo illumination correction, 
tissue distortion correction , and stitching in CyteHub system to generate stitched 
ome.tiff images. These ome.tiff images can be viewed in the Tissue Results in 
CyteHub. 
All pre-selected ROIs were then transferred from the adjacent t -CycIF image to this 
picking slide . There were only very minor tissue differences between adjacent 
sections. All ROIs were reviewed and corrected manually to ensure their correct 
positions on target cells . After that, ROIs were stored in the CyteHub and they can be 
further transferred automatically from this up -side-down scanning image to the real 
picking position on the upward tissue. 
After scanning, the slides were merged in a box of sterile 1x PBS with 0.0025% 
RNasin to remove the coverslip and then washed in sterile 1x PBS with 0.0025% 
RNasin for 3 min to remove residual glycerol.

There are 6 small nuclease-free PCR tubes on the needle holder.

Next, load all six needles into the needle holder. Set the needle holder on the right of

rinsing solution before moving it to the calibration slide. The calibration is very 
important to ensure precise control of the needle. Both automatic calibration and 
manual calibration can be chosen. A successful calibration can align the actual 
position (x, y) of the needle tip with the displayed position (x, y) shown on the Imager 
interface. Sample positions can also be calibrated to ensure the needle tip precisely 
touches the slide (z position) as indicated in the needle controller. A successful

49 
 3.2.5.4 Picking & collection 
Check all picking parameters and select Hoechst as the focusing channel. There are 
several steps in a single picking process. The needle needs to be flushed in the rinsing 
solution three times and then filled with retrieval buffer. Next, move the needle to the 
target ROIS and complete picking. The extracted microtissue should be deposited 
immediately after picking . Repeat this process up to five times to complete the 
picking for one zone of TLS annotations. Before continuing to pick for another zone, 
the slide should be put back to 1xPBS with 0.1% RNasin Plus for three minutes to 
avoid tissue dehydration. 
The aim is to collect 10 picks of the same zone into one deposit tube. Therefore, a 
total of 30 picks should be done for one slide. If the interval exceeds half an hour or 
there is a significant deviation in the picking position, the needle needs to be re -
calibrated. After ten picks of each zone are completed, the tube is immediately stored 
in a -80°C refrigerator for subsequent tissue lysis and mRNA extraction. 
3.2.6 mRNA extraction 
3.2.6.1 Tissue digestion 
PCR tubes containing extracted microtissues were removed from the freezer and 
thawed at room temperature for five minutes. All tubes were then incubated at 56°C in 
a PCR thermocycler for one hour to lyse the tissue and release mRNA. Upon

vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf

the freezer and 
thawed at room temperature for five minutes. All tubes were then incubated at 56°C in 
a PCR thermocycler for one hour to lyse the tissue and release mRNA. Upon 
completion , the tubes were briefly vortexed, spun down, and placed on ice. 
3.2.6.2 mRNA extraction and purification 
The first step is beads preparation. The original NorthernMax buffer (10×) was taken 
out from the fridge to be well-mixed. New 2× NorthernMax buffer and 1× 
NorthernMax buffer were made in a 10 ml falcon tube. The Dynabeads Oligo (dT) 25 
beads were resuspended in the vial . 120 μl (for 6 samples) of beads were transferred 
to a 1.5ml Eppendorf tube and washed three times with 1 ml ice -cold 1×hybridization 
buffer (1×NorthernMax buffer including 0.05% Tween 20 and 0.0025% RNasin Plus ).

vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf

50 Wash for 1 minute, put the tube on a magnet for 1 minute, and remove all buffers each 
time. After washing, resuspend the beads in the original bead volume with 120 μl ice-
cold 2× hybridization buffer (2× NorthernMax buffer including 0. 1% Tween 20 and 
0.01% RNasin Plus). 
To capture mRNA, 20 μl of washed beads were added to each lysed sample . They 
were incubated at 56°C for 1 minute and at room temperature for 10 minutes. Samples 
were placed on a magnet and a ll supernatants were removed . Now all mRNA should 
be combined with the beads. 
The beads were washed twice with 150 μl ice-cold 1× hybridization buffer. Wash for 
1 minute, put on magnet for 1 minute , and remove all supernatants. Then wash the 
beads once with 150 μl ice-cold 1× PBS with 0.0025% RNasin Plus. Wash for 1 
minute, put on magnet for 1 minute, and remove all supernatants. To elute mRNA, all 
the pellets were resuspended in 10.5 μl nuclease-free water in 0.2ml PCR tubes. The 
tubes were incubated at 80°C for 2 minutes and immediately placed on a magnet. All 
supernatants were transferred to new RNase -Free PCR tubes on ice, waiting for 
reverse transcription. 
3.2.6.3 Reverse transcription 
SuperScript IV VILO Master Mix was used for fast reverse transcription. 8 μl mRNA 
template and 2 μl 5X master mix were added to an empty RNase -free 0.2ml PCR tube.

anscription 
SuperScript IV VILO Master Mix was used for fast reverse transcription. 8 μl mRNA 
template and 2 μl 5X master mix were added to an empty RNase -free 0.2ml PCR tube. 
All tubes were placed in a PCR thermocycler with this program: 10 minutes to anneal 
primers at 25℃, 10 minutes to reverse transcribe mRNA at 50℃, and 5 minutes to 
inactivate enzyme at 85℃. Remove all tubes from the PCR thermal cycler and place 
them on ice. cDNA products can be stored at -20℃ for up to a week and -80℃ for 
long-term storage.

52 4 Results 
4.1 Traditional t -CyCIF staining s successfully guide TLS identification 
According to the standard experimental protocols of t -CyCIF, all samples were 
stained for 4 cycles using the same antibody set and concentration ( Table 4). Two 
mature tumor -surrounded TLS regions from the same sample were selected to show 
the staining results ( Figure 3). Whole-section tissue images of all samples were 
accurately stitched together using ASHLAR, with no overlapping or missing areas. 
No obvious tissue shift, tissue deformation, or detachment were found in images from 
different cycles. 
As shown in Figure 3, BCL6 showed satisfactory staining results with minor 
background signals in cycle 1. Iba1, PanCK, CD8a, CD4, CD11c, CD20, and PD1 all 
exhibit clear positive signals with excellent signal -to-noise ratio , correlating with their 
expected staining pattern . These staining results clearly define GC boundaries , tumor 
areas, and other zones with distinct immune interactions.

 Based on this, three different types of annotations were manually added to the images, 
including tumor zones, myeloid – T cell zones, and T –B cell zones ( Figure 4B ). Based

4B, red dashed line). 
4.2 Iba1, PanCK, and CD45 stainings without antigen retrieval enable the 
recognition of distinct cell populations 
The slides adjacent to the t -CycIF slides are used for actual microtissue extraction. 
The traditional pretreatment process utilizes high temperatures for antigen retrieval , 
which can better expose all antigens and enhance the staining effect. In order to avoid 
damage to mRNA quality caused by high temperature , staining protocols were 
optimized to achieve close staining effects without antigen retrieval (Shown in 
methods). 
As shown in Figure 5, the staining quality of Iba1 and CD45 did not exhibit 
significant degradation in the absence of antigen retrieval. However, there were 
instances of antibody accumulation and incomplete cell staining in edge areas with 
suboptimal resolution. PanCK effectively labeled most cancer cells, yet it displayed 
noticeable variations in signal intensity across different regions with uneven 
fluorescence signals.

mal resolution. PanCK effectively labeled most cancer cells, yet it displayed 
noticeable variations in signal intensity across different regions with uneven 
fluorescence signals. Additionally, CK7 was also used to label cancer cells, but 
compared w ith PanCK, the staining results were notably inferior, with very low and 
severely uneven fluorescence intensity. Overall, despite the decreased signal intensity 
observed, Iba1, PanCK, and CD45 still showed satisfactory staining results, 
adequately facilitating the identification of diverse cell types and tissue structures in 
TLSs. The suboptimal staining quality in certain areas minimally impacts the 
execution of picking procedures.

57 
 4.3 Optimized parameter setting schemes achieve consistent microtissue 
extraction for different cell phenotypes 
Due to the pioneering nature of pick -seq technology, there has been a lack of 
published methods providing specific parameter combinations for efficient 
microtissue extraction from FFPE slides. Rarecyte's user manual offers only a broad 
range of available p arameter settings for each parameter. Based on this, the optimized 
parameter combination for microtissue extraction in FFPE slides was determined and 
shown here for the first time after numerous experiments ( Table 10). A series of 
optimized distance parameters on the left ensure precise microneedle positioning for 
effective extraction and deposition of the target microtissue. On the right, the 
optimized volume parameters are crucial for achieving a high extraction success rate 
and optimal collection efficiency. In addition, different strategies for wiggling are 
needed for different tissue types to promote the detachment of the target microtissue 
from the surroun ding tissue. 
Table 10: Optimized Picking Parameters 
Pick Standby 150 µm Fill Needle (FN) 210 nL 
Wet Standby 0 µm Wet Volume (WV) 160 nL 
Overdrive 10 µm Fill Buffer (FB) 0 nL 
Backoff 0 µm Draw Volume (DV) 100 nL 
Deposit Distance 500 µm Dispense (DP) 150 nL 
 Dispense Rate 100% 
Wiggle Radius 30 µm Stage Wiggles 2 or 3

vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
ive 10 µm Fill Buffer (FB) 0 nL 
Backoff 0 µm Draw Volume (DV) 100 nL 
Deposit Distance 500 µm Dispense (DP) 150 nL 
 Dispense Rate 100% 
Wiggle Radius 30 µm Stage Wiggles 2 or 3 
Specifically, 150 µm Pick Standby guarantees a safe distance for FFPE slices before 
picking. The 0 µm Wet Standby prevents wet droplets from displacing cells and 
compromising tissue integrity. With a 10 µm Overdrive, the needle tip maintains close 
contact with the FFPE section without damaging the needle, promoting effective 
tissue separation during the wiggle process. Employing a 0 µm Backoff ensures 
selective aspiration of only the target micro -tissues, avoiding extraction of tissues

vector_chunks.text:
58 
 outside the ROIs. Deposit Distance of 500 µm enables efficient collection of the 
extracted microtissues into the deposit tube. 
Throughout the entire picking process, the liquid volume within the microneedle tube 
undergoes multiple changes. Therefore, different volume parameters need to be well 
combined to ensure optimal wetting, strong suction, and a 100% dispense rate to 
collect all extracted cells. Following practical exploration, the calculation formula of 
the relationship between volume parameters is summarized: 
FN = WV + FB + DP - DV 
Considering the slight variation in the total volume of microneedle tubes across 
different needles, a universal volume of FN=210 nL serves as a more appropriate 
choice, nearing the upper limit to maximize tube utilization. With a 40 µm diameter 
microneedle , the Draw Volume was first determined to be 100 nL. This is because 
when the microtissue is suitable for extraction, a higher DV increases the probability 
of aspirating surrounding tissue, while a lower DV reduces the success rate of target 
tissue extract ion in each ROIs. Subsequently, it was found in control experiments that 
the Wet Volume of 160 nL best matched the DV of 100 nL, which can maximize the 
extraction success rate and accuracy. Finally, the Dispense was set at 150 nL, ensuring 
100% discharge of the extracted t issue into the dispense tube.

nL, which can maximize the 
extraction success rate and accuracy. Finally, the Dispense was set at 150 nL, ensuring 
100% discharge of the extracted t issue into the dispense tube. No additional Fill 
Buffer is required. 
Figure 6 illustrates the schematic diagram of the picking procedure using the 
optimized parameter combination. For cancer cells with stronger cell adhesion, Stage 
Wiggles can be set to 3 to improve the extraction success rate. Every time a 
microtissue is picked, i t goes through the process including microneedle contact, 
wiggle, tissue separation, and extraction. Actual images and path diagrams depicting 
the different stages of this process are presented in Figure 7.

 Results showed that most samples with primer RPS13 and GAPDH exhibited normal 
amplification curves, with an average Ct value of 33.8 ( Figure 10 A i,ii). However, all 
samples using B2M as primer failed to amplify (Figure 10A iii), which is possibly due 
to issues with the primer design. Notably, all non -template control (NTC) displayed 
signals only near the baseline, confirming positive amplification of the samples 
(Figure 10A iv). The melt curve showed a clear single peak with Tm around 81°C for 
the RPS13 sample group, indicating specific and unique amplification (Figure 10 B i). 
However, the GADPH sample group had non -single peak signals, suggesting potential 
primer dimers and non -specific amplification (Figure 10B ii). 
In general, high but consistent Ct values from qPCR indicate the presence of 
extremely low levels of mRNA in the sample. Given that 10 cycles of pre -
amplification is typically recommended for FFPE samples with a minimum of 1 ng 
RNA input, the qPCR results suggest that all samples obtained by this method require 
testing for at least 14 -16 cycles to determine the appropriate pre -amplification cycles 
to conduct nCounter gene panel analysis .

This thesis developed Nano -pick, a novel method that can achieve precise microtissue 
extraction from FFPE samples and reliable mRNA collection for nCounter gene

 5 Discussion 
The rise of spatial transcriptomics allows researchers to access gene expression 
signatures at near -single-cell resolution. In cancer research, this new method offers 
deeper insights into the spatial structure and complex intercellular interactions of the 
TME, revealing its great potential in exploring anti -tumor immune response 
mechanisms and potential key regulatory genes. The latest mainstream technologies, 
such as Visium and Geomx, mainly rely on next -generation sequencing based on 
spatial barcoding to preserve spatial information (Moses & Pachter, 2022 ). However, 
despite high sequencing analysis costs, these methods cannot accurately capture the 
entire transcriptome of a specific cell subpopulation. Therefore, a simple, reliable, and 
sensitive spatially resolved transcriptional profiling method is needed with great 
research value. 
This master's thesis develops and validates a new method, Nano -Pick, which applies 
optimized microtissue extraction technology to FFPE samples and isolates mRNA 
from microtissues for nCounter gene expression analysis. Using optimized parameters 
and a semi -automated, high -precision microneedle operating platform, multiple preset 
ROIs can be consistently collected with a success rate close to 100%. By innovatively 
modifying conventional immunostaining approaches, effective labeling of tumor cells,

vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
reset 
ROIs can be consistently collected with a success rate close to 100%. By innovatively 
modifying conventional immunostaining approaches, effective labeling of tumor cells, 
lymphocytes , and macrophages was achieved without antigen retrieval. This not only 
maximizes the mRNA integrity preservation but also upgrades the precision of the 
picking process. The ROIs recognition during picking is based on both tissue 
morphology and antibody fluorescence signals of specific cell types. This 
significantly improves the positioning accuracy for pre -selected ROIs and provides 
the feasibility of applying more complex R OIs in the future. 
Nano-Pick has optimized methods for low RNA amount extraction and has been 
proven to be completely viable for nCounter analysis after pre -amplification. The 
nCounter system has been widely used for transcriptome analysis due to its simple 
automated workflow and rapid analysis process (Kulkarni, 2011). The PanCancer

vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
65 
 gene panel provided by the nCounter system can detect the gene expression features 
of 770 genes (Cesano, 2015) , but the lack of sample spatial information limits its 
potential in cancer research. Therefore, Nano -Pick can provide multiple samples with 
unique spatial locations and cell subpopulations, enabling rapid spatially resolved 
transcriptome profiling by samp les or groups. On the one hand, cell type 
deconvolution is not necessary and the reliability of gene expression analysis for 
different cell subpop ulations is improved. On the other hand, the potential high -
resolution single -cell level deconvolution algorithm for cell subtype inference can 
further realize spatial transcription analysis at single -cell resolution, giving Nano -pick 
incomparable developm ent potential. 
TLSs are the main local center containing immune cells in the TME, which is 
involved in activating and regulating anti -tumor immune responses. In this study, 
different cellular regions in TLSs were annotated separately, and multiple ROIs were 
selected amon g them. The successful picking demonstrates the ability of Nano -Pick to 
explore critical microstructures such as TLSs in FFPE tissues . This can further 
facilitate the exploration of the spatial attraction and interactions of different immune

vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
ck to 
explore critical microstructures such as TLSs in FFPE tissues . This can further 
facilitate the exploration of the spatial attraction and interactions of different immune 
cell subtypes in TLSs, as well as the screening of highly expressed cytokines in its 
unique gene expression profile . Given that FFPE tissues are easily available for 
clinical cancer research and can be stored for years, this novel method holds promise 
for accessing histopathology archives and contributing to translational research . 
Therefore, it is possible to conduct large -scale spatially resolved transcriptome 
research with a large cohort of archived FFPE samples without the time constraints of 
sample collection. 
It must be acknowledged that this study has some shortcomings. Although Nano -Pick 
has been fully validated and is viable for nCounter gene panel analysis, only qPCR 
experiments were performed on multiple collected mRNA samples to initially verify 
the feasibility of the entire workflow. The quality control of mRNA and the number of 
pre-amplification cycles in nCounter analysis need to be further determined. Finally,

vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
66 
 many antibodies could be useful if validated to achieve satisfactory staining results 
without antigen retrieval. I aim to conduct m ore validation experiments to establish a 
standardized antibody library that can label different cell types during the picking 
process. It will further expand the application of Nano -Pick in other research areas. 
Nonetheless, this study innovatively establishes all experiment protocols of Nano-
Pick based on existing experimental methods . It not only provides a new approach for 
spatial transcription al analysis of FFPE samples, but also provides the possibility of 
other high -throughput analyses. Since microtissue extraction relies on simple and 
robust microneedles, it allows for consistent and accurate mechanical isolation and 
collection of microtissue from target areas. These microtissues contain complete 
whole transcriptomes, genomes , and proteom es, enabling highly sensitive multi -omics 
analysis including gene expression profiling. Given that the development of spatial 
genomics is still limited by the number of single -cell DNA copies, the development of 
new artificial intelligence -based computational methods will better utilize Nano -Pick 
to promote quantitative research in spatial genomics. 
The huge potential of Nano -Pick should not be ignored, as the development of

vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
ional methods will better utilize Nano -Pick 
to promote quantitative research in spatial genomics. 
The huge potential of Nano -Pick should not be ignored, as the development of 
corresponding automated procedures will extend its current capabilities significantly. 
After completing the calibration and ROIs selection, the picking process is actually a 
very tedious and repetitive process. Therefore, it is entirely possible that this process 
can be automated, which will greatly improve the efficiency of microtissue extraction. 
In addition, the success rate of picking is influenced by variations in tissue secti on 
thickness across different areas, which is also the main reason for extraction failure. 
One possible solution is to use advanced instruments to pre -measure the thickness 
distribution of entire tissue sections and integrate this data with all other saved 
information of each ROI. This will effectively facilitate the individual correction of 
each ROI during the picking process to ensure successful extraction. 
Furthermore, there is ongoing advancement in computational image analysis of t -
CycIF images that enables semi-automated cell type identification (Schapiro et al.,

vector_chunks.source_file: 6. Archive/MSc_theses/Nanopick Master_s thesis - Zhihan/Nano-Pick A Novel Method Enables Spatially Resolved Gene Expression Profiling of Tertiary Lymphoid Structures in Ovarian Cancer.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
67 
 2022; Kang et al., 2024 ). These computational pipelines will improve the annotation 
of target tissue regions and the selection of ROIs for the Nano -Pick method in the 
future. Such semi-automated cell type identification can effectively eliminate errors in 
human annotation and greatly improve the accuracy of the picking for different cell 
populations. Another prospect is to use Nano -Pick to extract microtissues from 
multiple consecutive F FPE sections of specific regions such as GC, and create a 3D 
gene expression map of the target tissue region. This will break through the two -
dimensional limitations of current spatial transcriptomic research and provide new 
possibilities for cancer research. 
In summary, this study developed Nano-Pick, a novel method for spatially resolved 
gene expression analysis based on precise microtissue extraction and the nCounter 
system. Nano-Pick can promote higher-resolution spatial analysis and interactions of 
immune cell populations in the TME . It also holds promise for translational research 
across diverse diseases, thereby advancing the prospects of precision medicine .
